passages.title: The Vault Beneath the House
passages.attribution: from The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe (1839)
passages.text:
At the request of Usher, I personally aided him in the arrangements for the temporary entombment. The body having been encoffined, we two alone bore it to its rest. The vault in which we placed it (and which had been so long unopened that our torches, half smothered in its oppressive atmosphere, gave us little opportunity for investigation) was small, damp, and entirely without means of admission for light; lying, at great depth, immediately beneath that portion of the building in which was my own sleeping apartment. It had been used, apparently, in remote feudal times, for the worst purposes of a donjon-keep, and, in later days, as a place of deposit for powder, or some other highly combustible substance, as a portion of its floor, and the whole interior of a long archway through which we reached it, were carefully sheathed with copper. The door, of massive iron, had been, also, similarly protected. Its immense weight caused an unusually sharp grating sound, as it moved upon its hinges.

Having deposited our mournful burden upon tressels within this region of horror, we partially turned aside the yet unscrewed lid of the coffin, and looked upon the face of the tenant. A striking similitude between the brother and sister now first arrested my attention; and Usher, divining, perhaps, my thoughts, murmured out some few words from which I learned that the deceased and himself had been twins, and that sympathies of a scarcely intelligible nature had always existed between them. Our glances, however, rested not long upon the dead—for we could not regard her unawed. The disease which had thus entombed the lady in the maturity of youth, had left, as usual in all maladies of a strictly cataleptical character, the mockery of a faint blush upon the bosom and the face, and that suspiciously lingering smile upon the lip which is so terrible in death. We replaced and screwed down the lid, and, having secured the door of iron, made our way, with toil, into the scarcely less gloomy apartments of the upper portion of the house.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A) A grieving brother's request draws two boyhood friends into a renewed closeness.
B) The narrator helps Usher lay his sister's body to rest in a vault beneath the house, an errand whose details leave both men shaken.
C) The long history of an ancient vault explains the decayed condition of the House of Usher.
D) The narrator discovers proof that the lady has been buried alive.
E) A narrator describes the elaborate funeral customs of an ancient family.
questions.explanation: The passage narrates the entombment and dwells on disquieting details—the "region of horror," the strange blush, the smile "so terrible in death." D is too extreme: the passage only hints uneasily that the lady seems lifelike; the narrator finds no proof of anything.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when Usher "murmured out some few words," the narrator learned that
choices: A) Usher blames the physicians for failing to cure his sister
B) Usher intends the entombment to be permanent
C) Usher and the deceased were twins between whom strange sympathies had always existed
D) Usher wishes the lady to rest far away from the house
E) Usher fears the vault's oppressive atmosphere will harm them both
questions.explanation: The murmured words are the source "from which I learned that the deceased and himself had been twins," sharing "sympathies of a scarcely intelligible nature." B contradicts the stated "temporary entombment," and D reverses the facts—the vault lies directly beneath the house.

questions.prompt: The description of "the mockery of a faint blush" and "that suspiciously lingering smile" most strongly suggests that
choices: A) Usher has arranged the body so as to conceal the true cause of death
B) the narrator's torch cast misleading shadows across the corpse's face
C) the lady died contented and at peace
D) the lady's body looks unsettlingly lifelike, as though death has not wholly claimed her
E) the disease had entirely erased the lady's resemblance to her brother
questions.explanation: "Mockery" (a false imitation) and "suspiciously lingering" frame the blush and smile as eerily lifelike—which is why the men "could not regard her unawed." C misreads the smile as peaceful, ignoring that the narrator calls it "so terrible in death."

questions.prompt: In the phrase "A striking similitude between the brother and sister," the word "similitude" most nearly means
choices: A) pretense
B) resemblance
C) tenderness
D) rivalry
E) mystery
questions.explanation: The "striking similitude" is what prompts Usher to explain that he and the deceased were twins—so it must mean a close likeness. "Pretense" is a trap built on the root shared with "simulate"; nothing in the sentence suggests fakery or disguise.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude throughout the entombment can best be described as
choices: A) clinical detachment
B) wry amusement
C) frantic panic
D) tender nostalgia
E) solemn and increasingly uneasy
questions.explanation: He is grave ("our mournful burden") yet ever more disturbed—"region of horror," "we could not regard her unawed." A is the tempting half-truth: he does catalog the vault's features precisely, but his admitted awe and horror rule out indifference; C overstates his controlled dread.

questions.prompt: The passage attributes all of the following to the vault EXCEPT
choices: A) it lies at great depth beneath the narrator's own sleeping apartment
B) it admits no light whatsoever
C) it apparently once served the purposes of a dungeon-keep
D) it holds the coffins of earlier generations of the Usher family
E) its massive iron door makes a sharp grating sound on its hinges
questions.explanation: The vault is described as deep, lightless, a former "donjon-keep" and powder store, with a grating iron door—but so "long unopened" and empty of any mention of other coffins. D tempts because a family vault implies prior burials, yet the passage never says so.

passages.context: The narrator is staying at the gloomy, decaying mansion of his boyhood friend Roderick Usher, whose health and nerves are failing. Usher's twin sister Madeline has just died, and the two men carry her coffin down into the house's depths.

passages.title: The Curiosity Dealer's Door
passages.attribution: from The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens (1841)
passages.text:
It was an old man with long grey hair, whose face and figure as he held the light above his head and looked before him as he approached, I could plainly see. Though much altered by age, I fancied I could recognize in his spare and slender form something of that delicate mould which I had noticed in a child. Their bright blue eyes were certainly alike, but his face was so deeply furrowed and so very full of care, that here all resemblance ceased.

The place through which he made his way at leisure was one of those receptacles for old and curious things which seem to crouch in odd corners of this town and to hide their musty treasures from the public eye in jealousy and distrust. There were suits of mail standing like ghosts in armour here and there, fantastic carvings brought from monkish cloisters, rusty weapons of various kinds, distorted figures in china and wood and iron and ivory: tapestry and strange furniture that might have been designed in dreams. The haggard aspect of the little old man was wonderfully suited to the place; he might have groped among old churches and tombs and deserted houses and gathered all the spoils with his own hands. There was nothing in the whole collection but was in keeping with himself; nothing that looked older or more worn than he.

As he turned the key in the lock, he surveyed me with some astonishment which was not diminished when he looked from me to my companion. The door being opened, the child addressed him as grandfather, and told him the little story of our companionship.

'Why, bless thee, child,' said the old man, patting her on the head, 'how couldst thou miss thy way? What if I had lost thee, Nell!'

'I would have found my way back to YOU, grandfather,' said the child boldly; 'never fear.'

'You must be tired, sir,' said he as he placed a chair near the fire, 'how can I thank you?'

'By taking more care of your grandchild another time, my good friend,' I replied.

'More care!' said the old man in a shrill voice, 'more care of Nelly! Why, who ever loved a child as I love Nell?'

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main concern of the passage?
choices: A) A catalogue of the rare and valuable goods for sale in a London shop
B) A narrator's first impressions of a strange old man and his shop as a lost child is returned to her home
C) An argument that the old man is unfit to raise his granddaughter
D) A child's terror at wandering the city streets after dark
E) The permanent rupture of a friendship between the narrator and the shopkeeper
questions.explanation: The passage traces the narrator's arrival, his observations of the shop and its owner, and their first exchange over the child. (A) is half-right: the goods are described, but only to characterize the old man and the setting. (C) overstates a single mild rebuke into an argument the passage never makes.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the resemblance between the old man and the child ceases because
choices: A) his hair has grown long and grey
B) his eyes are a different colour from the child's
C) his face is deeply lined and full of care
D) his figure is no longer spare and slender
E) he treats the child coldly and without affection
questions.explanation: The narrator says their bright blue eyes 'were certainly alike, but his face was so deeply furrowed and so very full of care, that here all resemblance ceased.' (A) is true — he does have long grey hair — but the passage never gives his hair as the reason the resemblance ends.

questions.prompt: In the sentence 'The haggard aspect of the little old man was wonderfully suited to the place,' the word 'haggard' most nearly means
choices: A) ancient
B) ghostly
C) ill-tempered
D) gaunt and careworn
E) untidy
questions.explanation: 'Haggard' names a worn, wasted look, echoing his 'deeply furrowed' face 'so very full of care' and 'nothing that looked older or more worn than he.' (A) tempts because age pervades the scene, but haggard describes his exhausted, wasted appearance rather than his years — even a young face can be haggard.

questions.prompt: From the old man's question 'how couldst thou miss thy way?' and the narrator's reply 'By taking more care of your grandchild another time,' it can be inferred that
choices: A) the child had lost her way while out alone, and the narrator holds the old man partly responsible
B) the narrator suspects the child invented the story of being lost
C) the old man deliberately abandoned the child in the streets
D) the child frequently runs away from home to spite her grandfather
E) the narrator found the child wandering but never learned where she lived
questions.explanation: The child 'told him the little story of our companionship,' he asks how she missed her way, and the narrator pointedly urges 'more care... another time' — she was lost, and her guardian is being blamed. (C) is too extreme: carelessness is implied, but nothing suggests deliberate abandonment.

questions.prompt: The old man's reply — ''More care!'... 'Why, who ever loved a child as I love Nell?'' — is best described as expressing
choices: A) amused tolerance
B) guilty evasion
C) wounded indignation
D) icy contempt
E) calm reassurance
questions.explanation: The 'shrill voice,' the echoed 'More care!' and the protesting question show a man stung by the suggestion that he neglects Nell — indignation born of hurt. (B) fails because he does not dodge the charge; he meets it head-on by insisting on his love. His heat also rules out 'icy' contempt.

questions.prompt: The author most likely includes the description of the shop's contents ('suits of mail standing like ghosts in armour... furniture that might have been designed in dreams') in order to
choices: A) show that the old man is a wealthy and successful merchant
B) explain why the child was afraid to return home
C) hint that the goods were stolen from churches and tombs
D) demonstrate the narrator's expert knowledge of antiques
E) evoke an eerie, timeworn setting that mirrors the old man himself
questions.explanation: Immediately after the description, the narrator calls the old man 'wonderfully suited to the place' and says nothing there 'looked older or more worn than he' — the setting is a portrait of its owner. (C) takes 'he might have groped among old churches and tombs' too literally; it is a fanciful impression, not an accusation of theft.

passages.context: Walking London late at night, the narrator has helped a lost little girl find her way home. Here her grandfather opens the door of the shop of old rarities where the two of them live.

passages.title: The Stranger in the Old Pew
passages.attribution: from The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë (1848)
passages.text:
The next day was Saturday; and, on Sunday, everybody wondered whether or not the fair unknown would profit by the vicar’s remonstrance, and come to church. I confess I looked with some interest myself towards the old family pew, appertaining to Wildfell Hall, where the faded crimson cushions and lining had been unpressed and unrenewed so many years, and the grim escutcheons, with their lugubrious borders of rusty black cloth, frowned so sternly from the wall above.

And there I beheld a tall, lady-like figure, clad in black. Her face was towards me, and there was something in it which, once seen, invited me to look again. Her hair was raven black, and disposed in long glossy ringlets, a style of coiffure rather unusual in those days, but always graceful and becoming; her complexion was clear and pale; her eyes I could not see, for, being bent upon her prayer-book, they were concealed by their drooping lids and long black lashes, but the brows above were expressive and well defined; the forehead was lofty and intellectual, the nose, a perfect aquiline and the features, in general, unexceptionable—only there was a slight hollowness about the cheeks and eyes, and the lips, though finely formed, were a little too thin, a little too firmly compressed, and had something about them that betokened, I thought, no very soft or amiable temper; and I said in my heart—‘I would rather admire you from this distance, fair lady, than be the partner of your home.’

Just then she happened to raise her eyes, and they met mine; I did not choose to withdraw my gaze, and she turned again to her book, but with a momentary, indefinable expression of quiet scorn, that was inexpressibly provoking to me.

‘She thinks me an impudent puppy,’ thought I. ‘Humph!—she shall change her mind before long, if I think it worth while.’

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes what happens in the passage?
choices: A) A young woman disrupts a church service by refusing to sit in her family's assigned pew.
B) A curious narrator studies a mysterious newcomer at church, judges her character from her face, and is nettled when she meets his stare with scorn.
C) A vicar persuades a reluctant widow to rejoin the congregation after years of absence.
D) A narrator falls hopelessly in love with a beautiful stranger the moment he sees her.
E) A congregation joins together to welcome a lonely stranger into the neighbourhood.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from curiosity to close inspection to the sting of her 'quiet scorn' and his vow that 'she shall change her mind.' (D) is too extreme and contradicted: he decides he would 'rather admire you from this distance' than share her home, hardly love at first sight.

questions.prompt: As used in the phrase 'the grim escutcheons, with their lugubrious borders of rusty black cloth,' the word 'lugubrious' most nearly means
choices: A) frayed and worn
B) brightly coloured
C) costly and elaborate
D) gloomy and mournful
E) unusually narrow
questions.explanation: 'Lugubrious' means exaggeratedly sad or dismal, fitting the 'grim' escutcheons that 'frowned so sternly.' (A) is tempting because the cloth is 'rusty' and the cushions 'faded'—true of the fabric's condition, but not what 'lugubrious' itself means; the word describes mood, not wear.

questions.prompt: From the detail that the pew's 'faded crimson cushions and lining had been unpressed and unrenewed so many years,' it can most reasonably be inferred that
choices: A) the church had recently replaced most of its furnishings
B) the narrator's family were the pew's rightful owners
C) Wildfell Hall had stood without a tenant for many years
D) the lady was too poor to rent a better seat
E) the congregation had grown smaller over the years
questions.explanation: The pew 'appertaining to Wildfell Hall' has gone unused so long its cushions are faded—so the Hall must have lacked occupants, which is why the lady is a 'fair unknown.' (E) is out of scope: one empty pew says nothing about the whole congregation's size.

questions.prompt: The narrator reads the lady's lips, 'a little too thin, a little too firmly compressed,' as a sign that she
choices: A) is overwhelmed by grief for a lost husband
B) has recently recovered from a serious illness
C) disapproves of the vicar's sermon
D) is not likely to have a soft or yielding disposition
E) is secretly amused by the congregation's attention
questions.explanation: He says the lips 'betokened, I thought, no very soft or amiable temper.' (B) is the trap: the 'slight hollowness about the cheeks' might hint at illness, but the question asks what the lips signify, and the narrator ties them explicitly to her temper, not her health.

questions.prompt: The narrator's closing remark—'she shall change her mind before long, if I think it worth while'—chiefly conveys
choices: A) sincere remorse for having stared at a stranger
B) genuine indifference to what the lady thinks of him
C) dread that she will publicly rebuke him
D) humble admiration for her composure
E) wounded pride and jaunty self-assurance
questions.explanation: Her scorn was 'inexpressibly provoking,' so he is stung, and he boasts he can change her opinion—injured vanity dressed up as confidence. (B) is the trap: 'if I think it worth while' is a pose of indifference, but a truly indifferent man would not have been provoked at all.

questions.prompt: The narrator observes all of the following about the lady EXCEPT
choices: A) the colour of her eyes
B) the style of her hair
C) the paleness of her complexion
D) the shape of her nose
E) the set of her lips
questions.explanation: He states plainly, 'her eyes I could not see,' since they were bent on her prayer-book and hidden by 'drooping lids and long black lashes.' Every other choice is described: raven ringlets, clear pale complexion, 'a perfect aquiline' nose, and thin, compressed lips. (A) is the only unobserved feature.

passages.context: In a quiet English village, a mysterious young widow has just moved into long-empty Wildfell Hall. The narrator, a young farmer, gets his first close look at her at Sunday church.

passages.title: A Prophetic Feeling
passages.attribution: from North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell (1855)
passages.text: 'I suffered too much myself; not that I was not extremely happy with the poor dear General, but still disparity of age is a drawback; one that I was resolved Edith should not have to encounter. Of course, without any maternal partiality, I foresaw that the dear child was likely to marry early; indeed, I had often said that I was sure she would be married before she was nineteen. I had quite a prophetic feeling when Captain Lennox'—and here the voice dropped into a whisper, but Margaret could easily supply the blank. The course of true love in Edith's case had run remarkably smooth. Mrs. Shaw had given way to the presentiment, as she expressed it; and had rather urged on the marriage, although it was below the expectations which many of Edith's acquaintances had formed for her, a young and pretty heiress. But Mrs. Shaw said that her only child should marry for love,—and sighed emphatically, as if love had not been her motive for marrying the General. Mrs. Shaw enjoyed the romance of the present engagement rather more than her daughter. Not but that Edith was very thoroughly and properly in love; still she would certainly have preferred a good house in Belgravia, to all the picturesqueness of the life which Captain Lennox described at Corfu. The very parts which made Margaret glow as she listened, Edith pretended to shiver and shudder at; partly for the pleasure she had in being coaxed out of her dislike by her fond lover, and partly because anything of a gipsy or make-shift life was really distasteful to her. Yet had any one come with a fine house, and a fine estate, and a fine title to boot, Edith would still have clung to Captain Lennox while the temptation lasted; when it was over, it is possible she might have had little qualms of ill-concealed regret that Captain Lennox could not have united in his person everything that was desirable. In this she was but her mother's child; who, after deliberately marrying General Shaw with no warmer feeling than respect for his character and establishment, was constantly, though quietly, bemoaning her hard lot in being united to one whom she could not love.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) describing the elaborate preparations for a fashionable wedding
B) revealing the gap between what the characters profess to feel about love and marriage and what they actually feel
C) condemning mothers who force their daughters into loveless marriages
D) contrasting Margaret's enthusiasm for travel with Edith's timidity
E) explaining why Edith's acquaintances disapproved of Captain Lennox
questions.explanation: The narrator repeatedly undercuts professed feelings: Mrs. Shaw sighs 'as if love had not been her motive,' and Edith 'pretended to shiver and shudder.' D is a real detail but only one supporting contrast, not the passage's central concern; C is too extreme, since Mrs. Shaw actually urged on a love match.

questions.prompt: As used in the statement that 'Mrs. Shaw had given way to the presentiment, as she expressed it,' the word 'presentiment' most nearly means
choices: A) a sentimental memory of her own wedding
B) a formal announcement of an engagement
C) a carefully reasoned judgment
D) a reluctant granting of permission
E) a feeling that something was about to happen
questions.explanation: The word refers back to Mrs. Shaw's 'quite a prophetic feeling' that Edith 'was likely to marry early'—a premonition. A is a word-part trap on 'sentiment,' and C is nearly opposite: a presentiment is an intuition, not the product of careful reasoning.

questions.prompt: When Mrs. Shaw's voice 'dropped into a whisper,' the narrator notes that 'Margaret could easily supply the blank.' This suggests that
choices: A) the story of how Edith's engagement came about was already so familiar to Margaret that she knew how the sentence would end
B) Margaret had been deliberately spying on her aunt's guests
C) Margaret shared her aunt's prophetic feelings about Captain Lennox
D) Mrs. Shaw had grown too weary to finish speaking
E) Mrs. Shaw secretly opposed Edith's marriage to Captain Lennox
questions.explanation: If Margaret can complete a sentence she cannot hear, Mrs. Shaw's tale of her 'prophetic feeling' must be a well-worn one. C is tempting because it echoes 'prophetic feeling,' but the passage credits Margaret with familiarity, not foresight; the whisper signals discretion, not fatigue (D).

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Edith 'pretended to shiver and shudder' at Captain Lennox's descriptions of life at Corfu partly because
choices: A) she dreaded the long sea voyage to the island
B) she hoped her mother would call off the engagement
C) she wanted to keep Margaret from glowing over the stories
D) she enjoyed being coaxed out of her dislike by her devoted lover
E) she feared a soldier's life would place the Captain in danger
questions.explanation: The passage states she shuddered 'partly for the pleasure she had in being coaxed out of her dislike by her fond lover,' and partly because makeshift living was distasteful to her. E invents a fear of danger the passage never mentions—her objection is to 'gipsy or make-shift life,' not to risk.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Mrs. Shaw can best be described as
choices: A) harshly condemning
B) warmly admiring
C) slyly ironic
D) detached and indifferent
E) anxiously sympathetic
questions.explanation: Touches like quoting her claim to speak 'without any maternal partiality' and noting she 'sighed emphatically, as if love had not been her motive' mock her gently, exposing self-deception without denunciation. A is too extreme—the mockery stays amused and understated, never harsh.

questions.prompt: The passage states or implies all of the following about Edith EXCEPT
choices: A) Her engagement fell short of what some acquaintances expected for a young heiress.
B) She begged to postpone the wedding until Captain Lennox left the army.
C) She was genuinely in love with Captain Lennox.
D) She would have preferred a fine London house to a picturesque life abroad.
E) She resembled her mother in wishing one person could unite everything desirable.
questions.explanation: No postponement is ever mentioned. A restates 'below the expectations which many of Edith's acquaintances had formed'; C, 'very thoroughly and properly in love'; D, her preference for 'a good house in Belgravia'; E, 'In this she was but her mother's child.' C may tempt readers who read her shudders as insincerity, but the narrator affirms her love.

passages.context: Margaret Hale sits listening while her aunt, Mrs. Shaw, holds forth to visitors about the engagement of her daughter Edith — Margaret's cousin — to a Captain Lennox of modest means.

passages.title: A Christmas Without Presents
passages.attribution: from Little Women by Louisa May Alcott (1868)
passages.text:
"Christmas won't be Christmas without any presents," grumbled Jo, lying on the rug.

"It's so dreadful to be poor!" sighed Meg, looking down at her old dress.

"I don't think it's fair for some girls to have plenty of pretty things, and other girls nothing at all," added little Amy, with an injured sniff.

"We've got Father and Mother, and each other," said Beth contentedly from her corner.

The four young faces on which the firelight shone brightened at the cheerful words, but darkened again as Jo said sadly, "We haven't got Father, and shall not have him for a long time." She didn't say "perhaps never," but each silently added it, thinking of Father far away, where the fighting was.

Nobody spoke for a minute; then Meg said in an altered tone, "You know the reason Mother proposed not having any presents this Christmas was because it is going to be a hard winter for everyone; and she thinks we ought not to spend money for pleasure, when our men are suffering so in the army. We can't do much, but we can make our little sacrifices, and ought to do it gladly. But I am afraid I don't," and Meg shook her head, as she thought regretfully of all the pretty things she wanted.

"But I don't think the little we should spend would do any good. We've each got a dollar, and the army wouldn't be much helped by our giving that. I agree not to expect anything from Mother or you, but I do want to buy Undine and Sintran for myself. I've wanted it so long," said Jo, who was a bookworm.

"I planned to spend mine in new music," said Beth, with a little sigh, which no one heard but the hearth brush and kettle-holder.

"I shall get a nice box of Faber's drawing pencils; I really need them," said Amy decidedly.

"Mother didn't say anything about our money, and she won't wish us to give up everything. Let's each buy what we want, and have a little fun; I'm sure we work hard enough to earn it," cried Jo, examining the heels of her shoes in a gentlemanly manner.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) explaining why a father has gone away to war
B) portraying four sisters as they struggle between making a holiday sacrifice and satisfying their own wishes
C) condemning the selfishness of children who refuse to aid the army
D) describing the hardships endured by soldiers during a hard winter
E) proving that poverty always destroys a family's happiness
questions.explanation: The scene moves from complaints about a present-less Christmas to each girl's private wish and Jo's proposal to spend after all, revealing each sister's character. The father's absence is one line of background, not the focus, and "condemning" or "proving" choices are far too harsh for the affectionate narration.

questions.prompt: According to Meg, "the reason Mother proposed not having any presents this Christmas" is that
choices: A) the family cannot afford even a single dollar for gifts
B) the girls' father has asked the family to give up celebrations
C) their mother hopes to surprise the girls with better gifts later
D) money should not go to pleasure while soldiers are suffering
E) the girls have behaved too badly to deserve presents
questions.explanation: Meg says it "is going to be a hard winter for everyone" and they "ought not to spend money for pleasure, when our men are suffering so in the army." The poverty choice tempts because the family is poor, but each girl actually has a dollar; affordability is not the reason Meg gives.

questions.prompt: The remark that Jo "didn't say 'perhaps never,' but each silently added it" suggests that the sisters
choices: A) privately fear that their father may die in the war
B) are angry at their father for abandoning them
C) expect the fighting to end before Christmas
D) refuse to discuss any of their troubles with one another
E) doubt that their father still remembers them
questions.explanation: "Perhaps never" completes Jo's thought about how long Father will be gone; each sister supplies it while "thinking of Father far away, where the fighting was" — a fear he may not return. The "refuse to discuss any troubles" choice is too sweeping: the girls complain freely; only this one dread goes unspoken.

questions.prompt: In the phrase "added little Amy, with an injured sniff," the word "injured" most nearly means
choices: A) physically wounded
B) permanently damaged
C) resentful
D) apologetic
E) exhausted
questions.explanation: Amy has just called it unfair that other girls "have plenty of pretty things" while she has none; her sniff expresses a sense of being wronged, so "injured" means resentful. "Physically wounded" is the trap of the word's literal meaning, which nothing in the scene supports.

questions.prompt: The tone of the passage as a whole is best described as
choices: A) grimly pessimistic
B) mournful and sentimental throughout
C) coldly objective
D) sarcastic and contemptuous
E) affectionate, with touches of humor and sadness
questions.explanation: Playful touches like Jo "examining the heels of her shoes in a gentlemanly manner" mix with the somber thought of Father "where the fighting was." "Mournful and sentimental throughout" is half-right: sadness surfaces in one moment, but "throughout" ignores the gentle humor coloring most of the scene.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, each of the following is true EXCEPT:
choices: A) Each of the sisters has a dollar of her own.
B) The girls' mother has forbidden them to spend their money on themselves.
C) Beth plans to spend her money on new music.
D) Meg thinks regretfully of pretty things she wants.
E) The girls' father is away where the fighting is.
questions.explanation: Jo says "Mother didn't say anything about our money"; Mother proposed skipping presents but never forbade spending. The other four statements appear directly in the passage. The forbidding choice tempts because self-denial is the spirit of Mother's proposal, yet the passage distinguishes her suggestion from a command.

passages.context: New England, during the Civil War: the four March sisters — Jo, Meg, Amy, and Beth — sit by the fire facing a Christmas without money for presents, while their father serves with the Union army far away.

passages.title: The Last Good Influence
passages.attribution: from The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson (1886)
passages.context: The novel opens not with its monster but with a portrait: Mr. Utterson, a dry London lawyer, and the puzzling weekend walks he takes with his kinsman Mr. Enfield.
passages.text:
MR. UTTERSON the lawyer was a man of a rugged countenance, that was never lighted by a smile; cold, scanty and embarrassed in discourse; backward in sentiment; lean, long, dusty, dreary, and yet somehow lovable. At friendly meetings, and when the wine was to his taste, something eminently human beaconed from his eye; something indeed which never found its way into his talk, but which spoke not only in these silent symbols of the after-dinner face, but more often and loudly in the acts of his life. He was austere with himself; drank gin when he was alone, to mortify a taste for vintages; and though he enjoyed the theatre, had not crossed the doors of one for twenty years. But he had an approved tolerance for others; sometimes wondering, almost with envy, at the high pressure of spirits involved in their misdeeds; and in any extremity inclined to help rather than to reprove.

"I incline to Cain's heresy," he used to say quaintly: "I let my brother go to the devil in his own way." In this character, it was frequently his fortune to be the last reputable acquaintance and the last good influence in the lives of down-going men. And to such as these, so long as they came about his chambers, he never marked a shade of change in his demeanour.

No doubt the feat was easy to Mr. Utterson; for he was undemonstrative at the best, and even his friendship seemed to be founded in a similar catholicity of good-nature. His friends were those of his own blood or those whom he had known the longest; his affections, like ivy, were the growth of time, they implied no aptness in the object. Hence, no doubt, the bond that united him to Mr. Richard Enfield, his distant kinsman, the well-known man about town. It was a nut to crack for many, what these two could see in each other, or what subject they could find in common. It was reported by those who encountered them in their Sunday walks, that they said nothing, looked singularly dull, and would hail with obvious relief the appearance of a friend. For all that, the two men put the greatest store by these excursions, counted them the chief jewel of each week, and not only set aside occasions of pleasure, but even resisted the calls of business, that they might enjoy them uninterrupted.

questions.prompt: The passage as a whole serves primarily to
choices: A) portray Mr. Utterson as an austere yet quietly humane man whose attachments defy easy explanation
B) explain why Mr. Utterson gave up attending the theatre
C) expose the hypocrisy of respectable London professionals
D) recount how Utterson and Enfield first became acquainted
E) establish Enfield as the story's central character
questions.explanation: Every detail — the dreary-yet-lovable face, the gin, the tolerance for others, the inexplicable walks — builds one portrait of Utterson. (C) is the trap: his contradictions are drawn with warmth, not unmasked as hypocrisy.

questions.prompt: As used in 'drank gin when he was alone, to mortify a taste for vintages,' the word 'mortify' most nearly means
choices: A) embarrass
B) discipline
C) satisfy
D) conceal
E) poison
questions.explanation: Utterson is 'austere with himself': he drinks the plainer spirit to punish and suppress his fondness for fine wine. (A) is the modern-sense trap — public embarrassment makes no sense for a strictly private habit.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when other people went wrong, Utterson was inclined to
choices: A) report them to the proper authorities
B) end the acquaintance at once
C) deliver stern lectures on their conduct
D) help rather than reprove
E) envy them too much to judge them at all
questions.explanation: Stated directly: 'in any extremity inclined to help rather than to reprove.' (E) twists a real detail — he wonders 'almost with envy' at their spirits, but the envy is at their vitality, and it does not replace judgment; helping does.

questions.prompt: From the statement that Utterson was frequently 'the last reputable acquaintance and the last good influence in the lives of down-going men,' it can most reasonably be inferred that
choices: A) men whose reputations were collapsing found that Utterson alone stood by them
B) Utterson's own reputation suffered from these associations
C) Utterson sought out disreputable company for entertainment
D) most of Utterson's friends eventually abandoned him
E) Utterson profited from his clients' misfortunes
questions.explanation: If he is the LAST reputable friend of 'down-going men,' the others must have fallen away while he remained — and 'he never marked a shade of change in his demeanour.' (B) invents a cost the passage never suggests; he stays 'reputable.'

questions.prompt: The description of Utterson as 'lean, long, dusty, dreary, and yet somehow lovable' establishes a tone toward him that is
choices: A) contemptuous
B) reverent
C) suspicious
D) wry but affectionate
E) indifferent
questions.explanation: The list piles up unflattering adjectives, then upends them with 'and yet somehow lovable' — teasing yet warm. (A) ignores that turn; the 'yet' is the whole point of the sentence.

questions.prompt: The passage states all of the following about the Sunday walks EXCEPT
choices: A) the two men said very little during them
B) observers found the pair singularly dull
C) the men used the walks to discuss points of business
D) the men counted the walks the chief jewel of each week
E) the men put aside pleasure, and even business, to keep them
questions.explanation: They 'said nothing' on the walks and 'even resisted the calls of business, that they might enjoy them uninterrupted' — the walks displaced business rather than hosting it. Every other statement appears on the page nearly word for word.

passages.title: The Herb of the Night
passages.attribution: from The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett (1896)
passages.text:
Mrs. Todd and I were not separated or estranged by the change in our business relations; on the contrary, a deeper intimacy seemed to begin. I do not know what herb of the night it was that used sometimes to send out a penetrating odor late in the evening, after the dew had fallen, and the moon was high, and the cool air came up from the sea. Then Mrs. Todd would feel that she must talk to somebody, and I was only too glad to listen. We both fell under the spell, and she either stood outside the window, or made an errand to my sitting-room, and told, it might be very commonplace news of the day, or, as happened one misty summer night, all that lay deepest in her heart. It was in this way that I came to know that she had loved one who was far above her.

"No, dear, him I speak of could never think of me," she said. "When we was young together his mother didn't favor the match, an' done everything she could to part us; and folks thought we both married well, but 't wa'n't what either one of us wanted most; an' now we're left alone again, an' might have had each other all the time. He was above bein' a seafarin' man, an' prospered more than most; he come of a high family, an' my lot was plain an' hard-workin'. I ain't seen him for some years; he's forgot our youthful feelin's, I expect, but a woman's heart is different; them feelin's comes back when you think you've done with 'em, as sure as spring comes with the year. An' I've always had ways of hearin' about him."

She stood in the centre of a braided rug, and its rings of black and gray seemed to circle about her feet in the dim light. Her height and massiveness in the low room gave her the look of a huge sibyl, while the strange fragrance of the mysterious herb blew in from the little garden.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) the healing properties of the herbs that Mrs. Todd gathers
B) a moment of confidence in which the narrator learns of Mrs. Todd's hidden sorrow
C) the narrator's regret over ending a business arrangement with Mrs. Todd
D) Mrs. Todd's plan to reunite with the man she loved in her youth
E) the daily routines of a small seaside village in summer
questions.explanation: The passage traces a "deeper intimacy" that culminates in Mrs. Todd revealing "all that lay deepest in her heart" — her lost love. (C) is tempting but reverses the opening, which says the change in business relations did NOT separate or estrange them; the herbs in (A) only frame the confession.

questions.prompt: According to Mrs. Todd, she and the man she loved were parted chiefly because
choices: A) he chose a life at sea over their engagement
B) his mother opposed the match and worked to separate them
C) she had already promised to marry someone else
D) his family lost its fortune and social standing
E) he gradually forgot her after moving away
questions.explanation: Mrs. Todd says plainly, "his mother didn't favor the match, an' done everything she could to part us." (E) is half-right: she suspects "he's forgot our youthful feelin's," but that came years after the separation, not as its cause. (A) and (D) contradict the text — he prospered and was "above bein' a seafarin' man."

questions.prompt: In the phrase "gave her the look of a huge sibyl," the word "sibyl" most nearly means
choices: A) a grieving widow
B) a practiced healer
C) a mysterious prophetess
D) an imposing statue
E) a watchful guardian
questions.explanation: A sibyl is an ancient oracle or seeress; paired with "the strange fragrance of the mysterious herb" and the circling rings of the rug, the word casts Mrs. Todd as a figure of uncanny, prophetic wisdom. (B) tempts because she deals in herbs, but the sentence describes her mystical aspect, not her medical skill.

questions.prompt: Mrs. Todd's remark that she has "always had ways of hearin' about him" most strongly suggests that she
choices: A) has never entirely given up her attachment to him
B) intends to seek him out now that they are both alone
C) keeps up a regular correspondence with his family
D) blames him for the hardships of her working life
E) discusses him openly with her neighbors in the village
questions.explanation: Quietly keeping track of him for years, along with her claim that "them feelin's comes back... as sure as spring," shows an attachment that never died. (B) goes too far: she insists "him I speak of could never think of me," expecting nothing. (C) and (E) invent specifics the passage never supports.

questions.prompt: In recalling the man she loved, Mrs. Todd's tone is best described as
choices: A) bitter and accusing
B) wistful yet resigned
C) lighthearted and dismissive
D) hopeful and expectant
E) fearful and evasive
questions.explanation: She dwells on what "either one of us wanted most" and how they "might have had each other all the time," yet accepts that he "could never think of me" — longing without expectation. (D) fails because, though she says feelings return, she voices no hope of reunion; nothing in her speech is bitter or accusing.

questions.prompt: The narrator's closing description of Mrs. Todd on the braided rug, its "rings of black and gray" seeming "to circle about her feet," chiefly serves to
choices: A) reveal the poverty of Mrs. Todd's household furnishings
B) suggest that Mrs. Todd secretly performs magic rituals on her neighbors
C) transform the familiar landlady into a figure of mysterious grandeur
D) explain why the narrator remained awake so late into the evening
E) show that the narrator has begun to fear her hostess
questions.explanation: After the intimate confession, the circling rings, her "height and massiveness," and the comparison to "a huge sibyl" elevate an ordinary landlady into something timeless and oracular. (B) overreads the atmosphere as literal ritual, and nothing suggests fear (E) or comments on her poverty (A).

passages.context: The narrator, a writer boarding for the summer in a small Maine seaside village, has grown close to her landlady, Mrs. Todd, a gatherer and seller of herbs.

passages.title: For Your Mother's Sake
passages.attribution: from Dracula by Bram Stoker (1897)
passages.context: Jonathan Harker, a young English lawyer, keeps a journal as he travels through Transylvania to advise a foreign nobleman, Count Dracula, on buying property in England. On the morning of May 4th, as he is about to leave for the castle, his innkeeper's wife comes up to his room.
passages.text:
Just before I was leaving, the old lady came up to my room and said in a very hysterical way:

“Must you go? Oh! young Herr, must you go?” When I told her that I must go at once, and that I was engaged on important business, she asked again:

“Do you know what day it is?” I answered that it was the fourth of May. She shook her head as she said again:

“Oh, yes! I know that! I know that, but do you know what day it is?” On my saying that I did not understand, she went on:

“It is the eve of St. George’s Day. Do you not know that to-night, when the clock strikes midnight, all the evil things in the world will have full sway? Do you know where you are going, and what you are going to?”

She was in such evident distress that I tried to comfort her, but without effect. Finally she went down on her knees and implored me not to go; at least to wait a day or two before starting. It was all very ridiculous but I did not feel comfortable. However, there was business to be done, and I could allow nothing to interfere with it. I therefore tried to raise her up, and said, as gravely as I could, that I thanked her, but my duty was imperative, and that I must go. She then rose and dried her eyes, and taking a crucifix from her neck offered it to me. I did not know what to do, for, as an English Churchman, I have been taught to regard such things as in some measure idolatrous, and yet it seemed so ungracious to refuse an old lady meaning so well and in such a state of mind. She saw, I suppose, the doubt in my face, for she put the rosary round my neck, and said, “For your mother’s sake,” and went out of the room. I am writing up this part of the diary whilst I am waiting for the coach, which is, of course, late; and the crucifix is still round my neck. Whether it is the old lady’s fear, or the many ghostly traditions of this place, or the crucifix itself, I do not know, but I am not feeling nearly as easy in my mind as usual. If this book should ever reach Mina before I do, let it bring my good-bye. Here comes the coach!

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes what happens in the passage?
choices: A) A traveler records his landlady's desperate warnings and parting gift, then admits an unease he cannot reason away
B) A landlady persuades her guest to postpone a dangerous journey
C) A young lawyer abandons his business out of respect for local custom
D) A traveler openly mocks the villagers' superstitions and offends his hosts
E) A guest and his hostess quarrel over the lateness of the coach
questions.explanation: She begs him to wait, he refuses ('my duty was imperative'), accepts her crucifix, and ends 'not feeling nearly as easy in my mind as usual.' (B) fails — he leaves anyway. (D) fails — whatever he privately thinks, he answers 'as gravely as I could,' offending no one.

questions.prompt: According to the landlady, the danger of that particular night is that
choices: A) the mountain roads become impassable after dark
B) it is the eve of St. George's Day, when at midnight all evil things have full sway
C) Count Dracula refuses visitors who arrive after midnight
D) travelers have recently been robbed near the pass
E) no coach will run again until after the feast day
questions.explanation: 'It is the eve of St. George's Day. Do you not know that to-night, when the clock strikes midnight, all the evil things in the world will have full sway?' The other choices name dangers the passage never mentions.

questions.prompt: As used in 'I have been taught to regard such things as in some measure idolatrous,' the word 'idolatrous' most nearly means
choices: A) involving the worship of objects
B) charmingly old-fashioned
C) scientifically unsound
D) needlessly expensive
E) protective against evil
questions.explanation: As 'an English Churchman,' Harker was taught to see devotion directed at objects like crucifixes as misdirected worship — idolatry. (E) reverses the perspective: protection is what the landlady believes the crucifix offers, not what the word means.

questions.prompt: Harker's remark that 'It was all very ridiculous but I did not feel comfortable' most strongly suggests that
choices: A) his rational dismissal of the warnings is beginning to fail him
B) he has decided to turn back at the first opportunity
C) he now fully believes the landlady's account of evil spirits
D) he is chiefly annoyed that the coach is running late
E) he regrets having accepted the Count's business
questions.explanation: He labels the scene absurd yet cannot shake it: by the entry's end he is 'not feeling nearly as easy in my mind as usual.' (C) is too extreme — discomfort is not belief, and he still calls it all 'ridiculous.'

questions.prompt: Harker's attitude toward the landlady is best described as
choices: A) contempt thinly disguised as courtesy
B) sympathetic and gentle, though unpersuaded
C) fearful obedience
D) complete and grateful belief
E) amused indifference
questions.explanation: He 'tried to comfort her,' answers 'as gravely as I could,' and accepts the rosary rather than be 'ungracious to an old lady meaning so well.' (A) is the trap: 'ridiculous' targets the situation in private; his conduct toward her stays kind.

questions.prompt: The detail that 'the crucifix is still round my neck' as Harker waits for the coach primarily serves to
choices: A) suggest that, despite his skepticism, Harker is keeping the protection he was given
B) demonstrate that Harker fears the landlady will return to check on him
C) establish that the crucifix is too valuable to leave behind
D) prove that Harker has adopted the local faith
E) remind the reader that the coach is late
questions.explanation: He cannot say why he is uneasy — 'the old lady's fear... or the crucifix itself, I do not know' — yet the token stays on, quietly contradicting 'ridiculous.' (D) overshoots: wearing a well-meant gift is not conversion; he still calls such objects 'idolatrous.'

passages.title: The Hunger Strike
passages.attribution: from His New Mittens by Stephen Crane (1898)
passages.text:
Alone in the kitchen, Horace stared with somber eyes at the plate of food. For a long time he betrayed no sign of yielding. His mood was adamantine. He was resolved not to sell his vengeance for bread, cold ham, and a pickle, and yet it must be known that the sight of them affected him powerfully. The pickle in particular was notable for its seductive charm. He surveyed it darkly.

But at last, unable to longer endure his state, his attitude in the presence of the pickle, he put out an inquisitive finger and touched it, and it was cool and green and plump. Then a full conception of the cruel woe of his situation swept upon him suddenly, and his eyes filled with tears, which began to move down his cheeks. He sniffled. His heart was black with hatred. He painted in his mind scenes of deadly retribution. His mother would be taught that he was not one to endure persecution meetly, without raising an arm in his defense. And so his dreams were of a slaughter of feelings, and near the end of them his mother was pictured as coming, bowed with pain, to his feet. Weeping, she implored his charity. Would he forgive her? No; his once tender heart had been turned to stone by her injustice. He could not forgive her. She must pay the inexorable penalty.

The first item in this horrible plan was the refusal of the food. This he knew by experience would work havoc in his mother's heart. And so he grimly waited.

But suddenly it occurred to him that the first part of his revenge was in danger of failing. The thought struck him that his mother might not capitulate in the usual way. According to his recollection, the time was more than due when she should come in, worried, sadly affectionate, and ask him if he was ill. It had then been his custom to hint in a resigned voice that he was the victim of secret disease, but that he preferred to suffer in silence and alone. If she was obdurate in her anxiety, he always asked her in a gloomy, low voice to go away and leave him to suffer in silence and alone in the darkness without food. He had known this manoeuvering to result even in pie.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) a mother's unusually harsh punishment of her disobedient son
B) a boy's theatrical scheme to make his mother regret punishing him
C) a starving child's desperate struggle to resist the sight of food
D) the lasting damage that injustice inflicts on a tender heart
E) a boy's successful revenge upon his entire family
questions.explanation: The passage traces Horace's plan to punish his mother by refusing supper, narrated with irony. (D) is the trap: it takes the narrator's mock-serious words ("injustice," "persecution") at face value, when the passage frames them as a child's self-dramatizing fantasy, not fact.

questions.prompt: In the sentence "His mood was adamantine," the word "adamantine" most nearly means
choices: A) sorrowful
B) dazzling
C) vengeful
D) unyielding
E) restless
questions.explanation: The preceding sentence glosses the word: "he betrayed no sign of yielding," so adamantine means hard and unyielding. (C) tempts because Horace is plotting revenge, but that describes his motive, not the meaning of the word, which comes from "adamant," a legendary unbreakable stone.

questions.prompt: The statement that Horace "had known this manoeuvering to result even in pie" implies that Horace
choices: A) has successfully used displays of suffering to win his mother's sympathy before
B) is usually rewarded with dessert for good behavior
C) genuinely believes he is the victim of a secret disease
D) belongs to a family that can rarely afford such treats
E) intends to hold out until his mother offers him pie
questions.explanation: "Had known... to result" reports past outcomes: his rehearsed performance of silent suffering has paid off before, sometimes lavishly. (E) fails because the sentence recalls previous successes rather than stating a present demand; pie is evidence of the ruse's history, not his current goal.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Horace's dreams of "deadly retribution" can best be described as
choices: A) bitter contempt
B) solemn pity
C) detached indifference
D) mounting alarm
E) amused irony
questions.explanation: Grand language ("deadly retribution," "a slaughter of feelings," "the inexorable penalty") applied to a boy sulking over supper creates comic exaggeration; the narrator is entertained, not worried. (B) tempts because some sympathy is present, but the mock-heroic tone is playful rather than grave.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, in Horace's imagined revenge his mother finally
choices: A) promises never to punish him again
B) falls gravely ill from worry
C) comes weeping to beg a forgiveness he refuses to grant
D) offers him pie as a peace offering
E) comes to understand and pardon his disobedience
questions.explanation: In the fantasy she comes "bowed with pain, to his feet. Weeping, she implored his charity. Would he forgive her? No." (D) is the trap: it confuses the imagined scene with Horace's separate real-life memory that the ruse once produced pie.

questions.prompt: The author most likely notes that the pickle "was notable for its seductive charm" in order to
choices: A) suggest that Horace has eaten nothing since morning
B) comically undercut the grandeur of Horace's vengeance with a trivial temptation
C) illustrate the mother's skill at preparing appealing meals
D) foreshadow Horace's decision to abandon his plan
E) emphasize how meager the family's supper is
questions.explanation: Pitting a solemn vow of vengeance against a "cool and green and plump" pickle deflates Horace's grim resolve for comic effect—the passage's central irony. (D) fails because within the passage Horace never abandons the plan; the detail creates humor, not foreshadowing.

passages.context: Little Horace has been marched home in disgrace by his aunt after snowballing in his new mittens. Punished by his mother, he sits alone in the kitchen and refuses to touch his supper.

passages.title: Lying in Wait for the Oracle
passages.attribution: from When I Knew Stephen Crane by Willa Cather (1900)
passages.text:
Crane spent several days in the town, living from hand to mouth and waiting for his money. I think he borrowed a small amount from the managing editor. He lounged about the office most of the time, and I frequently encountered him going in and out of the cheap restaurants on Tenth Street. When he was at the office he talked a good deal in a wandering, absent-minded fashion, and his conversation was uniformly frivolous. If he could not evade a serious question by a joke, he bolted. I cut my classes to lie in wait for him, confident that in some unwary moment I could trap him into serious conversation, that if one burned incense long enough and ardently enough, the oracle would not be dumb. I was Maupassant mad at the time, a malady particularly unattractive in a Junior, and I made a frantic effort to get an expression of opinion from him on "Le Bonheur." "Oh, you're Moping, are you?" he remarked with a sarcastic grin, and went on reading a little volume of Poe that he carried in his pocket. At another time I cornered him in the Funny Man's room and succeeded in getting a little out of him. We were taught literature by an exceedingly analytical method at the University, and we probably distorted the method, and I was busy trying to find the least common multiple of Hamlet and the greatest common divisor of Macbeth, and I began asking him whether stories were constructed by cabalistic formulae. At length he sighed wearily and shook his drooping shoulders, remarking:

"Where did you get all that rot? Yarns aren't done by mathematics. You can't do it by rule any more than you can dance by rule. You have to have the itch of the thing in your fingers, and if you haven't,—well, you're damned lucky, and you'll live long and prosper, that's all."—And with that he yawned and went down the hall.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the passage as a whole?
choices: A) An explanation of the methods by which a famous author composed his stories
B) An argument that literature cannot be taught by the analytical methods used in universities
C) An account of a young admirer's persistent attempts to draw a reluctant writer into serious conversation
D) A criticism of Crane's rudeness toward the students who admired him
E) A description of the daily operations of a small-town newspaper office
questions.explanation: The passage turns on the narrator cutting classes to "lie in wait" for Crane, his evasions, and the outburst she finally provokes. (B) mistakes Crane's own claim—that yarns "aren't done by mathematics"—for the author's thesis; the passage recounts an encounter rather than making that argument.

questions.prompt: In the phrase "the oracle would not be dumb," the word "dumb" most nearly means
choices: A) foolish
B) confused
C) stubborn
D) unintelligent
E) silent
questions.explanation: The narrator hopes that if she "burned incense long enough," the oracle (Crane) would finally speak; "dumb" means mute or silent. "Foolish" and "unintelligent" import the word's modern slang sense, which contradicts the image of an oracle withholding its pronouncements.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when Crane "could not evade a serious question by a joke," he
choices: A) left abruptly
B) responded with a sarcastic grin
C) pretended to be absorbed in a volume of Poe
D) referred the questioner to the managing editor
E) answered briefly and then changed the subject
questions.explanation: The passage states directly that in such cases "he bolted." Choices (B) and (C) describe his response to the "Le Bonheur" question—but that was an evasion by joke that succeeded, not what he did when a joke failed.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that when Crane replies "Oh, you're Moping, are you?" he is
choices: A) expressing concern that the narrator seems unhappy
B) confessing that he has never read Maupassant
C) inviting the narrator to discuss "Le Bonheur" at a later time
D) deliberately mangling the name Maupassant to tease the narrator and dodge her question
E) quoting a line from the volume of Poe he carries in his pocket
questions.explanation: The narrator, "Maupassant mad," presses him about "Le Bonheur"; Crane answers with a mock mispronunciation, a "sarcastic grin," and returns to his book—a joke used to evade. (A) takes "Moping" literally, but the grin and his habit of evading serious questions mark the remark as mockery.

questions.prompt: In calling her enthusiasm for Maupassant "a malady particularly unattractive in a Junior," the narrator treats her younger self with
choices: A) bitter regret
B) amused self-mockery
C) defensive pride
D) cold indifference
E) open admiration
questions.explanation: Calling her devotion a "malady," her effort "frantic," and admitting she "cut my classes to lie in wait" shows the older narrator gently ridiculing her youthful excess. "Bitter regret" is too extreme: the recollection is comic and affectionate, with no note of remorse.

questions.prompt: Crane most likely says "You can't do it by rule any more than you can dance by rule" in order to
choices: A) admit that he himself never studied the rules of writing
B) suggest that writers should train as rigorously as dancers do
C) insist that storytelling springs from instinct rather than from formulas
D) prove that dancing is a more difficult art than writing
E) warn the narrator that she is certain to fail as a writer
questions.explanation: The analogy answers the narrator's question about "cabalistic formulae": like dancing, writing demands "the itch of the thing in your fingers"—innate feel, not rules. (E) is too extreme; his "you're damned lucky" is a wry aside about the writing life, not a judgment of the narrator's prospects.

passages.context: Willa Cather recalls her days as a college junior and campus journalist in Nebraska, when Stephen Crane — a famous young writer — passed through town, broke and waiting for a paycheck.

passages.title: The King of Judge Miller's Place
passages.attribution: from The Call of the Wild by Jack London (1903)
passages.context: Buck, a big, comfortable family dog, rules a California estate in 1897 — just as the Klondike gold rush creates a sudden demand in the far North for strong dogs like him.
passages.text:
Buck did not read the newspapers, or he would have known that trouble was brewing, not alone for himself, but for every tide-water dog, strong of muscle and with warm, long hair, from Puget Sound to San Diego. Because men, groping in the Arctic darkness, had found a yellow metal, and because steamship and transportation companies were booming the find, thousands of men were rushing into the Northland. These men wanted dogs, and the dogs they wanted were heavy dogs, with strong muscles by which to toil, and furry coats to protect them from the frost.

Buck lived at a big house in the sun-kissed Santa Clara Valley. Judge Miller's place, it was called. It stood back from the road, half hidden among the trees, through which glimpses could be caught of the wide cool veranda that ran around its four sides. And over this great demesne Buck ruled. Here he was born, and here he had lived the four years of his life. It was true, there were other dogs, There could not but be other dogs on so vast a place, but they did not count. They came and went, resided in the populous kennels, or lived obscurely in the recesses of the house after the fashion of Toots, the Japanese pug, or Ysabel, the Mexican hairless,—strange creatures that rarely put nose out of doors or set foot to ground. On the other hand, there were the fox terriers, a score of them at least, who yelped fearful promises at Toots and Ysabel looking out of the windows at them and protected by a legion of housemaids armed with brooms and mops.

But Buck was neither house-dog nor kennel-dog. The whole realm was his. He plunged into the swimming tank or went hunting with the Judge's sons; he escorted Mollie and Alice, the Judge's daughters, on long twilight or early morning rambles; on wintry nights he lay at the Judge's feet before the roaring library fire; he carried the Judge's grandsons on his back, or rolled them in the grass, and guarded their footsteps through wild adventures down to the fountain in the stable yard, and even beyond, where the paddocks were, and the berry patches. Among the terriers he stalked imperiously, and Toots and Ysabel he utterly ignored, for he was king,—king over all creeping, crawling, flying things of Judge Miller's place, humans included.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main purpose of the passage?
choices: A) To explain why gold seekers in the Northland needed heavy, long-haired dogs
B) To introduce Buck's privileged, lordly life just as distant events gather to upend it
C) To compare Buck's strength with that of the other dogs on Judge Miller's estate
D) To describe the layout and routines of a wealthy California household
E) To argue that pampered dogs make poor workers
questions.explanation: The opening — Buck 'did not read the newspapers, or he would have known that trouble was brewing' — hangs coming upheaval over every cozy detail that follows. (A) is the trap: true, but it covers only the first paragraph, which exists to threaten Buck's world, not to explain mining.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, thousands of men rushed into the Northland because
choices: A) steamship companies offered them free passage
B) they had been hired to breed sled dogs
C) a 'yellow metal' had been found and transport companies were promoting the discovery
D) farmland there had become newly cheap
E) newspapers exaggerated the wealth to be found there
questions.explanation: 'Because men... had found a yellow metal, and because steamship and transportation companies were booming the find.' (A) distorts the companies' role — they publicized ('boomed') the find; nothing says passage was free.

questions.prompt: As used in 'And over this great demesne Buck ruled,' the word 'demesne' most nearly means
choices: A) family
B) climate
C) fortune
D) estate
E) kennel
questions.explanation: The 'demesne' is everything just described — the house, grounds, and lands that are Buck's territory: an estate. (E) is the trap the passage itself rejects: Buck 'was neither house-dog nor kennel-dog. The whole realm was his.'

questions.prompt: It can most reasonably be inferred from the first paragraph that
choices: A) Buck will soon be caught up in the demand for strong, warm-coated dogs
B) Buck is too old to be of use to the men of the Northland
C) Judge Miller plans to sell Buck to a transportation company
D) dogs from warm regions cannot survive in the Arctic
E) the trouble brewing concerns only dogs that live near the sea
questions.explanation: Trouble brews 'for every tide-water dog, strong of muscle and with warm, long hair' — and the rest of the passage establishes Buck as exactly such a dog. (C) goes too far: the threat gathers outside the estate; no plan of the Judge's is mentioned.

questions.prompt: The narrator's statement that Buck was 'king,—king over all creeping, crawling, flying things of Judge Miller's place, humans included' is best described as
choices: A) bitter and resentful
B) anxious and foreboding
C) playfully grand
D) coldly scientific
E) mocking Buck's vanity
questions.explanation: Crowning a dog king over the humans is affectionate mock-majesty; the paragraph celebrates Buck's easy rule. (B) is tempting because the first paragraph forebodes trouble, but this sentence itself is comic-royal, not ominous.

questions.prompt: The description of Toots, Ysabel, and the fox terriers primarily serves to
choices: A) suggest that Judge Miller preferred small, delicate breeds
B) set Buck's free run of the whole estate against the confined lives of the other dogs
C) show that the estate was a dangerous place for small animals
D) explain why Buck slept indoors on winter nights
E) demonstrate the housemaids' authority over the grounds
questions.explanation: Toots and Ysabel 'rarely put nose out of doors,' and the terriers live behind a 'legion of housemaids' — while Buck 'was neither house-dog nor kennel-dog. The whole realm was his.' The little dogs exist to be measured against him. (A) reverses the emphasis.

passages.title: City Clothes
passages.attribution: from My Ántonia by Willa Cather (1918)
passages.text:
I hurried to the door and when I opened it saw a woman standing in the dark hall.

'I expect you hardly know me, Jim.'

The voice seemed familiar, but I did not recognize her until she stepped into the light of my doorway and I beheld—Lena Lingard! She was so quietly conventionalized by city clothes that I might have passed her on the street without seeing her. Her black suit fitted her figure smoothly, and a black lace hat, with pale-blue forget-me-nots, sat demurely on her yellow hair.

I led her toward Cleric's chair, the only comfortable one I had, questioning her confusedly.

She was not disconcerted by my embarrassment. She looked about her with the naive curiosity I remembered so well. 'You are quite comfortable here, aren't you? I live in Lincoln now, too, Jim. I'm in business for myself. I have a dressmaking shop in the Raleigh Block, out on O Street. I've made a real good start.'

'But, Lena, when did you come?'

'Oh, I've been here all winter. Didn't your grandmother ever write you? I've thought about looking you up lots of times. But we've all heard what a studious young man you've got to be, and I felt bashful. I didn't know whether you'd be glad to see me.' She laughed her mellow, easy laugh, that was either very artless or very comprehending, one never quite knew which. 'You seem the same, though—except you're a young man, now, of course. Do you think I've changed?'

'Maybe you're prettier—though you were always pretty enough. Perhaps it's your clothes that make a difference.'

'You like my new suit? I have to dress pretty well in my business.'

She took off her jacket and sat more at ease in her blouse, of some soft, flimsy silk. She was already at home in my place, had slipped quietly into it, as she did into everything. She told me her business was going well, and she had saved a little money.

I watched Lena sitting there so smooth and sunny and well-cared-for, and thought of how she used to run barefoot over the prairie until after the snow began to fly, and how Crazy Mary chased her round and round the cornfields. It seemed to me wonderful that she should have got on so well in the world.

questions.prompt: This passage is primarily about
choices: A) the difficulties of running a dressmaking business in a frontier city
B) a surprise reunion in which the narrator marvels at how an old acquaintance has risen in the world
C) the narrator's regret that a visitor has interrupted his evening of study
D) a quarrel between two childhood friends who have grown apart
E) the hardships Lena endured during her girlhood on the prairie
questions.explanation: The scene builds to Jim's astonishment that Lena has 'got on so well in the world': an unrecognizable, prosperous visitor from his past. E tempts because her barefoot girlhood is mentioned, but only briefly, as a contrast—it is not the passage's focus.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'disconcerted' in 'She was not disconcerted by my embarrassment' most nearly means
choices: A) discouraged
B) amused
C) offended
D) convinced
E) flustered
questions.explanation: Jim questions her 'confusedly,' yet Lena stays composed, looking about with easy curiosity—so 'not disconcerted' means not flustered or unsettled. 'Discouraged' echoes the dis- prefix, but the contrast here is between Jim's fluster and Lena's calm, not between hope and despair.

questions.prompt: The narrator's remark that Lena's laugh 'was either very artless or very comprehending, one never quite knew which' suggests that
choices: A) he cannot decide whether Lena is genuinely innocent or quietly aware of her own effect
B) Lena laughs deliberately in order to deceive the people around her
C) Lena has become cold and calculating since leaving the country
D) the narrator no longer remembers what Lena was like as a girl
E) Lena is nervous and laughs to disguise her embarrassment
questions.explanation: 'One never quite knew which' states genuine uncertainty between innocence ('artless') and knowingness ('comprehending'). B goes too far—finding her unreadable is not an accusation of deceit—and D is contradicted by the curiosity he 'remembered so well.'

questions.prompt: According to Lena, she put off visiting Jim because she
choices: A) was too busy with her dressmaking shop to spare an evening
B) assumed his grandmother had already told him of her arrival
C) had heard he was studious and felt bashful about calling on him
D) feared he would not recognize her in her new city clothes
E) had only recently arrived in Lincoln
questions.explanation: Lena says it directly: 'we've all heard what a studious young man you've got to be, and I felt bashful.' B tempts because she asks whether his grandmother wrote, but that is a question, not her stated reason; E is contradicted by 'I've been here all winter.'

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Lena's success is best described as
choices: A) envious resentment
B) polite indifference
C) admiring wonder
D) amused condescension
E) wary suspicion
questions.explanation: He finds it 'wonderful' that she has 'got on so well in the world,' and his description of her—'smooth and sunny and well-cared-for'—is warm. D fails because recalling her barefoot past sets up admiring contrast; nothing in his framing looks down on her.

questions.prompt: The narrator recalls how Lena 'used to run barefoot over the prairie' primarily in order to
choices: A) suggest that Lena secretly misses her country childhood
B) imply that Lena is ashamed of her humble origins
C) explain why Crazy Mary chased her around the cornfields
D) highlight the contrast between her rough girlhood and her polished present
E) criticize Lena for adopting fashionable city manners
questions.explanation: The barefoot memory is set directly against Lena 'so smooth and sunny and well-cared-for,' measuring how far she has risen. C reverses the logic—Crazy Mary belongs to the same remembered picture and is not what the detail is there to explain.

passages.context: Jim Burden, who grew up in a Nebraska prairie town, is now a university student in Lincoln. One evening there is a knock at his door — Lena Lingard, a country girl from home, now a dressmaker in the city.

passages.title: The Purse Strings
passages.attribution: from The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie (1920)
passages.context: Captain Hastings, sent home wounded from the First World War, runs into an old acquaintance, John Cavendish, who invites him to recuperate at Styles Court, the family's country estate. Here Hastings recalls the household he is about to rejoin.
passages.text:
I had been invalided home from the Front; and, after spending some months in a rather depressing Convalescent Home, was given a month's sick leave. Having no near relations or friends, I was trying to make up my mind what to do, when I ran across John Cavendish. I had seen very little of him for some years. Indeed, I had never known him particularly well. He was a good fifteen years my senior, for one thing, though he hardly looked his forty-five years. As a boy, though, I had often stayed at Styles, his mother's place in Essex.

We had a good yarn about old times, and it ended in his inviting me down to Styles to spend my leave there.

"The mater will be delighted to see you again--after all those years," he added.

"Your mother keeps well?" I asked.

"Oh, yes. I suppose you know that she has married again?"

I am afraid I showed my surprise rather plainly. Mrs. Cavendish, who had married John's father when he was a widower with two sons, had been a handsome woman of middle-age as I remembered her. She certainly could not be a day less than seventy now. I recalled her as an energetic, autocratic personality, somewhat inclined to charitable and social notoriety, with a fondness for opening bazaars and playing the Lady Bountiful. She was a most generous woman, and possessed a considerable fortune of her own.

Their country-place, Styles Court, had been purchased by Mr. Cavendish early in their married life. He had been completely under his wife's ascendancy, so much so that, on dying, he left the place to her for her lifetime, as well as the larger part of his income; an arrangement that was distinctly unfair to his two sons. Their step-mother, however, had always been most generous to them; indeed, they were so young at the time of their father's remarriage that they always thought of her as their own mother. John practiced for some time as a barrister, but had finally settled down to the more congenial life of a country squire. He had married two years ago, and had taken his wife to live at Styles, though I entertained a shrewd suspicion that he would have preferred his mother to increase his allowance, which would have enabled him to have a home of his own. Mrs. Cavendish, however, was a lady who liked to make her own plans, and expected other people to fall in with them, and in this case she certainly had the whip hand, namely: the purse strings.

questions.prompt: The passage primarily serves to
choices: A) introduce the narrator and sketch the wealthy, strong-willed stepmother on whose money the family depends
B) recount the narrator's experiences at the Front
C) explain how Styles Court came to be put up for sale
D) describe John Cavendish's career as a barrister
E) celebrate Mrs. Cavendish's charitable achievements
questions.explanation: Hastings's invitation is only the frame; the substance is the stepmother — 'energetic, autocratic,' rich, newly remarried — and everyone's dependence on her 'purse strings.' (E) mistakes a detail for the point: her charity illustrates her self-image, with a wry edge.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when John's father died he left
choices: A) Styles Court to his sons and his income to his wife
B) the estate to his wife for her lifetime, along with the larger part of his income
C) everything to charity, at his wife's urging
D) the property in equal shares to the whole family
