E) instructions that Styles be sold and the money divided
questions.explanation: 'On dying, he left the place to her for her lifetime, as well as the larger part of his income; an arrangement that was distinctly unfair to his two sons.' (A) reverses the arrangement exactly.

questions.prompt: As used in 'an energetic, autocratic personality,' the word 'autocratic' most nearly means
choices: A) generous
B) elderly
C) domineering
D) sociable
E) dishonest
questions.explanation: This is a woman who 'liked to make her own plans, and expected other people to fall in with them' — one who rules by her own will. (A) is the trap: the passage separately calls her 'most generous,' a different trait from what 'autocratic' means.

questions.prompt: The narrator's 'shrewd suspicion' about John most strongly suggests that
choices: A) John resents living at Styles under his stepmother's financial control
B) John intends to contest his father's will in court
C) John's marriage has made him indifferent to money
D) John hopes Hastings will lend him money
E) John would prefer to return to practicing law
questions.explanation: Hastings suspects John 'would have preferred his mother to increase his allowance, which would have enabled him to have a home of his own' — dependence, quietly chafed at. (B) escalates far beyond anything stated.

questions.prompt: The narrator's account of Mrs. Cavendish — 'playing the Lady Bountiful,' holding 'the whip hand, namely: the purse strings' — is best described as
choices: A) openly hostile
B) worshipful
C) wryly ironic
D) fearful
E) pitying
questions.explanation: 'Playing the Lady Bountiful' and the dry 'whip hand... purse strings' poke gentle fun at her self-presentation and power while still granting that she is generous — irony, not attack. (A) overstates it; he concedes she was 'a most generous woman.'

questions.prompt: The passage states all of the following about Mrs. Cavendish EXCEPT that she
choices: A) possessed a considerable fortune of her own
B) had always been generous to her stepsons
C) was fond of charitable and social prominence
D) could not be a day less than seventy
E) regretted her recent remarriage
questions.explanation: Her remarriage is reported — 'she has married again' — with no hint of her own feelings about it. Choices (A) through (D) are all stated nearly word for word in the passage.

passages.title: The Water Charmer
passages.attribution: original passage in the style of Laurie Lee
passages.text:
The summer I was seven, the well went quiet. I knew it first by the pump, which had always coughed up water like an old donkey and now gave only a dry wheeze and a breath of iron. The garden shrank and stiffened; the beans rattled on their sticks; the pond at the field's foot dwindled to a saucer of cracked brown china, printed with the arrows of birds' feet. Mother stood at the kitchen door with an empty jug, studying the sky as though it owed her money.

Then one morning Mr. Tansy came up the lane, and I knew at once that he was a magician. He was a small whiskered man in a coat gone green at the seams, carrying only a forked hazel twig, the bark still wet at the split. Farmers trailed behind him, caps in hands, as though following a hearse. My brothers said he could smell water. Uncle Ray said he was an old fraud who charged half a crown whether he found any or not. But I had seen the twig and knew better: it was a wand, and he would charm the water out of the ground as the man at the fair had charmed a penny from my ear.

He walked the top field for an hour, slow as a heron, the twig held before him, eyes half shut. Nobody spoke. Even the rooks seemed to hold their gossip. Then, by the elder hedge, the twig turned in his hands — turned by itself, wrenching downward like a live fish making for the river. I stood rooted. The water, I understood, had answered him. It was alive down there, coiled and listening, and it had heard its name called.

The men dug for three days and struck the spring at thirty feet, and everyone drank the first bucket like christening wine. All that autumn I went about the fields with forked twigs of my own, whispering, holding my breath, waiting for the ground to speak. It never did; and when I complained, Mother laughed and said the gift was in the man, not the stick — which only proved what I had long suspected: that the grown-ups kept a private language, and would not teach it to me.

questions.prompt: The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with
choices: A) proving that water divining is a genuine though mysterious skill
B) recalling a childhood episode in which the narrator mistook a practical adult skill for magic
C) describing the hardships a farming family suffered during a ruinous drought
D) criticizing skeptical adults like Uncle Ray who dismiss what they cannot explain
E) explaining the traditional methods once used to locate underground springs
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the child's belief that Mr. Tansy was a magician and the water 'alive' — a misreading the adult narrator recalls fondly. C is tempting but half-right: the drought is only the setting for the episode, not the passage's focus, and it is never called ruinous.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Uncle Ray regarded Mr. Tansy as
choices: A) a magician with a genuine gift for finding water
B) a skilled tradesman who earned his fee honestly
C) a cheat who collected his money whether or not he succeeded
D) a dignified visitor to be followed with 'caps in hands'
E) a performer like the penny-charming man at the fair
questions.explanation: Uncle Ray called him 'an old fraud who charged half a crown whether he found any or not.' A and E describe the child's view, not Uncle Ray's; D describes the farmers who trailed behind him. The trap is attributing another character's attitude to Uncle Ray.

questions.prompt: The description of Mother 'studying the sky as though it owed her money' most strongly suggests that she
choices: A) was calculating what a newly dug well would cost the family
B) felt the weather had unfairly withheld the rain she was due
C) suspected that Mr. Tansy intended to cheat the family
D) had lent money to a neighboring farmer before the drought
E) had given up all hope that the dry summer would ever end
questions.explanation: The simile pictures Mother eyeing the sky like a creditor: rain is a debt the sky has failed to pay. A and D take 'owed her money' literally, the classic trap of reading a figure of speech as fact. E is too extreme — resentful waiting is not despair.

questions.prompt: In the sentence 'I stood rooted,' the word 'rooted' most nearly means
choices: A) planted firmly in the soil
B) established by long tradition
C) digging or searching through something
D) unable to move from wonder
E) supported by solid evidence
questions.explanation: Watching the twig that 'turned by itself,' the awestruck child freezes in place — 'rooted' here means transfixed, motionless. A is the literal trap, echoing the passage's plants and soil; B, C, and E are real senses of 'root' that do not fit a boy standing spellbound in a field.

questions.prompt: The adult narrator's attitude toward his childhood belief in Mr. Tansy's magic is best described as
choices: A) lingering resentment at having been deceived
B) detached scientific curiosity about dowsing
C) affectionate amusement at his younger self
D) unshaken conviction that the magic was real
E) embarrassment he would rather not revisit
questions.explanation: The narrator retells his errors warmly and with humor — the wand, the listening water, the autumn spent whispering to twigs. D is the trap: the boy's certainty ('I had seen the twig and knew better') belongs to the child, while the retrospective, wry framing shows the adult no longer shares it. Nothing signals resentment or shame.

questions.prompt: All of the following are mentioned as signs of the dry summer EXCEPT
choices: A) a pump that gave 'only a dry wheeze'
B) beans that 'rattled on their sticks'
C) a pond shrunk to 'a saucer of cracked brown china'
D) a garden that 'shrank and stiffened'
E) an elder hedge that had lost its leaves
questions.explanation: The pump, beans, pond, and garden all appear in the opening catalogue of drought signs. The elder hedge is mentioned only as the spot where the twig dipped — nothing says it lost its leaves. The trap is recognizing a familiar phrase from the passage without checking what is actually said about it.

passages.context: A drought summer in the countryside: the family well has failed, and the seven-year-old narrator watches a dowser — a man said to find underground water with a forked twig — walk the top field.

passages.title: The Wrong Notes
passages.attribution: original passage in the style of E. B. White
passages.text:
I have played the piano, after a fashion, for fifty years, and in all that time I have never once played it well. This does not appear to discourage me. Most of the amateurs I know eventually arrive at something like competence; I have arrived instead at a comfortable arrangement with my own limitations, the way an old married couple arrives at silence.

The instrument that started the trouble was an upright in my grandmother's front room, black as a stove and nearly as heavy, with a cracked middle C that buzzed like a wasp against a window. I was seven. Nobody in the family played, and the piano stood there mostly as furniture, holding up a lace runner and a photograph of an uncle in uniform. When I discovered that pressing one key produced a sound, and pressing two keys produced either an agreement or an argument, I understood that I had found my life's minor occupation.

I took lessons, briefly, from a Mrs. Kessler, who rapped the lid when I slouched and who despaired of my fingering, which I had invented myself and defended like a small nation defending its borders. The lessons ended; the habits stayed. To this day I read the bass clef the way a traveler reads a foreign timetable — slowly, with suspicion, and often with disastrous results. I play the same dozen pieces I played at thirty, and I play them a little worse each year, and I look forward all day to playing them.

Lately my hands have begun to file complaints. There are mornings when the stiffness in my knuckles suggests, reasonably, that the whole enterprise ought to be wound up — the lid closed, the bench pushed in, an ad placed. A sensible man would agree.

I have drafted that ad several times in my head, and I notice that I always draft it in the evening, at the hour when the house is quiet and the piano sits waiting in the lamplight. Then I sit down, and strike the old chords, and the wrong notes rise up to meet me like family.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A) Formal instruction is essential for anyone who hopes to master a musical instrument.
B) The author regrets devoting fifty years to an instrument he never learned to play well.
C) A lifelong pastime can remain sustaining even when its practitioner never becomes skilled.
D) Childhood discoveries inevitably determine the course of a person's adult life.
E) Aging forces people to abandon the hobbies of their youth.
questions.explanation: The essay's arc — fifty unskilled years, daily anticipation, the abandoned plan to quit — shows the pastime sustains him despite mediocrity. B is the opposite trap: he catalogs his failings with humor, but "I look forward all day to playing them" rules out regret.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, before the author began playing, the piano in his "grandmother's front room"
choices: A) was played each evening by the author's uncle
B) had recently been repaired
C) was reserved for lessons with Mrs. Kessler
D) had been purchased especially for the author
E) served the household mainly as a piece of furniture
questions.explanation: The piano "stood there mostly as furniture, holding up a lace runner and a photograph." A is a distortion: the uncle appears only in the photograph, and "nobody in the family played." The cracked, buzzing middle C rules out B.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, "despaired of" most nearly means
choices: A) lost hope regarding
B) strongly disapproved of
C) was frightened by
D) grew weary of
E) refused to acknowledge
questions.explanation: Mrs. Kessler "despaired of" fingering the author "defended" against all correction — she gave up hope of fixing it. B is the tempting half-right trap: she certainly disapproved, but "despair" specifically means losing hope, not merely objecting.

questions.prompt: It can most reasonably be inferred from the author's remark that he "always draft[s] it in the evening" that
choices: A) the author is too busy during the day to consider selling the piano
B) the author has already placed the advertisement several times
C) the author's family has urged him to give up playing
D) the author's resolve to quit dissolves at the very hour the piano most tempts him
E) the author no longer enjoys playing during daylight hours
questions.explanation: Evening is "the hour when the house is quiet and the piano sits waiting in the lamplight" — and instead of finishing the ad, he sits down and plays. B contradicts the text: the ad is drafted only "in my head," never actually placed.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward his own shortcomings as a pianist is best described as
choices: A) bitter regret
B) wry affection
C) studied indifference
D) mounting frustration
E) solemn reverence
questions.explanation: He teases his failings ("a little worse each year") yet cherishes them — the wrong notes "rise up to meet me like family." A is too extreme: the steady humor and daily anticipation exclude bitterness, while C fails because he plainly cares deeply.

questions.prompt: The author mentions all of the following as features of his musical life EXCEPT
choices: A) fingering that he invented for himself
B) difficulty reading the bass clef
C) a brief period of childhood lessons
D) a repertoire that has scarcely changed in decades
E) gradual improvement earned through steady practice
questions.explanation: Improvement is contradicted, not mentioned: he plays "a little worse each year." A, B, C, and D all appear directly — the invented fingering, the foreign-timetable bass clef, lessons with Mrs. Kessler, and "the same dozen pieces I played at thirty."

passages.context: An aging amateur looks back over fifty years of playing the piano badly, and considers why he has never once wanted to stop.

passages.title: A Call at Fenwick House
passages.text:
The carriage had gone before Mr. Alden reached the gate, and so he stood a moment in the road, hat in hand, regarding the house as one regards a face grown older in one's absence. The ivy had climbed past the second window. Someone had painted the door a decent green.

Miss Prewitt received him in the parlour, where a single lamp burned though the afternoon was not yet spent. She did not rise at once. The room smelled of beeswax and of old paper, precisely as it had a dozen years before.

"You have kept the house well," he said.

"It has kept itself. I merely reside in it." She folded her hands. "You will take tea. You always did, at this hour, when you lived among us."

He remarked that she had remembered. She replied that remembering cost nothing, and was, besides, a habit she had never troubled to break — as though memory were a servant kept on past all usefulness, out of a reluctance to seem hard.

He asked, for want of surer ground, whether the orchard still bore.

"Two trees came down in the March gales," she said. "I did not replace them. One does not, at my age, plant what one will not live to see bear fruit." She reached for the pot. "You take no sugar, I think."

"You need not have troubled over that."

"It is no trouble to pour a cup as a man likes it. The trouble," she said, setting the pot down with great care, "is in the wanting to."

For a while neither spoke. The lamp hissed faintly. Outside a thrush ran through its evening argument, though evening was hours off, and Mr. Alden understood — as one understands a thing long known and never said — that he had been expected, and expected long, and that nothing in that quiet room would ever confess it plainly.

questions.prompt: The passage is chiefly concerned with conveying that
choices: A) Miss Prewitt resents Mr. Alden for his long absence
B) Mr. Alden has returned to reclaim a house he once owned
C) the passing years have made the two of them strangers
D) beneath her composure, Miss Prewitt has awaited Mr. Alden far more deeply than she will admit
E) Miss Prewitt intends to sell the property and leave the district
questions.explanation: The daytime lamp, the remembered tea, and 'the trouble is in the wanting to' reveal longing beneath her calm (D). (A) mistakes her guardedness for resentment; nothing in the scene shows anger at his absence.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what change does Mr. Alden notice about the house as he arrives?
choices: A) The door had been painted green and ivy had climbed past the second window
B) The orchard had been cleared away entirely
C) A new gate had been installed at the road
D) The parlour had been enlarged
E) The lamp had been replaced with a brighter one
questions.explanation: The narrator notes the 'decent green' door and ivy 'past the second window' (A). The orchard merely lost two trees, so it was not 'cleared' (B); no gate, parlour, or lamp change is mentioned.

questions.prompt: The detail that 'a single lamp burned though the afternoon was not yet spent' most nearly suggests that
choices: A) the parlour windows had been shuttered against the light
B) Miss Prewitt is careless with household expenses
C) Miss Prewitt had been anticipating a visitor before he arrived
D) the house had fallen into disrepair
E) evening had come earlier than usual that day
questions.explanation: A lamp lit while the afternoon is still bright implies she lit it in readiness for a caller (C), a reading the ending confirms — 'he had been expected, and expected long.' (B) misreads a single lamp as waste; her exactness with the tea shows anything but carelessness.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'spent' (in 'the afternoon was not yet spent') most nearly means
choices: A) exhausted of money
B) elapsed or used up
C) weakened by illness
D) paid out in full
E) forgiven
questions.explanation: An afternoon 'not yet spent' has not yet elapsed or been used up (B). (A) and (D) seize the money sense of 'spent'; (C) invokes physical exhaustion — neither fits the passing of time.

questions.prompt: The tone of Miss Prewitt's remark, 'It is no trouble to pour a cup as a man likes it. The trouble... is in the wanting to,' is best described as
choices: A) bitter and accusing
B) cheerfully welcoming
C) coldly indifferent
D) anxious and pleading
E) guarded yet quietly confessional
questions.explanation: She hides feeling behind manners yet nearly confesses that the difficulty lies in her own 'wanting' (E). (A) reads bitterness and (C) indifference — both miss the tenderness she barely conceals.

questions.prompt: The author hints at Miss Prewitt's unspoken regard for Mr. Alden through all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: A) the single lamp burning in daylight
B) her precise memory of how he takes his tea
C) her refusal to replace the two orchard trees lost in the March gales
D) her admission that 'the trouble... is in the wanting to'
E) her keeping of memory 'as a servant kept on past all usefulness'
questions.explanation: The lamp, the remembered tea, the 'wanting' remark, and memory kept 'past all usefulness' all betray her regard. Refusing to replant speaks to her age and mortality, not her feelings for Alden (C).

passages.attribution: original passage
passages.context: After years away, Mr. Alden pays a long-postponed call on Miss Prewitt. Neither says what the visit is really about — but the room, the lamp, and the tea say a great deal.

passages.title: What the Fungi Carry
passages.text:
Beneath the floor of a healthy forest runs a web of fungal threads so fine that a single teaspoon of soil may hold miles of them. These threads, called mycorrhizae, sheathe the roots of trees and trade with them: the fungus draws sugar from the tree, and in return delivers water and the scarce minerals the roots alone could not reach. Where the threads of one fungus touch the roots of several trees, they may knit those trees into a shared network. Radioactive tracers have confirmed the rest: carbon drawn from the air by one tree can turn up, days later, in the tissues of a neighbor.

It is a genuinely startling finding, and it has been dressed in language that outruns the evidence. Popular writers speak of "mother trees" that "nurse" their seedlings, and of a forest that "talks." Such words imply purpose — that the older tree intends the gift, and that the fungus means to carry it.

But a fungus is not a postal clerk. It moves carbon along gradients of concentration and along its own appetite, feeding whichever partner best repays it. When sugar flows from a large tree to a small one, the simplest explanation is not generosity but physics: material drifts from where it is abundant toward where it is scarce, and the fungus takes its cut whichever tree is served.

None of this makes the network less remarkable. That soil, root, and fungus together move resources across a whole forest is astonishing enough without borrowing the vocabulary of family and speech. The danger of that vocabulary is not merely that it is imprecise. It is that it invites us to stop asking how the system works, satisfied that we have already named its heart.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: A) proving that older trees deliberately care for their seedlings
B) describing how fungal threads physically connect the roots of trees
C) arguing that popular descriptions of forest networks wrongly attribute purpose to them
D) showing that fungi matter more to a forest than the trees do
E) warning that radioactive tracers have distorted forest research
questions.explanation: The essay grants the network is real, then argues that 'mother tree' language wrongly implies intention (C). (B) is only the setup; (A) states the very view the author sets out to reject.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the mycorrhizal fungus supplies the tree with
choices: A) water and the scarce minerals its roots cannot reach
B) sugar produced by photosynthesis
C) carbon drawn from the air
D) protection from competing fungi
E) new root tissue
questions.explanation: The fungus 'delivers water and the scarce minerals the roots alone could not reach' (A). The tree, not the fungus, provides sugar and draws carbon from the air, so (B) and (C) reverse the exchange.

questions.prompt: The statement 'a fungus is not a postal clerk' chiefly emphasizes that the fungus
choices: A) works more slowly than a delivery service
B) cannot move carbon over long distances
C) does not act with intention or on any tree's behalf
D) actively harms the trees it connects
E) is too simple an organism to be worth studying
questions.explanation: Denying the fungus is a 'postal clerk' rejects the idea that it delivers with intention or on another's behalf (C). (A) takes the delivery image too literally; the passage affirms carbon does travel far.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'dressed' (in 'it has been dressed in language that outruns the evidence') most nearly means
choices: A) bandaged
B) scolded
C) arranged in rows
D) clothed or presented
E) prepared for cooking
questions.explanation: To be 'dressed in language' is to be clothed or presented in words (D). (A) the wound sense and (E) the culinary sense are literal traps unrelated to describing a scientific finding.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the phrases 'mother trees' and a forest that 'talks' is best described as
choices: A) enthusiastic agreement
B) total indifference
C) confused uncertainty
D) bitter contempt
E) measured skepticism
questions.explanation: The author calls the phrases evidence-outrunning yet still finds the network 'remarkable,' so measured skepticism fits (E). (D) 'bitter contempt' overstates a controlled, still-respectful critique.

questions.prompt: The final sentence ('It is that it invites us to stop asking how the system works...') primarily functions to
choices: A) concede that the popular writers were right after all
B) propose a new experiment using radioactive tracers
C) explain why the loose language is genuinely harmful, not merely inaccurate
D) summarize the chemistry of nutrient exchange
E) soften the author's earlier criticism
questions.explanation: The close argues the loose language is harmful because it makes us quit investigating, going beyond mere imprecision (C). (A) and (E) wrongly reverse or soften the author's stance.

passages.attribution: original passage
passages.context: A science writer explains the hidden network of fungal threads that runs beneath a forest floor — and pushes back on the cozy story often told about it.

passages.title: The Hour the Railroads Made
passages.text:
Before the 1880s, noon was a local fact. When the sun stood highest over a town, that town's clocks read twelve, and a town a hundred miles east kept its own noon, some minutes apart. For most of human history this caused no trouble; a farmer had no reason to compare his midday with a stranger's. The railroad changed that, and in changing it altered something we seldom think to question: the meaning of the word "now."

A train cannot obey a hundred local noons. A single line running through dozens of towns needed one timetable, and a timetable needs one clock. And so, in 1883, the American railroads simply agreed among themselves to carve the continent into time zones and to run their trains by them. No law compelled it; no vote approved it. Towns reset their clocks to match the rails, because the rails would not adjust to them.

We are tempted to file this under convenience, alongside the standardized screw and the postage stamp. That is the error. To set every clock in a zone to one hour is to declare that the sun no longer tells the time — that noon is a decision, not an observation. A man in the western corner of his zone now ate his lunch while the sun still climbed, and thought nothing of it. Within a generation, the older, sky-read time seemed quaint, and nearly wrong.

What the railroads accomplished was not a tidier schedule but a quiet transfer of authority. Time had belonged to the heavens, and to each place its own; afterward it belonged to a committee and a map. We should take notice when the ordinary machinery of commerce reaches up and rewrites something as intimate as the hour we call our own.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main argument of the passage?
choices: A) The adoption of standard time was a profound transfer of authority, not a mere convenience
B) Railroads simply began running their trains more efficiently after 1883
C) Local solar noon kept more accurate time than the new zones did
D) Time zones should have required a national vote before being adopted
E) The postage stamp mattered as much to daily life as the clock did
questions.explanation: The essay insists standard time was a 'quiet transfer of authority,' not convenience (A). (B) is background; (C) and (E) invert or misuse ideas the author raises only to reject.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the American railroads established time zones
choices: A) in the year 1800
B) in 1883, by agreement among themselves
C) only after a national vote
D) by an act of Congress
E) in the early twentieth century
questions.explanation: The railroads acted 'in 1883... among themselves' (B). The passage stresses that 'no law compelled it; no vote approved it,' making (C) and (D) direct traps.

questions.prompt: The statement 'the rails would not adjust to them' most strongly implies that
choices: A) the railroads lacked the technology to change their schedules
B) the towns held more power than the railroad companies
C) the railroads' economic power forced towns to conform without consent
D) most towns refused to change their clocks
E) train schedules were frequently inaccurate
questions.explanation: That the rails would not yield shows the railroads' power compelled towns to conform without asking (C). (D) contradicts the same sentence, which says towns did reset their clocks.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'file' (in 'We are tempted to file this under convenience') most nearly means
choices: A) to sharpen with a tool
B) to submit a legal document
C) to classify or categorize
D) to march in a line
E) to smooth away
questions.explanation: To 'file this under convenience' is to classify or categorize it (C). (A) to sharpen and (E) to smooth exploit other senses of 'file'; (B) the legal sense does not fit.

questions.prompt: The author most likely mentions 'the standardized screw and the postage stamp' in order to
choices: A) praise the inventiveness of the nineteenth century
B) list other technologies the railroads themselves invented
C) prove that standardization always improves daily life
D) name the 'convenience' category he insists is the wrong bin for standard time
E) argue that those objects mattered more than time zones did
questions.explanation: The screw and stamp name the 'convenience' category the author calls 'the error' for standard time (D). (A) and (E) miss that he cites them only to reject the comparison.

questions.prompt: The author supports his claim that standard time was a 'transfer of authority' through all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: A) noting that no law or vote created the time zones
B) observing that a man might eat lunch while the sun still climbed
C) stating that noon became 'a decision, not an observation'
D) recalling that a farmer once had no reason to compare his midday with a stranger's
E) contrasting time that 'belonged to the heavens' with time that 'belonged to a committee and a map'
questions.explanation: The authority claim rests on the unlegislated adoption, the misaligned lunch, 'a decision, not an observation,' and heavens-versus-committee. The farmer detail merely sketches the old world before railroads (D).

passages.attribution: original passage
passages.context: A historian argues that something as basic as clock time — everyone's noon agreeing — is not natural at all, but an invention forced into being by the railroads in the 1880s.

passages.title: The Button Tin
passages.text:
My grandmother's needle was never bare;
it waited, threaded, in her chair,
as though the house might split at any hour
and need the mending then and there.

She saved a tin of buttons, none agreed —
bone, brass, a blue like winter sky —
that fit no coat now left on earth;
she could not say the reason why.

The torn things came to her: a cuff,
a sleeve worn thin as breath, the seam
that gave along my father's back
the year he ceased to come, it seemed.

She'd draw the raw edges close, and speak
of patience — how a stitch is tension, kept —
then bite the thread and set it down.
I thought her hands could close whatever wept.

I did not see, until the buttons
outlived the coats, and her, that some
things keep because they cannot mend:
we save the pieces, and call the saving love.

questions.prompt: The poem is chiefly about
choices: A) the difficulty of learning to sew by hand
B) a grandmother's habit of hoarding worthless objects
C) how the speaker came to understand that some losses can be kept but never repaired
D) the coldness that grew between the speaker and the father
E) the virtue of thrift during hard times
questions.explanation: The buttons that 'outlived the coats' and the closing line teach that some losses can be kept but not mended (C). (A) and (E) stay on the literal surface of sewing and thrift.

questions.prompt: The seam 'that gave along my father's back' is associated in the poem with
choices: A) the year the father 'ceased to come'
B) a sudden winter storm
C) the grandmother's death
D) the sorting of the buttons after she died
E) a coat the speaker once wore
questions.explanation: The split seam is tied to 'the year he ceased to come' (A). No storm, death, or button-sorting is linked to that seam, and the coat is the father's, not the speaker's.

questions.prompt: The detail that the buttons 'fit no coat now left on earth' most nearly suggests that
choices: A) the grandmother preferred buttons to clothing
B) the buttons were too precious for ordinary coats
C) the speaker had carelessly lost the coats
D) the garments the buttons once belonged to are gone, yet she keeps them anyway
E) the grandmother meant to buy everyone new coats
questions.explanation: Buttons fitting no surviving coat means their garments are gone while she saves the buttons still (D). (B) and (C) invent a value or a blame the poem never states.

questions.prompt: As used in the poem, 'gave' (in 'the seam that gave along my father's back') most nearly means
choices: A) donated
B) yielded a profit
C) gave way and came apart
D) surrendered in battle
E) produced or generated
questions.explanation: A seam that 'gave' gave way and split open (C). (A) donated, (E) produced, and (D) the battlefield sense all misread the plain meaning of failing cloth.

questions.prompt: The tone of the poem's final lines ('things keep because they cannot mend: / we save the pieces, and call the saving love') is best described as
choices: A) cheerful and triumphant
B) angry and accusing
C) detached and clinical
D) fearful and anxious
E) tender and mournful
questions.explanation: The close grieves gently while affirming love in the keeping — tender and mournful (E). (A) triumphant and (C) clinical miss the grief; (B) reads an anger the poem does not carry.

questions.prompt: The lines 'I did not see, until the buttons / outlived the coats, and her' primarily serve to
choices: A) introduce a new character into the poem
B) mark the turn from the speaker's childhood faith in the grandmother's hands to a grown understanding of loss
C) explain how the grandmother taught the speaker to sew
D) describe the layout of the grandmother's house
E) circle back to the opening image of the threaded needle
questions.explanation: 'I did not see, until…' pivots from the child's belief in her mending hands to an adult grasp of loss — the poem's turn (B). (E) is tempting because the needle opens the poem, but these lines look to the buttons and her death, not back to that image; (A), (C), and (D) describe things the poem never presents.

passages.attribution: original passage
passages.context: The speaker remembers a grandmother who mended everything, and the tin of mismatched buttons she left behind.

passages.title: Buck Fights the Collar
passages.attribution: from The Call of the Wild by Jack London (1903)
passages.context: Buck, a stolen ranch dog, has been crated and shipped north. Here he is hauled from the crate by rough handlers.
passages.text: Dazed, suffering intolerable pain from throat and tongue, with the life half throttled out of him, Buck attempted to face his tormentors. But he was thrown down and choked repeatedly, till they succeeded in filing the heavy brass collar from off his neck. Then the rope was removed, and he was flung into a cagelike crate. There he lay for the remainder of the weary night, nursing his wrath and wounded pride. He could not understand what it all meant. What did they want with him, these strange men? Why were they keeping him pent up in this narrow crate? He did not know why, but he felt oppressed by the vague sense of impending calamity. Several times during the night he sprang to his feet when the shed door rattled open, expecting to see the Judge, or the boys at least. But each time it was the bulging face of the saloon-keeper that peered in at him by the sickly light of a tallow candle. And each time the joyful bark that trembled in Buck's throat was twisted into a savage growl. But the saloon-keeper let him alone, and in the morning four men entered and picked up the crate. More tormentors, Buck decided, for they were evil-looking creatures, ragged and unkempt; and he stormed and raged at them through the bars. They only laughed and poked sticks at him, which he promptly assailed with his teeth till he realized that that was what they wanted. Whereupon he lay down sullenly and allowed the crate to be lifted into a wagon. Then he, and the crate in which he was imprisoned, began a passage through many hands. Clerks in the express office took charge of him; he was carted about in another wagon; a truck carried him, with an assortment of boxes and parcels, upon a ferry steamer; he was trucked off the steamer into a great railway depot, and finally he was deposited in an express car. For two days and nights this express car was dragged along at the tail of shrieking locomotives; and for two days and nights Buck neither ate nor drank.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Buck gradually learns to trust the men who take charge of him during his travels.
Buck is forcibly confined and shipped far from home into a frightening, unknown ordeal.
Buck devises a careful plan to escape from the crate that imprisons him.
The kindly saloon-keeper rescues Buck from a band of cruel tormentors.
Buck settles happily into a comfortable new life aboard the express car.
questions.explanation: The passage traces Buck's caging, his dread, and his long transport, so (B) fits. (A) reverses the truth—he growls and rages rather than trusting; the men are captors, not caretakers.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how long did Buck go without eating or drinking?
choices: A single weary night
Two days and two nights
Three full days
An entire week
Only half a day
questions.explanation: The passage states that for two days and nights Buck 'neither ate nor drank,' making (B) correct. (A) is the trap—he spends one night in the crate, but the fasting spans the two-day express-car ride.

questions.prompt: Which of the following can best be inferred about Buck's life before this episode?
choices: He had long been abused and expected nothing but cruelty from people.
He had been a well-treated dog accustomed to kind, familiar masters.
He had never before encountered any human beings.
He clearly understood why the men had seized and caged him.
He had belonged to the saloon-keeper since he was a puppy.
questions.explanation: The 'joyful bark' Buck feels when expecting the Judge or the boys implies past affection and trust, so (B). (A) is contradicted by that joyful expectation, and (D) is contradicted by 'He could not understand what it all meant.'

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'assailed' most nearly means
choices: avoided
attacked
sniffed
swallowed
ignored
questions.explanation: Buck 'assailed with his teeth' the sticks poked at him—he lunged and bit, so 'attacked' (B) fits. (E) 'ignored' is the opposite; the sentence shows aggressive response, which is exactly what the men 'wanted.'

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Buck is best described as
choices: sympathetic and compassionate
cold and mocking
bored and indifferent
alarmed and panicked
playfully amused
questions.explanation: Phrases like 'nursing his wrath and wounded pride' and the trembling 'joyful bark' render Buck's suffering with feeling, so (A). (E) 'amused' misreads the laughter—that belongs to the tormentors, not the narrator, whose voice stays earnest and pitying.

questions.prompt: The passage indicates that Buck's journey involved all of the following EXCEPT
choices: a ride on a ferry steamer
a period inside an express car
being carted about in a wagon
a flight aboard an airplane
being handled by clerks in the express office
questions.explanation: The passage names the ferry steamer, express car, wagon, and express-office clerks, but never an airplane, so (D) is the exception. Each other choice appears explicitly in the list of conveyances through which Buck passed.

passages.title: The Light Through the Glass
passages.attribution: from The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens (1841)
passages.context: Late at night a stranger, who has walked a lost child home, gets his first look at the old man and the shop of oddments where the child lives.
passages.text: When she had knocked twice or thrice there was a noise as if some person were moving inside, and at length a faint light appeared through the glass which, as it approached very slowly, the bearer having to make his way through a great many scattered articles, enabled me to see both what kind of person it was who advanced and what kind of place it was through which he came. It was an old man with long grey hair, whose face and figure as he held the light above his head and looked before him as he approached, I could plainly see. Though much altered by age, I fancied I could recognize in his spare and slender form something of that delicate mould which I had noticed in a child. Their bright blue eyes were certainly alike, but his face was so deeply furrowed and so very full of care, that here all resemblance ceased. The place through which he made his way at leisure was one of those receptacles for old and curious things which seem to crouch in odd corners of this town and to hide their musty treasures from the public eye in jealousy and distrust. There were suits of mail standing like ghosts in armour here and there, fantastic carvings brought from monkish cloisters, rusty weapons of various kinds, distorted figures in china and wood and iron and ivory: tapestry and strange furniture that might have been designed in dreams. The haggard aspect of the little old man was wonderfully suited to the place; he might have groped among old churches and tombs and deserted houses and gathered all the spoils with his own hands. There was nothing in the whole collection but was in keeping with himself nothing that looked older or more worn than he. As he turned the key in the lock, he surveyed me with some astonishment which was not diminished when he looked from me to my companion. The door being opened, the child addressed him as grandfather, and told him the little story of our companionship.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: a narrator's first sight of an aged shopkeeper and the strange, antique-crammed place he inhabits
a grandfather's lifelong quest to gather relics from churches, tombs, and deserted houses
a child who leads a stranger to rob an old man of his musty treasures
an old man recounting to visitors the history of each curious object he owns
a narrator's joyful reunion with a friend he had known since childhood
questions.explanation: The passage describes the narrator watching the old man approach with a candle and surveying his shop of curiosities. B twists the speculative "might have groped among old churches"; E distorts the noticed resemblance to "a child," which is not a prior friendship.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, all resemblance between the old man and the child ceased because of the old man's
choices: grey hair
deeply furrowed, careworn face
bright blue eyes
spare and slender form
hurried, nervous manner
questions.explanation: The text states his face was "so deeply furrowed and so very full of care, that here all resemblance ceased." The blue eyes (C) and slender form (D) are precisely where he DID resemble the child, making them traps.

questions.prompt: The old man's astonishment on seeing the narrator most strongly suggests that he
choices: had been robbed by strangers in the past
seldom received unexpected visitors
harbored a deep dislike of children
had been waiting eagerly for company
mistook the narrator for a lost relative
questions.explanation: His astonishment, "not diminished" when he saw the companion, implies a reclusive figure unused to callers. D contradicts his surprise; E reverses the text, in which the narrator, not the old man, notices a resemblance.

questions.prompt: In context, the word "spare" (in "his spare and slender form") most nearly means
choices: additional
lean
merciful
unoccupied
held in reserve
questions.explanation: Paired with "slender" to describe the old man's body, "spare" means lean or thin. "Additional" (A) and "held in reserve" (E) are common meanings of "spare" that do not fit a description of physical form.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward the shop and its contents is best described as one of
choices: eerie fascination
cheerful nostalgia
cold indifference
scornful dismissal
paralyzing terror
questions.explanation: Phrases like "musty treasures," armor "like ghosts," and furniture "designed in dreams" convey absorbed interest tinged with the uncanny. "Terror" (E) overstates the mood; "indifference" (C) contradicts the rich, engaged detail.

questions.prompt: The passage mentions all of the following among the shop's contents EXCEPT
choices: suits of mail
rusty weapons
fantastic carvings
leather-bound books
tapestry
questions.explanation: The collection lists suits of mail, carvings from cloisters, rusty weapons, distorted figures, tapestry, and strange furniture. Leather-bound books are never named, making D the exception; the others appear explicitly in the inventory.

passages.title: At the Inn Door
passages.attribution: from Dracula by Bram Stoker (1897)
passages.context: Jonathan Harker, travelling to advise Count Dracula, reaches the inn where he'll spend the night before the last leg to the castle.
passages.text: When I came close she bowed and said, “The Herr Englishman?” “Yes,” I said, “Jonathan Harker.” She smiled, and gave some message to an elderly man in white shirt-sleeves, who had followed her to the door. He went, but immediately returned with a letter:— “My Friend.—Welcome to the Carpathians. I am anxiously expecting you. Sleep well to-night. At three to-morrow the diligence will start for Bukovina; a place on it is kept for you. At the Borgo Pass my carriage will await you and will bring you to me. I trust that your journey from London has been a happy one, and that you will enjoy your stay in my beautiful land. “Your friend, “Dracula.” 4 May.—I found that my landlord had got a letter from the Count, directing him to secure the best place on the coach for me; but on making inquiries as to details he seemed somewhat reticent, and pretended that he could not understand my German. This could not be true, because up to then he had understood it perfectly; at least, he answered my questions exactly as if he did. He and his wife, the old lady who had received me, looked at each other in a frightened sort of way. He mumbled out that the money had been sent in a letter, and that was all he knew. When I asked him if he knew Count Dracula, and could tell me anything of his castle, both he and his wife crossed themselves, and, saying that they knew nothing at all, simply refused to speak further. It was so near the time of starting that I had no time to ask any one else, for it was all very mysterious and not by any means comforting. Just before I was leaving, the old lady came up to my room and said in a very hysterical way: “Must you go? Oh! young Herr, must you go?” She was in such an excited state that she seemed to have lost her grip of what German she knew, and mixed it all up with some other language which I did not know at all.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A traveler arrives at an inn and meets the locals' uneasy dread at the mention of his host
A dishonest landlord cheats a traveler out of the money for his coach fare
Two old friends, the Count and Jonathan, are joyfully reunited after a long journey
A kind woman patiently teaches Jonathan an unfamiliar foreign language
A detailed description celebrates the natural beauty of the Carpathian mountains
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Harker's arrival and the hosts' fearful reactions to Dracula. B misreads the mention of money; the landlord is honest but frightened. C mistakes the letter's polite tone; the two never actually meet here.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when Jonathan asks the landlord for details about his journey, the landlord
choices: carefully explains the entire route to Bukovina
claims that he cannot understand Jonathan's German
offers to accompany Jonathan to the castle himself
hands Jonathan a second letter written by the Count
angrily refuses to accept any payment for the coach
questions.explanation: The text says he grew reticent and "pretended that he could not understand my German," though he had understood it perfectly before. A contradicts his evasiveness; D confuses the single letter he'd already received from the Count.

questions.prompt: The behavior of the landlord and his wife—crossing themselves and refusing to speak—most strongly suggests that they
choices: strongly dislike travelers who come from England
are devout people who bless every guest who arrives
regard the Count with fear or superstitious dread
are offended that Jonathan speaks German poorly
hope to persuade Jonathan to stay on as a lodger
questions.explanation: Their frightened looks, silence, and crossing themselves imply superstitious fear of Dracula. B takes the religious gesture literally, ignoring the "frightened" context; the couple in fact wish Jonathan would NOT go.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage ("the diligence will start for Bukovina"), the word "diligence" most nearly means
choices: steady and careful effort
a public stagecoach or carriage
a persistent, hardworking attitude
a formal welcoming ceremony
a printed travel timetable
questions.explanation: Context shows the diligence is a vehicle: "a place on it is kept for you," later called "the coach." A and C give the familiar dictionary sense of diligence, the classic trap, which makes no sense with "start for Bukovina."

questions.prompt: By the end of the passage, the narrator's attitude is best described as
choices: cheerfully optimistic about the trip ahead
coldly indifferent to his surroundings
increasingly uneasy and apprehensive
completely paralyzed by open terror
fondly nostalgic for his home in London
questions.explanation: He calls things "very mysterious and not by any means comforting," and notes the hysterical old lady—growing unease. D overstates it; he is disquieted, not terrified. A mistakes the letter's cheer for Jonathan's own outlook.

questions.prompt: The passage states all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: Dracula's letter welcomes Jonathan to the Carpathians
A carriage is to await Jonathan at the Borgo Pass
The old lady spoke calmly and clearly in German
The landlord had received a letter from the Count
The landlord and his wife crossed themselves
questions.explanation: The opposite is true: the old lady was "hysterical" and "seemed to have lost her grip of what German she knew," mixing in another language. A, B, D, and E are all explicitly stated in the text.

passages.title: The Blistered Door
passages.attribution: from The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson (1886)
passages.context: Mr. Utterson and his kinsman, on one of their walks, pass a grim, neglected door set into an otherwise respectable street.
passages.text: The door, which was equipped with neither bell nor knocker, was blistered and distained. Tramps slouched into the recess and struck matches on the panels; children kept shop upon the steps; the schoolboy had tried his knife on the mouldings; and for close on a generation, no one had appeared to drive away these random visitors or to repair their ravages. Mr. Enfield and the lawyer were on the other side of the by-street; but when they came abreast of the entry, the former lifted up his cane and pointed. "Did you ever remark that door?" he asked; and when his companion had replied in the affirmative, "It is connected in my mind," added he, "with a very odd story." "Indeed?" said Mr. Utterson, with a slight change of voice, "and what was that?" "Well, it was this way," returned Mr. Enfield: "I was coming home from some place at the end of the world, about three o'clock of a black winter morning, and my way lay through a part of town where there was literally nothing to be seen but lamps. Street after street, and all the folks asleep—street after street, all lighted up as if for a procession and all as empty as a church—till at last I got into that state of mind when a man listens and listens and begins to long for the sight of a policeman. All at once, I saw two figures: one a little man who was stumping along eastward at a good walk, and the other a girl of maybe eight or ten who was running as hard as she was able down a cross street. Well, sir, the two ran into one another naturally enough at the corner; and then came the horrible part of the thing; for the man trampled calmly over the child's body and left her screaming on the ground. It sounds nothing to hear, but it was hellish to see. It wasn't like a man; it was like some damned Juggernaut.

questions.prompt: The passage as a whole primarily serves to
choices: describe the deepening friendship between Enfield and Utterson
introduce a neglected door and begin an unsettling story of a man's cruelty
explain why the streets of the town were deserted at night
criticize the town for failing to maintain its public buildings
recount a legal case that the lawyer Utterson is investigating
questions.explanation: The passage sets up the ominous door, then Enfield launches his 'very odd story' of the trampling. B captures both. A is real but incidental; the streets (C) and door's disrepair (D) are supporting details, not the point; no legal case (E) is mentioned.

questions.prompt: According to Enfield, his encounter with the two figures took place
choices: at noon on a bright summer day
in the early hours of a dark winter morning
just after dawn in the spring
late on an autumn afternoon
during a public procession
questions.explanation: Enfield says he was coming home 'about three o'clock of a black winter morning.' B matches exactly. E misreads the simile that the empty streets looked 'as if for a procession'; the others contradict the stated season and time.

questions.prompt: The description of how the little man 'trampled calmly over the child's body and left her screaming' most strongly suggests that he
choices: was rushing to keep an urgent appointment
felt deep remorse for what he had done
was completely indifferent to the harm he caused
had failed to notice the child in the darkness
meant to stop and help the child to her feet
questions.explanation: 'Calmly' and leaving her screaming show cold indifference, reinforced by 'It wasn't like a man.' C fits. 'Calmly' rules out not seeing her (D) or haste (A); remorse (B) and helping (E) directly contradict his conduct.

questions.prompt: In the question "Did you ever remark that door?" the word "remark" most nearly means
choices: comment aloud upon
take notice of
find fault with
make a sketch of
offer to purchase
questions.explanation: Enfield asks whether Utterson had ever observed the door; Utterson answers 'in the affirmative.' 'Take notice of' fits. A is the familiar modern sense of 'remark' (to say something), the intended trap, but the context is about noticing, not speaking.

questions.prompt: Enfield's attitude as he recalls the incident at the corner is best described as one of
choices: amused detachment
cool indifference
horrified revulsion
nostalgic fondness
detached scientific curiosity
questions.explanation: Enfield calls it 'the horrible part,' says it was 'hellish to see,' and likens the man to 'some damned Juggernaut.' C fits. B describes the trampler, not Enfield; the loaded language rules out amusement (A), fondness (D), or clinical curiosity (E).

questions.prompt: The passage states all of the following about the door and its treatment EXCEPT that
choices: it had neither a bell nor a knocker
tramps struck matches on its panels
children had made use of its steps
a schoolboy had cut into its mouldings with a knife
its owner regularly repaired and repainted it
questions.explanation: The text says the door was 'blistered and distained' and that 'for close on a generation, no one had appeared... to repair.' E is the opposite of the passage and is the EXCEPT answer. A through D are all stated directly.

passages.title: The Four Sisters
passages.attribution: from Little Women by Louisa May Alcott (1868)
passages.context: An introduction to the four March sisters at home — here, quiet Beth and vain little Amy.
passages.text: Elizabeth, or Beth, as everyone called her, was a rosy, smooth-haired, bright-eyed girl of thirteen, with a shy manner, a timid voice, and a peaceful expression which was seldom disturbed. Her father called her 'Little Miss Tranquility', and the name suited her excellently, for she seemed to live in a happy world of her own, only venturing out to meet the few whom she trusted and loved. Amy, though the youngest, was a most important person, in her own opinion at least. A regular snow maiden, with blue eyes, and yellow hair curling on her shoulders, pale and slender, and always carrying herself like a young lady mindful of her manners. What the characters of the four sisters were we will leave to be found out. The clock struck six and, having swept up the hearth, Beth put a pair of slippers down to warm. Somehow the sight of the old shoes had a good effect upon the girls, for Mother was coming, and everyone brightened to welcome her. Meg stopped lecturing, and lighted the lamp, Amy got out of the easy chair without being asked, and Jo forgot how tired she was as she sat up to hold the slippers nearer to the blaze. "They are quite worn out. Marmee must have a new pair." "I thought I'd get her some with my dollar," said Beth. "No, I shall!" cried Amy. "I'm the oldest," began Meg, but Jo cut in with a decided, "I'm the man of the family now Papa is away, and I shall provide the slippers, for he told me to take special care of Mother while he was gone." "I'll tell you what we'll do," said Beth, "let's each get her something for Christmas, and not get anything for ourselves." "That's like you, dear! What will we get?" exclaimed Jo. Everyone thought soberly for a minute, then Meg announced, as if the idea was suggested by the sight of her own pretty hands, "I shall give her a nice pair of gloves."

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: It describes a quarrel that permanently divides the four sisters
It introduces the sisters and shows their shared wish to give to their mother rather than to themselves
It explains why the girls' father left home
It contrasts a wealthy family with a poor one in the town
It gives a detailed account of Beth's daily household chores
questions.explanation: The passage sketches the sisters, then centers on their unanimous decision to buy Mother gifts and forgo their own. B captures both halves. A misreads the friendly gift-debate as a lasting quarrel; C, D, and E seize on details never developed as the focus.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does Beth do immediately after the clock strikes six?
choices: She lights the lamp
She gets out of the easy chair without being asked
She sweeps the hearth and puts a pair of slippers down to warm
She goes out and buys her mother a new pair of slippers
She announces she will give her mother a pair of gloves
questions.explanation: The text says that as the clock struck six, Beth, "having swept up the hearth," put slippers down to warm. A is Meg's action, B is Amy's, and E is Meg's gift idea. D never happens; the girls only propose to buy things.

questions.prompt: What can most reasonably be inferred about the girls' father?
choices: He has recently died
He is away from home and has entrusted the care of their mother to the girls
He disapproves of the girls' plan to buy gifts
He is a wealthy man who indulges the girls
He is present in the house but too ill to appear
questions.explanation: Jo says she is "the man of the family now Papa is away" and that he told her to "take special care of Mother while he was gone." This shows an absent father who left them responsible. A overreads "away/gone" as death; E contradicts his absence; C and D lack support.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "soberly" (in "Everyone thought soberly for a minute") most nearly means
choices: in a drunken manner
sadly and mournfully
seriously and thoughtfully
hastily and carelessly
unwillingly
questions.explanation: The girls pause to consider what gifts to buy, so "soberly" means gravely, in earnest. C fits. A is the literal opposite meaning that the context excludes; B and E add unsupported gloom or reluctance; D contradicts the deliberate pause.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward the four sisters is best described as
choices: mocking and dismissive
detached and clinical
warm and affectionate
anxious and fearful
bitter and resentful
questions.explanation: Fond touches like "Little Miss Tranquility" and "That's like you, dear!" convey admiration and tenderness, making C right. A and E invert the warmth; B ignores the evident fondness; D imports a dread the cozy fireside scene never suggests.

questions.prompt: The passage states or suggests all of the following about Amy EXCEPT:
choices: She has blue eyes
She has a shy and timid manner
She is the youngest of the sisters
She carries herself like a young lady mindful of her manners
She regards herself as a most important person
questions.explanation: A shy manner and timid voice describe Beth, not Amy, so B is the exception. The passage explicitly gives Amy blue eyes (A), youngest rank (C), ladylike bearing (D), and self-importance "in her own opinion" (E).

passages.title: The Wagon in the Dark
passages.attribution: from My Ántonia by Willa Cather (1918)
passages.context: Young Jim Burden, newly orphaned, rides by night wagon across the dark Nebraska prairie toward his grandparents' farm.
passages.text: The immigrants rumbled off into the empty darkness, and we followed them. I tried to go to sleep, but the jolting made me bite my tongue, and I soon began to ache all over. When the straw settled down, I had a hard bed. Cautiously I slipped from under the buffalo hide, got up on my knees and peered over the side of the wagon. There seemed to be nothing to see; no fences, no creeks or trees, no hills or fields. If there was a road, I could not make it out in the faint starlight. There was nothing but land: not a country at all, but the material out of which countries are made. No, there was nothing but land—slightly undulating, I knew, because often our wheels ground against the brake as we went down into a hollow and lurched up again on the other side. I had the feeling that the world was left behind, that we had got over the edge of it, and were outside man's jurisdiction. I had never before looked up at the sky when there was not a familiar mountain ridge against it. But this was the complete dome of heaven, all there was of it. I did not believe that my dead father and mother were watching me from up there; they would still be looking for me at the sheep-fold down by the creek, or along the white road that led to the mountain pastures. I had left even their spirits behind me. The wagon jolted on, carrying me I knew not whither. I don't think I was homesick. If we never arrived anywhere, it did not matter. Between that earth and that sky I felt erased, blotted out. I did not say my prayers that night: here, I felt, what would be would be.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A boy struggles to fall asleep during a rough and uncomfortable wagon ride.
A boy crossing an empty prairie at night feels cut off from everything familiar yet oddly at peace with the unknown.
A boy grieves for his dead parents and yearns to return to his mountain home.
A boy is terrified of the strange men who are carrying him across the country.
A boy is captivated by the beauty of the star-filled prairie sky.
questions.explanation: The passage dwells on the featureless land and the narrator's sense of being "erased," ending in quiet acceptance. C overstates grief—he says he wasn't homesick and "left even their spirits behind." A and E each seize on a single detail rather than the whole.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what first made it hard for the narrator to fall asleep?
choices: The cold coming through the buffalo hide
The jolting of the wagon
His fear of the immigrants ahead of them
Thoughts of his dead father and mother
The brightness of the starlight
questions.explanation: He states "the jolting made me bite my tongue, and I soon began to ache all over." D is a trap: his parents are mentioned later as a reflection, not as the cause of his sleeplessness.

questions.prompt: The passage most strongly suggests that before this journey the narrator had lived —
choices: in a crowded, noisy city
on the wide open plains
in a mountainous region
aboard ships at sea
in a distant foreign country
questions.explanation: He had never before seen the sky "when there was not a familiar mountain ridge against it," and imagines his parents seeking him near "mountain pastures." B is the trap—the prairie is precisely what feels new and strange to him.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, "jurisdiction" ("outside man's jurisdiction") most nearly means —
choices: knowledge
authority
settlement
memory
judgment
questions.explanation: He feels beyond the reach of human control or authority, in a place "outside" man's domain. E, "judgment," exploits the shared Latin root (juris-), but jurisdiction denotes the sphere of authority, not the act of judging.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward his situation can best be described as —
choices: terrified and panicked
bitterly resentful
calmly resigned
joyfully excited
deeply homesick
questions.explanation: "If we never arrived anywhere, it did not matter" and "what would be would be" signal acceptance, not fear. Despite the strangeness he does not panic (A), and he explicitly denies being homesick (E).

questions.prompt: The narrator reports being unable to see all of the following in the darkness EXCEPT —
choices: fences
creeks
trees
hills
the undulating land
questions.explanation: He lists "no fences, no creeks or trees, no hills or fields," but insists "there was nothing but land—slightly undulating," which he does perceive. The four wrong choices name things the passage says were absent.

passages.title: Margaret at Home
passages.attribution: from North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell (1855)
passages.context: Margaret Hale recalls her childhood, divided between a country parsonage and her aunt's fashionable London house.
passages.text: At home—before she came to live in Harley Street—her mother's dressing-room had been her nursery; and, as they kept early hours in the country parsonage, Margaret had always had her meals with her father and mother. Oh! well did the tall stately girl of eighteen remember the tears shed with such wild passion of grief by the little girl of nine, as she hid her face under the bed-clothes, in that first night; and how she was bidden not to cry by the nurse, because it would disturb Miss Edith; and how she had cried as bitterly, but more quietly, till her newly-seen, grand, pretty aunt had come softly upstairs with Mr. Hale to show him his little sleeping daughter. Then the little Margaret had hushed her sobs, and tried to lie quiet as if asleep, for fear of making her father unhappy by her grief, which she dared not express before her aunt, and which she rather thought it was wrong to feel at all after the long hoping, and planning, and contriving they had gone through at home, before her wardrobe could be arranged so as to suit her grander circumstances, and before papa could leave his parish to come up to London, even for a few days. Now she had got to love the old nursery, though it was but a dismantled place; and she looked all round, with a kind of cat-like regret, at the idea of leaving it for ever in three days. 'Ah Newton!' said she, 'I think we shall all be sorry to leave this dear old room.' 'Indeed, miss, I shan't for one. My eyes are not so good as they were, and the light here is so bad that I can't see to mend laces except just at the window, where there's always a shocking draught—enough to give one one's death of cold.' Well, I dare say you will have both good light and plenty of warmth at Naples. You must keep as much of your darning as you can till then.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: the hardships Margaret's family endured while living in the country parsonage
Margaret's memory of first arriving at Harley Street and her present reluctance to leave the old nursery
the reasons Margaret's family has decided to relocate to Naples
Newton's growing dissatisfaction with her duties as a servant
the sharp contrast between quiet country life and busy city life
questions.explanation: The passage moves from young Margaret's tearful arrival to the grown Margaret's 'cat-like regret' at leaving the nursery. B captures both halves. C names Naples, mentioned only in the closing lines; it is a trap that mistakes a detail for the whole focus.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the nurse told the young Margaret not to cry because
choices: her crying would disturb Miss Edith
her father would be displeased with her
her aunt believed that grief was wrong
it was late and time to fall asleep
Newton needed quiet to mend the laces
questions.explanation: The text states she was 'bidden not to cry... because it would disturb Miss Edith.' B confuses this with a separate idea: Margaret hushed her sobs herself to avoid making her father unhappy, but the nurse's stated reason concerned Edith, not the father.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Margaret's family regarded her move to London as
choices: a temporary arrangement soon to be reversed
a punishment for some childhood misbehavior
a rise to grander social circumstances
a financial burden they secretly resented
a chance for her father to gain wealth
questions.explanation: The 'grand, pretty aunt,' the 'grander circumstances,' and arranging a wardrobe to suit them imply a step up in station. A is contradicted by Margaret's dread of leaving 'for ever,' which signals permanence, not a temporary stay.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, "contriving" most nearly means
choices: deceiving others deliberately
complaining bitterly
traveling from place to place
devising and arranging
resting in idle comfort
questions.explanation: Paired with 'hoping, and planning,' contriving here means working out arrangements for the move, so D fits. A applies the word's negative sense of scheming or trickery, which does not match the earnest, hopeful preparations the family undertook.

questions.prompt: Newton's attitude toward leaving the old nursery can best be described as
choices: mournful and sentimental
bitter and resentful
practical and unsentimental
anxious and fearful
proud and boastful
questions.explanation: Newton bluntly says 'I shan't' be sorry, citing bad light and a draught, and looks ahead to Naples's warmth: level-headed practicality. A describes Margaret's feeling, not Newton's, and is the trap for readers who blur the two speakers' opposite reactions.

questions.prompt: Newton names all of the following as drawbacks of the old room EXCEPT
choices: the light in it is poor
there is a draught by the window
her eyesight has grown weaker
the room is too small and cramped
mending laces there is difficult
questions.explanation: Newton cites weak eyes, bad light, a draught 'enough to give one one's death of cold,' and trouble mending laces. She never calls the room small; Margaret calls it 'dismantled,' not cramped. D is the unstated option and thus the correct EXCEPT choice.

passages.title: The Business Agreement
passages.attribution: from The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett (1896)
passages.context: The narrator, a summer boarder in a Maine village, has rented a spare room from her landlady to use as a private study.
passages.text: For various reasons, the seclusion and uninterrupted days which had been looked forward to proved to be very rare in this otherwise delightful corner of the world. My hostess and I had made our shrewd business agreement on the basis of a simple cold luncheon at noon, and liberal restitution in the matter of hot suppers, to provide for which the lodger might sometimes be seen hurrying down the road, late in the day, with cunner line in hand. It was soon found that this arrangement made large allowance for Mrs. Todd's slow herb-gathering progresses through woods and pastures. The spruce-beer customers were pretty steady in hot weather, and there were many demands for different soothing syrups and elixirs with which the unwise curiosity of my early residence had made me acquainted. Knowing Mrs. Todd to be a widow, who had little beside this slender business and the income from one hungry lodger to maintain her, one's energies and even interest were quickly bestowed, until it became a matter of course that she should go afield every pleasant day, and that the lodger should answer all peremptory knocks at the side door. In taking an occasional wisdom-giving stroll in Mrs. Todd's company, and in acting as business partner during her frequent absences, I found the July days fly fast, and it was not until I felt myself confronted with too great pride and pleasure in the display, one night, of two dollars and twenty-seven cents which I had taken in during the day, that I remembered a long piece of writing, sadly belated now, which I was bound to do. To have been patted kindly on the shoulder and called "darlin'," to have been offered a surprise of early mushrooms for supper, to have had all the glory of making two dollars and twenty-seven cents in a single day, and then to renounce it all and withdraw from these pleasant successes, needed much resolution. Literary employments are so vexed with uncertainties at best, and it was not until the voice of conscience sounded louder in my ears than the sea on the nearest pebble beach that I said unkind words of withdrawal to Mrs.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: The beauty and seclusion of a coastal corner of the world inspire the narrator's finest writing.
A writer lodging with Mrs. Todd becomes so absorbed in helping run the widow's little herb business that the writing she came to do is neglected until conscience forces her back to it.
Mrs. Todd's spruce-beer and herbal remedies make her the most successful merchant in the village.
The narrator resolves to give up writing entirely, preferring the steady profits of trade.
A struggling widow can barely survive on the income from a single lodger.
questions.explanation: The passage traces how business duties crowded out the narrator's belated writing until conscience prevailed. Choice 4 names Mrs. Todd's slender income, a supporting detail, not the central point; and the narrator does return to writing, so it is not the main idea.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how much money does the narrator report having taken in during the single day of business?
choices: Two dollars
Two dollars and twenty-seven cents
Twenty-seven cents
Two dollars and seventy-two cents
Two hundred and twenty-seven dollars
questions.explanation: The text twice cites "two dollars and twenty-seven cents" taken in during the day. The other figures scramble or shrink that exact sum, which the passage states plainly and repeats when describing the day's small glory.

questions.prompt: The narrator's difficulty in withdrawing from the business most strongly suggests that she
choices: resented Mrs. Todd for taking advantage of her
found the tangible pleasures of the little business more immediately rewarding than uncertain literary work
secretly had no real talent for writing
intended to abandon writing permanently in favor of trade
disliked the coastal village and longed to leave it
questions.explanation: Her "pride and pleasure" in real earnings, set against literary work "vexed with uncertainties," shows concrete rewards outweighing writing in the moment. Choice 3 overreaches; her belated "long piece of writing" and eventual return imply she does write, just reluctantly, not that she lacks talent.

questions.prompt: In context, the word "peremptory" (used to describe the knocks the lodger answers at the side door) most nearly means
choices: faint and hesitant
insistent and demanding
cheerful and welcome
distant and infrequent
mysterious and alarming
questions.explanation: Customers rap urgently for goods, so "peremptory" means insistent and demanding. Choice 0 is its opposite; choice 4 borrows an eerie tone the businesslike scene never supports. The knocks require prompt answering, confirming the sense of commanding urgency.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward her weeks acting as Mrs. Todd's business partner is best described as
choices: fondly amused and appreciative
bitterly resentful
coldly detached and businesslike
anxious and fearful
scornful and condescending
questions.explanation: Phrases like "great pride and pleasure," "pleasant successes," and gentle self-mockery over $2.27 convey warm, amused fondness. Choice 2 is tempting given the "business agreement," but the affectionate, playful diction shows genuine delight, not cold detachment.

questions.prompt: The passage mentions all of the following EXCEPT
choices: spruce-beer sold in hot weather
soothing syrups and elixirs
a cunner line carried down the road
freshly baked loaves of bread
a simple cold luncheon at noon
questions.explanation: Spruce-beer, syrups and elixirs, the cunner line, and the cold noon luncheon all appear in the text. Freshly baked bread is never mentioned; the only supper surprise named is early mushrooms, making the loaves of bread the sole option absent from the passage.

passages.title: The Motor Drives Away
passages.attribution: from The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie (1920)
passages.context: Captain Hastings is staying at Styles Court. Here the household sees off a departing guest, and undercurrents show among them.
passages.text: Miss Howard was swallowed up in an eager chorus of protests and good-byes. The Inglethorps did not appear. As the motor drove away, Mrs. Cavendish suddenly detached herself from the group, and moved across the drive to the lawn to meet a tall bearded man who had been evidently making for the house. The colour rose in her cheeks as she held out her hand to him. "Who is that?" I asked sharply, for instinctively I distrusted the man. "That's Dr. Bauerstein," said John shortly. "And who is Dr. Bauerstein?" "He's staying in the village doing a rest cure, after a bad nervous breakdown. He's a London specialist; a very clever man--one of the greatest living experts on poisons, I believe." "And he's a great friend of Mary's," put in Cynthia, the irrepressible. John Cavendish frowned and changed the subject. "Come for a stroll, Hastings. This has been a most rotten business. She always had a rough tongue, but there is no stauncher friend in England than Evelyn Howard." He took the path through the plantation, and we walked down to the village through the woods which bordered one side of the estate.
