As we passed through one of the gates on our way home again, a pretty young woman of gipsy type coming in the opposite direction bowed and smiled. "That's a pretty girl," I remarked appreciatively. John's face hardened. "That is Mrs. Raikes." "The one that Miss Howard---- " "Exactly," said John, with rather unnecessary abruptness. I thought of the white-haired old lady in the big house, and that vivid wicked little face that had just smiled into ours, and a vague chill of foreboding crept over me. I brushed it aside. "Styles is really a glorious old place," I said to John. He nodded rather gloomily. "Yes, it's a fine property. It'll be mine some day--should be mine now by rights, if my father had only made a decent will. And then I shouldn't be so damned hard up as I am now."

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: The narrator recounts a peaceful country walk that finally cures his worries
Through the narrator's introductions and reactions, the passage builds an atmosphere of suspicion and unease at Styles
John Cavendish explains the legal grounds on which he should inherit the estate
The passage chronicles the medical career of a celebrated London poison expert
Mary Cavendish's open romance with Dr. Bauerstein becomes the central conflict
questions.explanation: The scene introduces Bauerstein, Mrs. Raikes, and John's gloom while the narrator feels 'a vague chill of foreboding,' establishing unease. The closest trap (C) seizes on John's inheritance remark, but that is one closing detail, not the passage's overall thrust.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Dr. Bauerstein is staying in the village because he is
choices: a close friend John invited to visit
recovering from a nervous breakdown by doing a rest cure
secretly investigating a poisoning at Styles
managing property he owns near the estate
waiting to meet the Inglethorps
questions.explanation: John states he is 'doing a rest cure, after a bad nervous breakdown.' Trap (C) misuses the detail that he is a poisons expert, but the passage never says he is investigating anything; that link is the reader's inference, not the text.

questions.prompt: John's 'hardened' face and 'unnecessary abruptness' when Mrs. Raikes passes most strongly suggest that
choices: John has never seen Mrs. Raikes before
the subject of Mrs. Raikes is a sensitive or uncomfortable one for John
John is delighted to greet an old friend
John resents Hastings for slowing their walk
John dislikes anyone of gipsy appearance
questions.explanation: His frown, hardened face, and clipped 'Exactly' signal discomfort with the topic, hinting at scandal. Trap (E) takes 'gipsy type' literally, but nothing shows general prejudice; his reaction is specific to this woman and what Miss Howard implied.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'stauncher' most nearly means
choices: fiercer
more loyal
older
more outspoken
wealthier
questions.explanation: 'No stauncher friend in England than Evelyn Howard' praises her steadfast loyalty. Trap (D) is baited by the nearby 'rough tongue,' but staunch describes reliability of friendship, not bluntness of speech.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Dr. Bauerstein is best described as
choices: warmly admiring
openly hostile
instinctively distrustful
coolly indifferent
respectfully curious
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'instinctively I distrusted the man' and asks about him 'sharply.' Trap (B) overstates this: his distrust is private and instinctive, not voiced as open hostility toward Bauerstein himself.

questions.prompt: The passage mentions all of the following characters EXCEPT
choices: Cynthia
John Cavendish
Dr. Bauerstein
Lawrence Cavendish
Mrs. Raikes
questions.explanation: Cynthia, John, Bauerstein, and Mrs. Raikes all appear by name; 'Lawrence Cavendish' never does. The trap relies on the familiar surname 'Cavendish' (shared with John and Mrs. Cavendish) to make the invented figure seem present.

passages.title: Compliments to the Recluse
passages.attribution: from The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë (1848)
passages.context: In a country village, the narrator's family gossips about a mysterious young widow, Mrs. Graham, newly moved to Wildfell Hall.
passages.text: But if he intended the speech to be hailed as a master-stroke of wit, he signally failed, for nobody laughed. However, he was not much disconcerted at that; for when he had taken a mouthful of bread and butter and was about to swallow a gulp of tea, the humour of the thing burst upon him with such irresistible force, that he was obliged to jump up from the table, and rush snorting and choking from the room; and a minute after, was heard screaming in fearful agony in the garden. As for me, I was hungry, and contented myself with silently demolishing the tea, ham, and toast, while my mother and sister went on talking, and continued to discuss the apparent or non-apparent circumstances, and probable or improbable history of the mysterious lady; but I must confess that, after my brother’s misadventure, I once or twice raised the cup to my lips, and put it down again without daring to taste the contents, lest I should injure my dignity by a similar explosion. The next day my mother and Rose hastened to pay their compliments to the fair recluse; and came back but little wiser than they went; though my mother declared she did not regret the journey, for if she had not gained much good, she flattered herself she had imparted some, and that was better: she had given some useful advice, which, she hoped, would not be thrown away; for Mrs. Graham, though she said little to any purpose, and appeared somewhat self-opinionated, seemed not incapable of reflection,—though she did not know where she had been all her life, poor thing, for she betrayed a lamentable ignorance on certain points, and had not even the sense to be ashamed of it. ‘On what points, mother?’ asked I. ‘On household matters, and all the little niceties of cookery, and such things, that every lady ought to be familiar with, whether she be required to make a practical use of her knowledge or not.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: A brother nearly dies while choking on food at the family table
A family meal disrupted by one member's amusement, followed by the mother's assessment of a new neighbor
A mother's detailed lesson on the rules of cookery and household management
The narrator's ongoing effort to keep his composure and dignity at dinner
An open quarrel between the mother and Mrs. Graham over proper manners
questions.explanation: The passage moves from the meal scene to the mother's report on Mrs. Graham, making B most complete. A is only one incident; C appears just at the end; D is too narrow; E never happens (no quarrel occurs).

questions.prompt: Why did the brother suddenly rush from the room?
choices: He was choking on a piece of ham he had swallowed
He was ashamed that no one had laughed at his joke
He was overcome by laughter at his own joke while drinking tea
He was angry that the others were ignoring him
He wished to be alone in the garden to think
questions.explanation: The humor "burst upon him" as he swallowed tea, so he rushed out "snorting and choking." C is correct. A names the wrong food and cause; B is contradicted because he was "not much disconcerted" by the silence.

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about the narrator from his handling of the teacup?
choices: He disliked the taste of the tea he was served
He feared the tea would physically harm him as it had his brother
He found the discussion of Mrs. Graham tedious
He wished to avoid embarrassing himself by laughing and choking as his brother had
He was simply too full to eat or drink anything more
questions.explanation: He set the cup down "lest I should injure my dignity by a similar explosion"—fearing he would laugh and choke like his brother. D captures this. B mistakes the concern: it is dignity, not physical harm, that worries him.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "recluse" most nearly means
choices: a strikingly beautiful woman
a person who lives withdrawn from society
a recent arrival to the village
a woman of questionable reputation
a stubborn, opinionated person
questions.explanation: The "fair recluse" is Mrs. Graham, who keeps to herself in seclusion, so B fits. A is tempting because "fair" means beautiful, but that adjective, not "recluse," carries that sense; C and E describe her but not the word.

questions.prompt: The mother's attitude toward Mrs. Graham can best be described as
choices: warmly admiring and affectionate
openly hostile and vengeful
condescending and mildly critical
indifferent and thoroughly bored
fearful and deeply suspicious
questions.explanation: The mother flatters herself she "imparted some" good and faults Mrs. Graham's "lamentable ignorance" and lack of shame—patronizing and critical, so C. B overstates it (no vengeance); A is the opposite of her judgmental tone.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the mother finds fault with Mrs. Graham for all of the following EXCEPT
choices: appearing somewhat self-opinionated
showing ignorance of household matters
lacking knowledge of cookery
displaying no shame about her ignorance
refusing to accept the advice offered to her
questions.explanation: The mother merely hopes her advice "would not be thrown away"; no refusal is stated, so E is the exception. A, B, C, and D are all explicitly named among Mrs. Graham's faults.

passages.title: The Blue Teacup
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
Mrs. Ashcombe poured the tea with the particular steadiness of a woman who has decided not to say the thing she means. Across the parlour, her niece Eleanor sat very upright in the good chair, her gloves folded in her lap like a small surrender.

"You will find Mr. Pruitt a sensible man," said Mrs. Ashcombe. "He does not indulge in fancies. A house wants a steady head, not a head full of poetry."

"I am sure he is everything sensible," Eleanor answered. She took the offered cup. It was the blue one, the one that had been her mother's, and she held it as though it might be asked back.

"You say that as if it were a fault." Mrs. Ashcombe's smile did not reach beyond her lips. "At my age, one learns that comfort is not the meanest thing a person can be given. Your mother married for feeling, and see what feeling left her."

Eleanor looked at the rain sliding down the glass, at the garden gone grey beneath it. "It left her me," she said, quite mildly, and drank.

For a moment the only sound was the clock, which had belonged to no one in particular and therefore ticked without reproach. Mrs. Ashcombe set down the pot.

"I only wish you to be provided for."

"I know what you wish." Eleanor turned the cup once in its saucer, so that the chipped rim faced away from them both. "And I am grateful, Aunt, truly. I shall give Mr. Pruitt my answer on Thursday."

She did not say what the answer would be. Mrs. Ashcombe, who had spent a lifetime reading the weather of other people's faces, found that for once she could not tell whether the sky before her promised fair or foul, and poured herself a second cup she did not want.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A wealthy aunt generously secures a comfortable future for a grateful orphaned niece
A quiet clash of values unfolds as an aunt urges a practical marriage while her niece withholds her true intentions
Two women reconcile their long-standing differences over the memory of a shared relative
A young woman eagerly prepares to accept a proposal arranged by her family
An aunt mourns her deceased sister while planning a household reorganization
questions.explanation: B captures the value conflict and Eleanor's guardedness. A and D take Mrs. Ashcombe's wishes as the outcome, but Eleanor never agrees; C invents a reconciliation the tension contradicts; E overstates the mourning, which is only a passing argument.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the blue teacup had previously belonged to
choices: Mrs. Ashcombe
Eleanor's mother
Mr. Pruitt
no one in particular
Eleanor herself
questions.explanation: The text states the blue cup 'had been her mother's.' D describes the clock, not the cup (a trap for skimmers); A, C, and E are never supported by the passage.

questions.prompt: The exchange most strongly suggests that Eleanor
choices: intends to accept Mr. Pruitt without hesitation
has not yet decided and remains genuinely uncertain
is privately inclined to refuse Mr. Pruitt despite her politeness
feels no affection or gratitude toward her aunt
plans to leave her aunt's house immediately
questions.explanation: Eleanor defends feeling, guards her mother's memory, and pointedly withholds a promised answer while turning the chipped rim away, hinting at refusal. A ignores her resistance; B mistakes deliberate concealment for uncertainty; D contradicts her sincere 'grateful'; E is unsupported.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "indulge" most nearly means
choices: spoil with kindness
give way to
forbid strictly
conceal from others
profit from
questions.explanation: 'He does not indulge in fancies' means he does not give way to or permit himself them. A reflects 'indulge' in the sense of pampering a person, a common but wrong fit here; C reverses the meaning; D and E do not match the idiom 'indulge in.'

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward Mrs. Ashcombe can best be described as
choices: openly contemptuous and mocking
warmly admiring and protective
coolly ironic yet quietly sympathetic
indifferent and strictly factual
bitterly resentful and accusing
questions.explanation: Details like the smile that 'did not reach beyond her lips' and the unwanted second cup are ironic, yet the closing shows her genuine uncertainty, inviting sympathy. A and E overstate hostility; B ignores the irony; D denies the evident, controlled judgment.

questions.prompt: The final sentence, in which Mrs. Ashcombe pours "a second cup she did not want," primarily serves to
choices: reveal that she is thirsty after a long conversation
emphasize her mastery over the situation and her niece
suggest her loss of composure and inability to read Eleanor
show that the tea has grown cold and unpleasant
indicate that the conversation has formally concluded
questions.explanation: The pointless second cup mirrors her admission that she 'could not tell' Eleanor's meaning, dramatizing lost control. A reads literally; B reverses the meaning, since she is unsettled, not masterful; D and E seize on incidental details rather than the sentence's emotional function.

passages.title: Downshift
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
The parking lot behind the shuttered grocery store was empty except for a shopping cart marooned near the curb, and that was exactly why Grandpa Ford had chosen it. Marisol gripped the wheel at ten and two, the way the manual insisted, and waited for him to say something encouraging. He didn't. He only pointed at the far light pole and said, "Get us there. No hurry."

She eased off the brake. The car crept forward in a series of apologetic lurches, and she braced for the lecture—the one her mother delivered nightly about focus, about the future, about how nothing came to people who flinched. Grandpa Ford said none of it. When the engine stalled, he simply reached over, turned the key, and folded his hands again.

"You're waiting for me to be angry," he said.

"You're allowed to be."

"I'm seventy-one. I've spent enough anger." He watched the shopping cart as if it might wander off. "Your grandmother taught me here. Same lot. It had a store then."

Marisol tried again. This time the car found its rhythm, and the light pole slid toward them, unremarkable and enormous. She realized she was holding her breath and let it out.

"She'd have grabbed the wheel," he added. "Loved me, couldn't help it. I decided I wouldn't do that to you." He said it lightly, but she heard the cost underneath—years of practice, of teaching his hands to stay still.

They reached the pole. She stopped, too hard, and the seatbelt caught them both.

"There," he said, unbothered. "Now the long way back."

She turned the car around. Somewhere in the turning she understood that he was not teaching her to drive. He was handing her something and trusting her not to drop it, and the trust was heavier than any wheel.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about
choices: an old man overcoming his own fear of driving
a bitter conflict between Marisol and her mother
a grandfather deliberately giving his granddaughter room to learn, and the trust that gesture carries
the slow decline of a neighborhood after its store closes
a driving test that Marisol ultimately fails
questions.explanation: C captures the central action and its meaning—restraint as trust. B and D seize on the mother and shuttered store, which are only background details, and E misreads the hard stop as failure.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Grandpa Ford chose this particular parking lot because
choices: it was close to his home
it was empty, making it safe for practice
his late wife had asked him to use it
the grocery store offered lessons there
Marisol's mother had suggested it
questions.explanation: The text says the lot was empty "and that was exactly why" he chose it. C twists the later memory of his wife into a false cause; D and E invent instructions the passage never states.

questions.prompt: The passage most strongly suggests that Marisol's mother
choices: is a patient, encouraging driving instructor
never learned how to drive herself
still works at the grocery store
tends to be critical and demanding
openly disapproves of Grandpa Ford
questions.explanation: Her nightly lecture about focus, the future, and not flinching implies a demanding nature. A reverses this; E overreaches, since the passage contrasts styles without showing disapproval between the adults.

questions.prompt: In the phrase "a series of apologetic lurches," the word "apologetic" most nearly means
choices: regretful and sorry
smooth and confident
tentative and hesitant
loud and sudden
rude and defiant
questions.explanation: Describing a beginner's stalling car, "apologetic" means hesitant and unsure. A gives the word's everyday meaning of expressing an apology, which doesn't fit a car; B is the opposite of a lurch.

questions.prompt: Grandpa Ford's attitude toward Marisol is best described as
choices: patient and quietly trusting
indifferent and distracted
anxious and controlling
stern and disappointed
playful and teasing
questions.explanation: He refuses to lecture, restarts the car calmly, and hands her control—patient trust. B misreads his silence as indifference, but the text shows the deliberate "cost" of his restraint; C and D contradict his composure.

questions.prompt: The author includes the detail that Grandpa Ford's wife "would have grabbed the wheel" primarily to
choices: show that his wife was an unusually nervous person
explain why Marisol is such a poor driver
reveal how deeply the grandfather still misses his wife
criticize the wife's outdated teaching methods
contrast her instinct to intervene with his deliberate restraint
questions.explanation: The comparison highlights the choice he made not to intervene, defining his gift of trust. C is tempting—grief is present—but the detail's function is contrast, not mourning; A and D misjudge a loving memory as criticism.

passages.title: The Rope's Argument
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
The mountain had been patient with them for three days, and Halloran had come to distrust its patience. Patience, he had learned in forty years of climbing, was only the slow arithmetic by which a peak added up its debts. Now, at the ninth hour, the debt came due. The storm broke over the ridge without ceremony, flinging ice that stung like buckshot, and the boy—Ewan, the outfitter's son, nineteen and certain of everything—was forty feet below him on the line, shouting words the wind unmade before they arrived.

Halloran braced his boots in the crusted snow and took the rope's weight across his hip. It bit through his coat. Below, Ewan had slipped; the slack had gone rigid, and now the whole mountain seemed to hang from Halloran's frozen hands. He thought, unbidden, of the knife at his belt. One motion and the weight would be gone, and he would climb down light and alive to tell the boy's father something noble.

He did not reach for it.

Instead he leaned back against the pull, made himself a stubborn thing, a knot in the mountain's rope, and began to haul. His shoulders screamed. The ice on his lashes cracked. He counted—not the feet, which would have discouraged him, but the pulls, each one a small refusal.

When Ewan's mittened hand finally found the ledge, the boy was weeping, and ashamed of it. Halloran looked away to spare him.

"You could have cut me loose," Ewan said, when the wind allowed. "I felt you thinking it."

"A man thinks a great many things," Halloran said, "and does about one of them." He coiled the rope. "Get up. The mountain isn't finished, and neither are we."

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: a veteran climber's choice to save a younger companion despite the temptation to abandon him
the specific dangers of climbing a mountain during a sudden winter storm
a bitter rivalry between an experienced guide and a reckless young man
the ways in which a mountain punishes those who are unprepared for it
a boy's gradual discovery that he must learn to respect the wilderness
questions.explanation: A captures the central action and moral turn: Halloran resists cutting Ewan loose and hauls him up. B and D fixate on the storm setting, mere backdrop; C overstates rivalry; E centers Ewan, but the passage's focus is Halloran's choice.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Halloran counts the pulls rather than the feet because
choices: the blowing snow makes it impossible to judge distance
counting the feet would have discouraged him
Ewan keeps shouting the numbers up to him
he has climbed this exact route many times before
his frozen hands can no longer feel the rope
questions.explanation: The text states he counted "not the feet, which would have discouraged him, but the pulls." B restates this directly. A and E borrow storm and cold imagery from elsewhere; C reverses the fact that the wind unmakes Ewan's words; D is never claimed.

questions.prompt: Halloran's remark, "A man thinks a great many things, and does about one of them," most nearly suggests that he believes
choices: most people are ultimately dishonest about their true intentions
a person should never confess to having dark or shameful thoughts
having a thought is separate from choosing to act on it
he has always been wiser and steadier than the reckless Ewan
reflection matters far more than action in a crisis
questions.explanation: He concedes he considered cutting the rope yet did not, distinguishing thought from deed—so C. B misreads it as denial (he admits the thought). D and E add claims of superiority or reflection over action that the line, and his hauling, contradict.

questions.prompt: As used in paragraph 1, "unmade" most nearly means
choices: invented
muffled
destroyed
delivered
hardened
questions.explanation: The wind unmakes Ewan's words "before they arrived"—it scatters and destroys them, so C. B (muffled) tempts because wind dulls sound, but the words never reach Halloran at all. D is the opposite; A and E ignore the context of loss.

questions.prompt: Halloran's attitude toward the mountain is best described as
choices: affectionate and reverent
wary and respectful
terrified and defeated
contemptuous and dismissive
indifferent and bored
questions.explanation: He distrusts the peak's "patience" and warns "the mountain isn't finished"—caution paired with hard respect, so B. C overshoots; he stays controlled, not terrified or beaten. D and E ignore that he takes the mountain's threat seriously; A misreads wariness as warmth.

questions.prompt: The author sets the sentence "He did not reach for it" apart as its own one-line paragraph chiefly in order to
choices: spotlight the pivotal moment of moral decision
show that Halloran had forgotten about the knife
signal that the storm had suddenly passed
imply that his hands were too cold to move at all
reveal that Ewan had already reached safety
questions.explanation: Isolating the line dramatizes the choice against the knife, its own weighty beat—so A. B contradicts that he clearly considered it; C and E invent plot events; D confuses the moment: he chooses not to cut, then immediately hauls with those same hands.

passages.title: The Architects of Absence
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
We like to imagine that intelligence announces itself loudly—with tools, with problem-solving, with the sort of cleverness we recognize because it resembles our own. So when the male satin bowerbird of eastern Australia builds his bower, we are tempted to admire the wrong thing. We notice the structure: two parallel walls of woven twigs, painstakingly assembled over weeks. We notice the collection arranged before it: berries, feathers, petals, and bottle caps, sorted almost obsessively by color, blue objects favored above all. It is easy to call him an artist and stop there.

But the bower is not a home; no eggs are ever laid in it. It is not shelter; it protects nothing from the weather. The bird invests enormous effort in a construction he will abandon the moment the breeding season ends. To understand the bower, we must resist the instinct to ask what it is for in any practical sense, and ask instead what it says.

What it says is directed at a critical, watchful audience: the female, who visits many bowers before choosing. She is not fooled by ornament alone. Researchers have found that she attends to subtler signals—the evenness of the walls, the consistency of the color scheme, even the male's restraint in not overwhelming her during his display. A clumsy bower betrays a clumsy builder. The decoration, in other words, is not decoration. It is evidence.

And here is the quiet lesson the bowerbird offers us. We tend to measure animal minds by what animals make. Yet the bower's true significance lies in what it demonstrates about the maker—his patience, his precision, his capacity to hold a plan across many days. The bird has not made an object at all. He has made an argument. The most sophisticated thing in the forest is not the structure standing in the clearing, but the judgment standing quietly beside it, deciding whether to believe.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Bowerbirds build elaborate nests to protect their eggs from harsh Australian weather.
The bowerbird's true intelligence lies less in the structure he builds than in what that structure reveals about him to a discerning female.
Female bowerbirds are easily deceived by males who collect the most colorful decorations.
Scientists have failed to explain why male bowerbirds prefer the color blue.
Human beings are the only species capable of genuine artistic expression.
questions.explanation: B captures the author's central claim: the bower matters as evidence of the maker's mind, judged by the female. A is contradicted (no eggs are laid there). C reverses the text—she is 'not fooled.' D and E fixate on minor or absent details.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, the female bowerbird attends to all of the following signals EXCEPT:
choices: the evenness of the bower's walls
the consistency of the color scheme
the male's restraint during his display
the number of eggs laid within the bower
the overall quality of the male's construction
questions.explanation: The passage states no eggs are ever laid in the bower, so D cannot be a signal she assesses. A, B, and C are named explicitly as subtler signals; E is supported by 'A clumsy bower betrays a clumsy builder.'

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "betrays" most nearly means
choices: deceives
reveals
abandons
decorates
protects
questions.explanation: 'A clumsy bower betrays a clumsy builder' means the bower reveals or exposes the builder's clumsiness. 'Deceives' (A) is the common meaning of betray but contradicts the logic here. C, D, and E do not fit the sentence's sense of disclosure.

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that the author would most likely agree with which of the following statements?
choices: Practical function is the best measure of an animal's intelligence.
A creation can be valuable chiefly for what it demonstrates about its creator's mind.
Elaborate ornamentation is usually a sign of deception in nature.
Female animals rarely play a meaningful role in evolutionary outcomes.
The most complex object in an environment is always the most important one.
questions.explanation: The closing paragraph argues the bower's significance is what it demonstrates about the maker, supporting B. A is what the author urges us to 'resist.' E is explicitly reversed in the final sentence. C and D distort claims the passage never makes.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the bowerbird's bower can best be described as
choices: dismissive and skeptical
thoughtfully admiring
coldly scientific and detached
nostalgic and sorrowful
alarmed and cautionary
questions.explanation: The author admires the bower but redirects that admiration toward the mind it reveals—'thoughtfully admiring.' A misreads the reframing as dismissal. C ignores the reflective, appreciative 'quiet lesson.' D and E introduce emotions absent from the text.

questions.prompt: The author most likely states that the bird "has made an argument" rather than an object in order to
choices: prove that bowerbirds can communicate using human language
emphasize that the bower functions as evidence of the builder's qualities rather than as a useful thing
suggest that the female and male are in genuine conflict
criticize scientists for studying bowerbirds at all
argue that the bower should be preserved as a work of art
questions.explanation: Calling the bower 'an argument' stresses that it persuades the female by serving as evidence of the male's patience and skill—supporting B. A takes 'argument' too literally. C, D, and E introduce conflicts, criticisms, or aims the passage never advances.

passages.title: The Slowing Day
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
Somewhere in a laboratory drawer, a fossilized coral holds a calendar no one meant to keep. Corals lay down a fine ridge of skeleton each day and a coarser band each year, and if you count the daily ridges inside a single yearly band, you can ask a startling question: how many days were in a year four hundred million years ago? The answer is not the familiar 365. It is closer to 400.

The planet has not sped around the Sun any faster or slower; the length of the year is fixed by orbit, not by mood. What has changed is the day. Earth's spin is gradually slowing, braked by the tug of the Moon's tides, which drag against the ocean floor like a hand trailing in water. Each century, the day lengthens by roughly two thousandths of a second. That sounds like nothing. Over hundreds of millions of years, it is the difference between a crowded ancient year and our own.

It is tempting to read this as a comfortable story of gentle progress, the Earth easing into a calmer rhythm. Resist that reading. The same tides that slow us are also nudging the Moon outward, a few centimeters a year, so the companion that steadies our tilt is quietly leaving. Nothing here is designed for our convenience; the coral simply recorded what happened to be true.

That is the discipline the coral demands. It did not know it was a clock, and it did not care what number we hoped to find. A good measurement is indifferent to our wishes, and the honest reader must be too. When the ridges say 400, you do not round them down to the figure you already believed. You let the small, patient scratches of a dead animal correct the confident arithmetic of the living, and you call that correction knowledge.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Coral fossils are the only reliable way to measure the age of the Earth.
Fossil corals record that Earth's day has lengthened over time, and such evidence should be trusted over our assumptions.
The Moon will eventually drift so far away that it will no longer affect Earth's tides.
Ancient years were more crowded with days because the Earth once orbited the Sun more quickly.
Scientists were surprised to learn that a year has always contained exactly 365 days.
questions.explanation: The passage uses coral bands to show the day has slowed and argues we must trust the data. (D) and (E) contradict the text (the orbit is fixed; ancient years held ~400 days). (A) and (C) are unsupported overstatements.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Earth's spin is slowing because of
choices: the gradual cooling of the planet's molten core
the friction of the Moon's tides dragging against the ocean floor
the Sun's gravity pulling more strongly over time
the accumulation of coral skeleton on the seabed
the changing tilt of Earth's rotational axis
questions.explanation: The text directly names the Moon's tides dragging against the ocean floor as the brake. The Sun (C), core (A), coral (D), and tilt (E) are never given as the cause; the Moon steadies the tilt rather than the reverse.

questions.prompt: The passage most strongly suggests that the author would agree that
choices: evidence should be revised to fit our most confident expectations
a measurement is more valuable precisely because it does not favor what we hope to find
the Moon's departure will eventually be reversed by the same tides that cause it
only living organisms can produce trustworthy scientific records
the length of the year changes as often as the length of the day
questions.explanation: The closing lines praise measurement that is 'indifferent to our wishes.' (A) inverts this. (C) and (E) contradict the physics described, and (D) is undercut by the coral, a dead animal, serving as the clock.

questions.prompt: As used in the third paragraph, the word "nudging" most nearly means
choices: scolding
gently pushing
illuminating
measuring
abandoning
questions.explanation: The Moon is being 'nudged outward' a few centimeters a year, meaning gently pushed. 'Scolding' (A) misreads the human sense of nudge; 'abandoning' (E) borrows from the Moon's leaving but names the effect, not the motion; (C) and (D) fit nothing.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the "comfortable story of gentle progress" (paragraph 3) is best described as
choices: nostalgic and mournful
skeptical and corrective
enthusiastic and approving
confused and hesitant
indifferent and detached
questions.explanation: The author says 'Resist that reading,' then supplies contrary facts, a skeptical, corrective stance. (C) reverses it. (A) and (E) miss the active pushback, and (D) mistakes a firm argument for uncertainty.

questions.prompt: The author mentions all of the following in support of the argument EXCEPT
choices: the daily ridges and yearly bands laid down by coral
the Moon's slow drift outward from Earth
the roughly two-thousandths-of-a-second lengthening of the day each century
the fixed length of Earth's orbit around the Sun
the discovery of coral fossils preserved in ancient ocean trenches
questions.explanation: The passage never mentions ocean trenches; the coral sits in a laboratory drawer. Ridges and bands (A), the Moon's drift (B), the per-century figure (C), and the fixed orbit (D) all appear explicitly as evidence.

passages.title: The Second Life of Timber
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
We are taught to read a forest by its canopy—to measure its health in the reach of living crowns, in the green ambition of trees straining toward light. A fallen trunk, by this logic, is an ending: a body laid down, a column of stored sunlight surrendered to rot. Foresters once called such logs "waste" and hauled them off, tidying the woods as one might clear a cluttered room. The assumption seemed obvious. Death is subtraction; a dead tree is simply a tree that has stopped.

But crouch beside a rotting log in an old-growth forest and count what lives there. A single fallen Douglas fir may host more organisms in decay than it ever sheltered while standing. Beetles tunnel its softening wood; fungi thread it with pale filaments, dissolving cellulose into nutrients the soil cannot make alone. Salamanders shelter in its damp hollows. Seedlings—unable to compete on the shaded forest floor—germinate along its spine, drawing moisture from the pulp, until entire rows of young hemlocks stand like a colonnade upon the corpse of their ancestor. Ecologists call these "nurse logs," and the name is not sentiment but description.

The error, it turns out, was one of pace. We watched the forest at the speed of our own lives and mistook slowness for stillness, silence for absence. A log does not stop; it merely changes its unit of time. What looks like ruin is a transfer—carbon, water, and habitat passing from one generation to the next through the long courtesy of decay.

So the tidy forest, swept clean of its dead, was never the healthy one. It was starving, though it looked immaculate. Nature, it seems, keeps no waste; it only keeps changing the address at which life is delivered.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: explaining why foresters no longer harvest Douglas fir
arguing that a decaying log sustains more life than its appearance suggests
describing the life cycle of a single hemlock seedling
comparing old-growth forests with managed timber plantations
warning that human tidiness will soon destroy all forests
questions.explanation: B captures the central claim that a dead log is teeming with life. A and C are minor details, too narrow to be the main point; D and E stray beyond or overstate what the passage actually argues.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, seedlings germinate on fallen logs because
choices: the logs protect them from beetles and salamanders
fungi deliberately plant them there
they cannot compete on the shaded forest floor
foresters arrange the seedlings in neat rows
the canopy directs extra sunlight onto the logs
questions.explanation: C restates the text: seedlings, 'unable to compete on the shaded forest floor,' grow along the log. D misreads the 'colonnade' simile as literal arrangement; A and E invent causes the passage never gives.

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that the earlier foresters' mistake stemmed chiefly from
choices: a shortage of proper equipment
judging the forest by a human sense of time
deliberate greed for timber profits
not knowing which trees were native
an inability to see the canopy clearly
questions.explanation: B matches 'the error was one of pace'—watching 'at the speed of our own lives.' C tempts because logs were called 'waste,' but the passage blames flawed perception, not motive. The others are never implied.

questions.prompt: As used in the final paragraph, "courtesy" (in "the long courtesy of decay") most nearly means
choices: polite manners
generous favor
legal permission
formal apology
social grace
questions.explanation: Decay is portrayed as a service that passes life onward, so 'generous favor' fits. A and E exploit the everyday sense of politeness, which the natural context does not support; C and D are unrelated meanings.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the decaying log is best described as
choices: detached and clinical
nostalgic and mournful
appreciative and quietly corrective
alarmed and urgent
skeptical and dismissive
questions.explanation: The author admires the log's hidden life while gently correcting a misconception—appreciative and quietly corrective. A ignores the evident warmth; D overstates the calm, measured urgency; B and E misread the closing wonder as grief or doubt.

questions.prompt: The passage mentions all of the following as living on or benefiting from a fallen log EXCEPT
choices: beetles
fungi
salamanders
hemlock seedlings
woodpeckers
questions.explanation: Beetles, fungi, salamanders, and young hemlocks are each named in the second paragraph. Woodpeckers, though plausible forest inhabitants, are never mentioned, making E the exception.

passages.title: Empty-Handed
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
Before the seventeenth century, nearly everyone carried a pocket the same way: as a small pouch, tied around the waist and hidden beneath layers of clothing, reached through a discreet slit in the skirt or coat. It held coins, keys, a letter, perhaps a sweet. Men and women alike wore these detachable bags, and in them a person kept the private inventory of a life.

Then fashion turned. As men's clothing grew tailored, the pouch was sewn directly into the garment—permanent, sturdy, plentiful. A modern suit boasts pockets in the jacket, the vest, the trousers: places to stash a wallet, a watch, a folded plan. Women's dress moved the opposite direction. Slim silhouettes left no room for a bulge of belongings, and the tied-on pocket was banished. In its place came the handbag: a pocket exiled to the end of the arm, a thing that must be held, watched, set down, and remembered.

We are told this was mere style, the neutral drift of taste. I am not convinced. A pocket is a quiet declaration of independence—it says that what you carry is yours, secure, and instantly at hand, requiring no permission and no spare grip. To hand someone a bag instead is to hand them a task. The person with sewn-in pockets walks through the world unburdened, hands free, ready. The person with a purse is forever managing it.

Look closely, then, at where the seams are placed, for clothing is never only cloth. Every garment makes an argument about what its wearer is expected to do—to labor, to hurry, to reach for a tool, or merely to be looked at. The pocket, small and overlooked, keeps score. Its absence is not an oversight. It is a sentence, stitched shut.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: tracing how detachable pouches were manufactured in the seventeenth century
arguing that the disappearance of women's pockets reflects unequal expectations rather than neutral fashion
explaining why handbags are more convenient than sewn-in pockets
celebrating the careful tailoring of the modern men's suit
cataloguing the personal items that people once kept close
questions.explanation: B names the stance and the turn ('I am not convinced' that it was 'mere style'). E and A seize on early details; C reverses the author, who treats the bag as a burden, not a convenience.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, before the seventeenth century, pockets were
choices: sewn permanently into the trousers
worn only by men
detachable pouches tied at the waist
carried at the end of the arm
reserved for holding coins alone
questions.explanation: The passage opens with the pouch 'tied around the waist.' A describes the later men's version; B contradicts 'men and women alike'; D is the handbag; E ignores keys, letters, and sweets.

questions.prompt: The author suggests that carrying a handbag rather than using pockets
choices: keeps valuables noticeably safer
imposes an ongoing burden of attention
became fashionable among men first
frees the hands for physical labor
was a choice women made freely
questions.explanation: The bag 'must be held, watched,' and one is 'forever managing it.' D and A describe the pocket's advantages, not the bag's; E contradicts the author's claim that the change was imposed, not neutral taste.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "boasts" most nearly means
choices: brags about
exaggerates
possesses
praises loudly
conceals
questions.explanation: 'A modern suit boasts pockets in the jacket, the vest, the trousers' means it has or features them. 'Brags' and 'praises' invoke the everyday sense of speaking proudly, but a suit cannot speak; 'conceals' is the opposite.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the claim that the change was "mere style" is best described as
choices: neutral and detached
skeptical and pointed
nostalgic and mournful
amused and playful
confused and uncertain
questions.explanation: 'I am not convinced,' followed by a sharp counterargument, signals pointed skepticism. 'Neutral and detached' misses that the author clearly takes a side; 'nostalgic' wrongly implies longing rather than critique.

questions.prompt: The final paragraph serves primarily to
choices: summarize the manufacturing history of garments
broaden the argument into a claim that all clothing reveals expectations
concede that the pocket's absence was accidental
supply statistics on modern fashion trends
introduce a new everyday object for comparison
questions.explanation: The ending widens from pockets to 'every garment makes an argument.' C directly contradicts 'its absence is not an oversight'; A, D, and E name material the paragraph never provides.

passages.title: The Woman Who Mapped the Rain
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
When Eunice Newton Foote presented her findings to a scientific gathering in 1856, she was not permitted to read them aloud. A male colleague delivered her paper while she sat in the audience, listening to another voice explain what her own hands had discovered.

The experiment itself was almost embarrassingly simple. Foote filled glass cylinders with different gases—ordinary air, hydrogen, carbon dioxide—and set them in sunlight. Then she watched her thermometers. The cylinder holding carbon dioxide grew hotter than the others and, once shaded, held its warmth far longer. From this modest observation she drew a conclusion that would take the wider world decades to accept: an atmosphere richer in that gas would give our planet a higher temperature.

She had, in a parlor with a few thermometers, glimpsed the greenhouse effect. Yet her name vanished almost immediately. The scientist John Tyndall, working with far more elaborate instruments a few years later, is usually credited as the effect's discoverer, and there is no evidence he ever knew of Foote's work. Neither malice nor theft buried her—only the quiet machinery of an age that expected women to observe the weather, not to explain it.

What strikes a modern reader is not that Foote was forgotten, but how calmly she seems to have accepted the arrangement. Her paper offers no complaint, no plea to be taken seriously. It simply records what the thermometers said and lets the reader draw the obvious inference. Perhaps she trusted that the measurements would outlast the men who doubted them.

In a sense, they have. Foote's cylinders were recovered from the archive more than a century and a half later, and her two brief pages now read like a message sealed in a bottle and set adrift. The rain she could not have predicted still falls on a warming world—but the small, patient experiment that first named the cause bears, at last, her name.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Nineteenth-century science relied on instruments too crude to produce reliable results.
A largely overlooked researcher made an important discovery whose credit went elsewhere for over a century.
John Tyndall deliberately concealed the work of a rival in order to claim a discovery.
The greenhouse effect was fully understood by scientists long before the modern era.
Women in the 1850s were forbidden from conducting any form of scientific experiment.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Foote's neglected discovery and its late recognition, making (B) correct. (C) fails because the text explicitly says Tyndall likely never knew of her work; (E) overstates, since Foote herself experimented.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, Foote's experiment involved which of the following?
choices: Heating metal plates over an open flame
Comparing thermometers placed in cylinders of different gases in sunlight
Measuring rainfall across several seasons
Mixing carbon dioxide with hydrogen inside a sealed furnace
Recording the temperature of ocean water at varying depths
questions.explanation: The passage states she filled cylinders with different gases, set them in sunlight, and watched her thermometers, so (B) is correct. (C) confuses the metaphorical title about rain with her actual method; the other choices name apparatus never mentioned.

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that Foote's discovery was overlooked primarily because
choices: her measurements were later found to be inaccurate
she never wrote her findings down in any permanent form
the assumptions of her era did not expect women to offer scientific explanations
Tyndall's instruments disproved her original conclusion
she chose to keep her work secret from other scientists
questions.explanation: The text blames "the quiet machinery of an age that expected women to observe the weather, not to explain it," supporting (C). (A) and (D) contradict the passage, which credits her measurements; (B) fails because her two-page paper survived.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "modest" most nearly means
choices: shy about attention
unassuming in scale
financially poor
carefully hidden
morally proper
questions.explanation: "Modest observation" describes the small, simple experiment, so "unassuming in scale" (B) fits. (A) and (E) reflect other common senses of "modest" that do not match a description of an observation's size, the intended meaning here.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward Eunice Foote can best be described as
choices: skeptical and dismissive
admiring and quietly indignant
detached and purely factual
nostalgic and sentimental
amused and ironic
questions.explanation: The author praises Foote's insight while noting the injustice of her erasure, supporting (B). (C) fails because the writer clearly editorializes; (A) is contradicted by the respectful portrayal, and (E) mistakes the somber closing for amusement.

questions.prompt: The passage mentions all of the following EXCEPT
choices: that a man read Foote's paper aloud in her place
that Tyndall used more elaborate instruments than Foote
that Foote's paper contained no complaint about her treatment
that Foote's cylinders were rediscovered in an archive
that Foote publicly protested being denied credit for her work
questions.explanation: The passage stresses that Foote's paper made "no complaint" and that she seemed to accept the arrangement calmly, so (E) is never stated and is the exception. Choices (A)-(D) each appear directly in the text.

passages.title: The Long Bench
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
Every few years, someone on the town council proposes removing the benches from Cutler Square. They are old, the argument goes; the paint blisters, the slats warp, and loiterers gather where commerce might flourish instead. Replace them with planters, the reformers say, and the square will finally look prosperous.

I have sat on those benches, and I am not persuaded.

Consider who uses them. The retired machinist who reads his paper each morning. The mother waiting for the school bus with a toddler grown heavy in her arms. The teenager who has, for reasons of his own, nowhere quieter to think. None of them is buying anything. That is precisely the point. A bench is one of the few places left in a town where a person may simply exist without first producing a receipt.

We are told this is inefficient. Perhaps. But a town is not a business, and its worth cannot be tallied in the register. The measure of a place is whether a stranger, arriving tired, can find somewhere to rest that costs nothing and asks nothing. Remove the benches, and you have not removed a nuisance; you have quietly announced that only paying visitors are welcome.

The reformers imagine the planters will make Cutler Square inviting. I suspect the opposite. Flowers behind a low iron rail invite admiration but not company; they are meant to be looked at, not lived with. The bench, scarred and unglamorous, invites something rarer — it invites you to stay.

So keep the benches. Sand them if you must; repaint the blistered arms. But do not mistake the cost of maintaining them for their price. What we spend on a bench, we spend on the idea that the square belongs to everyone, including those with empty pockets and an hour to fill.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: explaining the proper way to repair aging public furniture
arguing that the town's benches should be kept as spaces open to everyone
describing the daily routines of the people who visit Cutler Square
proving that planters would cost the town more than benches do
criticizing the town council's spending habits in general
questions.explanation: The essay defends the benches as places open to all, paying or not. B captures this. A (sanding, repainting) and C (the list of users) are supporting details, not the point; D is never argued; E is broader than the passage's single issue.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, which of the following is named as a person who uses the benches?
choices: a retired machinist
a shopkeeper
a town councilman
a police officer
a tourist with a camera
questions.explanation: The retired machinist reading his paper is listed among the benches' users. The councilman appears only as someone proposing removal, not as a user; the shopkeeper, officer, and tourist never appear at all.

questions.prompt: The author would most likely oppose which of the following?
choices: repainting the benches' blistered arms
adding a public park with free seating
converting the square into a space meant only for paying customers
allowing a teenager to sit and think in the square
letting a mother wait for the school bus there
questions.explanation: The author warns that removing benches 'announces that only paying visitors are welcome,' so C is what he opposes. A he explicitly endorses; B fits his values; D and E describe the very users he defends, not things he'd resist.

questions.prompt: As used in the phrase "cannot be tallied in the register," the word "register" most nearly means
choices: an official list of eligible voters
a machine that records money and sales
a range of musical pitch
a formal level of speech or writing
a vent that controls heat
questions.explanation: The clause contrasts a town with 'a business' whose worth is 'tallied,' pointing to the money-recording sense, B. A, C, D, and E are all genuine meanings of 'register' but ignore the commercial contrast the sentence sets up.

questions.prompt: The author's attitude toward the reformers' proposal can best be described as
choices: enthusiastically supportive
reasonably skeptical
violently outraged
completely indifferent
anxious and fearful
questions.explanation: He calmly answers 'I am not persuaded' and 'I suspect the opposite,' measured doubt, so B. A reverses his view; C overstates his composed tone; D contradicts his clear engagement; E imports an alarm the essay never expresses.

questions.prompt: The author mentions all of the following as people who use the benches EXCEPT
choices: a retired machinist reading his paper
a mother waiting for the school bus
a teenager with nowhere quieter to think
a tired stranger looking for somewhere to rest
a merchant counting his receipts
questions.explanation: The merchant belongs to the commerce side the author critiques, never a bench-user. The machinist, mother, and teenager are named directly, and the tired stranger is offered as someone the benches serve, making E the exception.

passages.title: The Weight of the Ladle
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
Every August my grandmother made pear preserves, and every August she let me believe I was helping. I was seven, then eight, then nine, and my job never changed: I held the ladle. It was heavier than it looked, blackened at the bowl, worn smooth on the handle by hands that were not mine. She stood at the stove stirring a pot that breathed sweetness into the whole kitchen, a smell so thick I felt I could lean against it.

I thought the ladle was the important thing. I gripped it with both fists and waited to be told to dip, to pour, to matter. But she rarely asked for it. Mostly I watched her wrists, how they knew the exact moment the fruit softened, how she skimmed the foam without seeming to look. When the jars were finally filled and turned upside down to seal, she would take the ladle back gently, as if returning a borrowed thing, and set it in the sink.

I understand now that the ladle was never the point. She had given me a small, safe corner of the work so I would stand beside her long enough to learn the rest without knowing I was learning it. The recipe was never written down. It lived in her wrists and in the particular hush she kept while she worked, and she was handing it to me one silent August at a time.

She is gone, and last summer I made the preserves myself. My wrists did not know the moment; I scorched the first batch and cried over a pot like a child. But the second batch sealed. I turned the jars upside down on the counter and felt, absurdly, that someone had just taken the ladle from my hands and set it quietly in the sink.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A grandmother's pear preserves were famous throughout the family.
Learning to cook requires written recipes and careful measurement.
A child unknowingly inherited a skill and a bond through years of quiet presence.
Childhood summers are happier than the responsibilities of adulthood.
The narrator regrets never being allowed to do real work in the kitchen.
questions.explanation: The passage traces how standing beside the grandmother transmitted both skill and love. (C) captures this arc. (E) inverts the meaning: the narrator ultimately values that quiet apprenticeship rather than regretting it, and (A) fixates on a minor detail.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what was the narrator's actual job during the preserve-making?
choices: To stir the pot until the fruit softened
To skim the foam from the surface
To hold the ladle
To write down the recipe
To fill and seal the jars
questions.explanation: The text states plainly, "my job never changed: I held the ladle." The grandmother did the stirring, skimming, and sealing (A, B, E), and the recipe was never written down (D), making those the trap choices drawn from nearby lines.

questions.prompt: The narrator's remark that she "let me believe I was helping" most strongly implies that the grandmother
choices: resented the child's presence in her kitchen
deliberately created a role to keep the child close and teaching
was too disorganized to assign a real task
distrusted the child with anything breakable
preferred to make the preserves entirely alone
questions.explanation: Later lines confirm she gave "a small, safe corner of the work" so the child would "learn the rest without knowing." (B) fits. (E) contradicts her intent to keep the child near, and (A) and (D) misread her tenderness as rejection.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "hush" (in "the particular hush she kept") most nearly means
choices: a stern command for silence
a quiet, attentive stillness
a secret kept from the family
a sudden loss of sound
an embarrassed pause
questions.explanation: The hush is the concentrated calm in which the grandmother works and teaches, so (B) fits. (A) treats hush as an order rather than a quality, and (C) confuses it with the unwritten recipe mentioned separately.

questions.prompt: The narrator's attitude toward her grandmother is best described as
choices: reverent and grateful
amused and detached
resentful and wounded
curious but indifferent
admiring yet fearful
questions.explanation: The tender recollection and the closing sense of inherited presence signal reverence and gratitude, so (A) is best. (C) contradicts the loving tone, and (E) adds a fear the passage never supports despite the grandmother's quiet authority.

questions.prompt: The final paragraph, in which the narrator makes the preserves alone, serves primarily to
choices: prove the narrator had memorized the exact recipe
show the narrator abandoning her grandmother's methods
demonstrate that the inheritance succeeded despite loss and imperfection
criticize the narrator's lack of skill in the kitchen
suggest the narrator will never cook again after failing
questions.explanation: The scorched first batch and sealed second batch show the skill and bond survived the grandmother's death, so (C) fits. (A) fails because the recipe lived in wrists, not memory, and (E) ignores the successful second batch and comforting close.

passages.title: The Mending
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
The Mending

1   Each winter she unspooled the amber thread
2   and drew the low lamp close against the dark,
3   her patient needle nosing through the bread
4   of coats worn thin, each careful stitch a spark.
5   I hid those coats behind my back at school;
6   I wanted store-bought seams, the flawless kind
7   that never puckered, never broke the rule,
8   that left no small repaired map-lines behind—
9   those raised white rivers running down a sleeve,
10  the proof we made poor, old things last too long.
11  Now she is gone. I do not merely grieve
12  the way I used to; grief has changed its song.
13  Up in the attic chest I find the coat,
14  its shoulder split, its worn lining frayed thin,
15  and something tightens, wordless, in my throat
16  to see how well the flaw was folded in
17  to hold. I thread the needle. My slow hands
18  begin the amber knot. The lamp. The mending.

questions.prompt: The poem is primarily concerned with
choices: the difficulty of learning to sew by hand
how the speaker's feelings about the mother's mending change over time
the poverty that forced a family to repair its old clothing
a daughter's decision to take up her mother's trade
the loneliness of cleaning out a deceased parent's attic
questions.explanation: The poem moves from the speaker hiding the coats to reverently continuing the mending, so B captures the central emotional shift. C names a real detail (line 10) but not the poem's focus; E treats the attic, merely the setting of the turn, as the whole point.

questions.prompt: According to the poem, the speaker hid the coats at school because
choices: they were too torn to be worn in public
the speaker wanted flawless, store-bought seams
the mother had asked that they be kept secret
classmates had tried to take them before
they no longer fit the growing speaker
questions.explanation: Lines 5-6 state the speaker wanted 'store-bought seams, the flawless kind,' making B a direct paraphrase. A misreads the coats as unwearable when the point is their visible repairs; C invents an instruction the mother never gives.

questions.prompt: It can most reasonably be inferred that the 'raised white rivers' (line 9) are
choices: actual streams near the family's home
tears running down the speaker's cheeks
the visible ridges left by the mother's repairs
wrinkles in the mother's aging hands
loose threads the speaker later discards
questions.explanation: The 'rivers' run 'down a sleeve' and parallel the 'repaired map-lines' of line 8, so C fits the mended ridges. B is tempting because a throat tightens later, but these marks sit on the coat, not the face; A reads the metaphor literally.

questions.prompt: In line 7, 'puckered' most nearly means
choices: kissed
soured
gathered into wrinkles
glowed brightly
came apart
questions.explanation: Describing seams the speaker dislikes, 'puckered' means drawn into small wrinkles, so C is correct. A and B rely on unrelated meanings of 'pucker' (lips, a sour taste); E confuses puckering with tearing, but a puckered seam is bunched, not broken.

questions.prompt: The speaker's attitude toward the mending shifts from
choices: curiosity to indifference
embarrassment to reverence
anger to forgiveness
fear to relief
pride to disappointment
questions.explanation: The speaker first hides the coats in shame, then takes up the needle with quiet awe, so B tracks the turn. C overstates the early feeling as anger and implies the mother was wronged; E reverses the actual direction of the speaker's emotion.

questions.prompt: The poem employs all of the following EXCEPT
choices: a metaphor likening worn coats to bread
imagery of a lamp and its light
direct spoken dialogue between mother and daughter
a turn signaled by 'Now she is gone'
repetition of the word 'never' in a single line
questions.explanation: No one speaks aloud in the poem, so C is the missing device. A appears in lines 3-4 ('the bread / of coats'), B in lines 2 and 18, D at line 11, and E in line 7 ('never puckered, never broke'), leaving dialogue as the sole absent technique.

passages.title: Windfall
passages.attribution: original passage
passages.text:
All August the orchard hoarded its green,
each apple a small fist that would not unclench,
and I mistook that clenching for a promise—
that whatever holds on tightly means to stay.
I walked the rows and counted, counted, counted,
as if the arithmetic could bank the summer,
as if a number were a kind of fence
that time would be too courteous to cross.
Then the first cold came and nudged the boughs,
and the fruit let go of everything, at once,
thudding the tall grass like a slow applause
for no one in particular—for the going itself.
Now I gather what the patient wind has felled,
the bruised, the split, the sweetening-toward-rot,
and cradle each one warm against my coat
the way you'd carry something that still mattered.
The tree stands emptied and does not seem to mourn.
It was never miserly. It was only teaching me to fall.

questions.prompt: The poem primarily uses the orchard and its falling apples to explore
choices: the practical difficulty of harvesting fruit before winter
the speaker's gradual movement toward accepting loss and mortality
a child's fond memory of one particular tree
the cruelty of winter toward all living things
the importance of keeping an accurate count
questions.explanation: The apples' letting go and the closing lesson "to fall" make acceptance of ending the poem's real subject. (D) is refuted by the turn, which calls the tree "patient," not cruel; (A) and (E) mistake literal details for the central meaning.

questions.prompt: According to the poem, what causes the apples to fall from the tree?
choices: The speaker deliberately shakes the boughs.
The apples grow too heavy with autumn rain.
The first cold comes and nudges the branches.
Birds land on and disturb the branches.
The tree drops them on purpose out of anger.
questions.explanation: The text states "the first cold came and nudged the boughs, / and the fruit let go." (E) contradicts the poem, which insists the tree does not mourn and is not miserly; (A) reverses roles—the speaker only gathers the fallen fruit.

questions.prompt: The speaker's repeated counting in lines 5-8 most strongly suggests that he once
choices: practiced arithmetic as a favorite pastime
believed he could somehow hold off or manage the passing of time
was hired to sell the orchard's fruit at market
distrusted the person who owned the orchard
worried he would forget how many trees there were
questions.explanation: Counting "as if the arithmetic could bank the summer" and build "a fence / that time would be too courteous to cross" reveals a wish to control time. (A) takes the counting literally as a hobby, ignoring the figurative purpose the similes make explicit.

questions.prompt: In the final line, "miserly" most nearly means
choices: forgetful
cruel
stingy
elderly
generous
questions.explanation: The tree was "never miserly"—never withholding—but "only patient," so miserly means stingy or hoarding. (E) is its opposite; (B) borrows the earlier emotional register but names the wrong quality, since the point is the tree did not selfishly cling.

questions.prompt: The speaker's attitude in the closing lines is best described as
choices: bitter and resentful
anxious and panicked
calm and accepting
cheerful and triumphant
indifferent and detached
questions.explanation: Cradling the fruit "the way you'd carry something that still mattered" and reframing the fall as a lesson conveys settled acceptance. (E) fails because such tender cradling is not indifference; (A) is contradicted by the gentle, unresentful view of the unmourning tree.

questions.prompt: The image of the fallen apples as "a slow applause / for no one in particular—for the going itself" chiefly serves to
choices: suggest that departure and ending can be met with a kind of dignity or celebration
scold readers for failing to notice the natural world
emphasize how noisy the orchard grows in autumn
reveal the speaker's fear of sudden loud sounds
prove that the fallen apples remain good to eat
questions.explanation: Casting the thud of loss as "applause" for "the going itself" dignifies ending, previewing the poem's acceptance. (C) literalizes "applause" as mere volume, missing the metaphor; (E) fixes on the unrelated "sweetening-toward-rot" detail rather than this image's purpose.

passages.title: The King of the Sun-Kissed Valley
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Call of the Wild by Jack London — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening: Buck's comfortable life on a California ranch ends when he is stolen and brutally broken in by a man with a club.
passages.text:
In the wide, warm reaches of the Santa Clara Valley, on the sprawling estate of Judge Miller, there lived a dog who wanted for nothing. Buck was enormous, a hundred and forty pounds of muscle inherited from a St. Bernard father and a Scotch-shepherd mother, and he ruled his sunlit domain with the easy confidence of a monarch who has never been challenged. He swam in the great cement pool, trotted beside the Judge's grandsons, and slept wherever he pleased. He did not know that far to the north, men had clawed yellow gold from the frozen earth, and that those men now hungered for strong, thick-coated dogs to drag their loads across the snow.

Buck did not know, either, that Manuel, one of the gardener's helpers, gambled and lost and owed money he could not pay. One quiet evening Manuel led Buck away with a friendly hand, and a rope changed everything. The rope tightened. Strangers threw Buck into the dark belly of a baggage car, and for two rattling days and nights he traveled without food or water, his fury swelling with every mile.

When at last the crate was pried open, a thick-set man in a red sweater waited with a club. Buck charged, and the club met him. Again he charged, and again the wood crashed down, until his body ached and his rage curdled into something colder. He understood, dimly but forever, a truth that would guide the rest of his days: a man holding a club was a ruler he had no choice but to obey. It was his first lesson in a merciless new world, and he would not forget it.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Buck's pampered life is destroyed when he is stolen and forced to learn brutal new rules.
Judge Miller mistreats his dogs and deserves to lose Buck.
The Klondike gold rush made many men wealthy overnight.
Manuel is a loyal servant who protects Buck from harm.
Buck's great size makes him the most valuable sled dog in the entire north.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from Buck's carefree life on the estate to his theft and violent 'first lesson,' showing how his comfortable world is destroyed and replaced by harsh rules.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does Manuel betray Buck?
choices: He is jealous of Buck's size and strength.
He owes gambling debts he cannot repay.
Judge Miller has ordered him to sell the dog.
He wants Buck to have a better life in the north.
He resents that Judge Miller favors his grandsons over the household servants.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Manuel 'gambled and lost and owed money he could not pay,' which is the reason he leads Buck away.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Buck's beating by the man in the red sweater teaches him to
choices: trust every human who offers him food
fear water and refuse to swim again
submit to any person who holds a weapon
escape from crates whenever possible
obey only the particular man who wore the red sweater
questions.explanation: After the clubbing, Buck understands 'forever' that a man holding a club 'was a ruler he had no choice but to obey,' implying he has learned to submit to armed people.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "curdled" most nearly means
choices: evaporated completely
turned or hardened into something else
grew warmer and gentler
was rewarded richly
spilled out and was wasted
questions.explanation: The text says his 'rage curdled into something colder,' meaning his hot fury transformed or hardened into a cold, controlled state.

passages.title: The Law of Club and Fang
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Call of the Wild by Jack London — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action: as a Yukon sled dog, Buck witnesses savage violence, grows cunning, and burns to destroy the tyrant lead dog, Spitz.
passages.text:
The north was a white silence broken by the crunch of runners and the crack of a whip. Sold to two French-Canadian mail carriers, Perrault and Francois, Buck was harnessed into a team and learned the trade of the trace. His masters were hard but fair, and Buck respected them, but the land itself taught the fiercest lessons of all.

He learned the second great law on the day the huskies swarmed. Curly, a good-natured dog who had made the mistake of being friendly, went down beneath the pack, and in seconds she was torn apart upon the snow. Buck watched, and something ancient stirred in him. Here there was no fair play. A dog that fell was a dog that died. He would never go down.

So Buck changed. He grew lean and hard and clever. He learned to steal food when the drivers slept, to sleep with his nose tucked beneath his tail against the killing frost, to read the thin ice and the coming storm. Muscles once soft with valley living turned to iron.

And he came to hate Spitz, the lead dog, a cold and cunning animal who ruled the team through terror. Between the two great dogs a rivalry smoldered, flaring in stolen snarls and challenged commands, until it could end only one way. Beneath a wide, frozen moon the pack gathered in a silent ring, and Buck and Spitz threw themselves at each other. It was a long, wearing battle, but Buck was patient and shrewd. He broke first one of Spitz's legs, then the other, and when the beaten champion could no longer stand, the waiting circle closed in. Buck stood alone in the moonlight, the victor, the new leader of the team.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about how Buck
choices: earns the friendship of Curly and the other huskies
hardens into a cunning fighter and defeats his rival for leadership
teaches Perrault and Francois how to drive a sled team
escapes from the Yukon to return home to California
survives the north only by stealing food from Perrault and Francois
questions.explanation: The passage traces Buck's transformation into a lean, clever survivor and climaxes with his defeat of Spitz to become the team's leader.

questions.prompt: What lesson does Buck take from the death of Curly?
choices: That his masters cannot be trusted
That a dog which falls in a fight will be killed
That stealing food is the only way to survive
That Spitz must be obeyed at all costs
That the wild huskies will always outnumber the harnessed sled dogs
questions.explanation: After watching Curly torn apart, Buck grasps that 'a dog that fell was a dog that died,' and resolves he will never go down.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Buck defeats Spitz mainly because he
choices: is larger and heavier than any other dog
fights with patience and cleverness rather than brute rage
has the help of Perrault and Francois
catches Spitz completely by surprise
waits until the killing frost has weakened Spitz before attacking
questions.explanation: The passage notes Buck 'was patient and shrewd' and methodically broke Spitz's legs, implying strategy, not size or surprise, wins the fight.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "smoldered" most nearly means
choices: burned openly and loudly
cooled and disappeared
burned quietly beneath the surface
froze solid in the cold
erupted suddenly and all at once
questions.explanation: The rivalry 'smoldered, flaring in stolen snarls,' describing a hostility that burned quietly and only occasionally flared into the open.

passages.title: The Man Who Cut the Traces
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Call of the Wild by Jack London — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The turning point: reckless owners nearly work Buck to death until John Thornton rescues him, and the doomed team meets its end on the ice.
passages.text:
After his days of leading the mail team, Buck passed through careless hands until he came to own three of the most foolish people the trail had ever seen. Hal and Charles were brothers-in-law, and Mercedes was their sister, and none of them knew the first thing about the north. They piled the sled with useless goods, tent and dishes and far too many clothes, until it towered absurdly high. They fed the dogs too much, then too little, and when the animals sickened and starved, the three quarreled among themselves and blamed everyone but their own ignorance.

By the time they staggered into John Thornton's camp, the team was a ruin of skin and bone. The dogs could barely stand. Yet Hal ordered them on, and when Buck, at the end of all his strength, refused to rise, Hal seized his club and beat him where he lay in the snow. Blow after blow fell, but Buck would not move. Something in him had finally said no.

Then a man stepped forward. John Thornton, who had watched in growing anger, knocked the club away and stood between Buck and his tormentor. He told Hal plainly that he would not let him kill the dog, and he cut Buck loose from the harness. Cursing, Hal drove the rest of the team out onto the frozen river. Buck and Thornton watched them go, small figures crawling across the white. Then a whole section of ice gave way. A hole yawned open, and sled, dogs, and people vanished into the black water. The trail was silent again, and Buck had been spared.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Buck learns to pull heavier loads than any other dog.
John Thornton saves Buck from cruel owners who soon perish on the ice.
Mercedes proves to be the wisest of the three travelers.
Buck decides to lead the mail team once again.
Hal and Charles learn from their mistakes and master the trail.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the foolish owners' cruelty, Thornton's rescue of Buck, and the drowning of the team, making the rescue and its aftermath the main idea.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what causes the deaths of Hal, Charles, and Mercedes?
