choices: Buck attacks them in revenge
They freeze during a sudden storm
The ice breaks beneath their sled on the river
Thornton drives them away at gunpoint
They starve after giving the last of their food to the dogs
questions.explanation: The passage states that as the team crossed the frozen river, 'a whole section of ice gave way' and 'sled, dogs, and people vanished into the black water.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Buck refuses to rise when beaten because he
choices: senses the ice ahead is deadly and is trying to save the team
no longer fears Hal's club at all
has reached the absolute limit of his strength and will
is waiting for a command from Thornton
has grown too stubborn to obey any master ever again
questions.explanation: The passage says Buck was 'at the end of all his strength' when he 'refused to rise,' and that 'something in him had finally said no,' indicating he had reached the limit of his strength and will and would go no farther. The passage never states that Buck knows about the ice ahead.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "ruin" most nearly means
choices: a destroyed, wasted condition
a valuable prize
a heavy load of cargo
a place of shelter
a hardened, iron-tough condition
questions.explanation: The team is called 'a ruin of skin and bone,' describing its wrecked, wasted physical state from starvation and abuse.

passages.title: The Sound Beyond the Firelight
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Call of the Wild by Jack London — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: Buck loves John Thornton yet is drawn to the wild, and when Thornton is killed, Buck severs his last bond with humanity forever.
passages.text:
With John Thornton, Buck knew love for the first time in his life. Thornton had saved him not out of duty but out of kindness, and Buck repaid it with a devotion that frightened even him. He would have died for the man gladly. Once, on a boastful wager, Thornton bet that Buck could break a sled loaded with a thousand pounds out of the frozen ground and haul it a hundred yards. The onlookers laughed, but Buck leaned into the harness, every muscle trembling, and inch by impossible inch the runners tore free. He won his master a small fortune and won for himself a fame that spread along the rivers.

Yet even love could not silence the other thing. Deep in the forest, a voice was calling, older than fire, older than men. At night Buck would slip away among the trees and answer the howl of the wild brothers who ran there, and each time it grew harder to come back. He was a creature of two worlds, and one of them was losing its hold.

Then came the day the Yeehats found the camp. When Buck returned from the woods, Thornton lay dead among the ruins, killed by the arrows of the tribe. A terrible grief broke loose in Buck, and behind it a fury like a river of fire. He fell upon the Yeehats and scattered them in terror, killing without fear, for the last chain binding him to mankind had shattered. His master was gone. Nothing human held him now. Buck lifted his head, listened to the call he had resisted so long, and ran at last into the forest to join the wolves. In time the tribes would whisper of a Ghost Dog that haunted the wild, and it was Buck, home at last.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about how Buck
choices: wins a famous bet for John Thornton and grows rich
finally leaves the human world behind to join the wild
learns to fear the Yeehat tribe and their arrows
forgets his old life on Judge Miller's ranch
grieves for Thornton but chooses to stay near the ruined camp
questions.explanation: Though it mentions the wager, the passage builds to Thornton's death and Buck's decision to answer the wild's call and run with the wolves, its central movement.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does John Thornton die?
choices: He drowns when the ice breaks beneath him
He is killed by the arrows of the Yeehats
He is attacked by a pack of wolves
He falls ill during the long winter
He is killed defending Buck from a pack of wolves
questions.explanation: The passage states that Buck found Thornton 'dead among the ruins, killed by the arrows of the tribe,' the Yeehats.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Thornton's death
choices: makes Buck decide to find a new human master
removes the last reason Buck had to stay among people
causes Buck to fear the wilderness even more
teaches Buck to distrust all other dogs
makes Buck vow to take revenge on every human tribe
questions.explanation: The text says 'the last chain binding him to mankind had shattered' and 'nothing human held him now,' implying Thornton was Buck's final tie to people.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "devotion" most nearly means
choices: deep loyalty and love
reluctant obedience
quiet suspicion
fierce jealousy
cautious respect
questions.explanation: Buck repays Thornton's kindness with 'a devotion... He would have died for the man gladly,' showing devotion means deep loyalty and love.

passages.title: The Stranger in the Newsroom
passages.attribution: Retelling of When I Knew Stephen Crane by Willa Cather — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening section, in which the young narrator first encounters the famous but unimpressive writer stranded in her town.
passages.text:
I was still a university student in Lincoln when I first laid eyes on him, and I remember thinking, before I knew his name, that he looked like a man who had been ill for a long time. I had come into the newspaper office where I did occasional work, expecting the usual clutter and no company, and instead I found a stranger already seated there. He was thin to the point of gauntness, pale, and dressed in clothes that had plainly seen better seasons. His fingers were stained a deep brown from the cigarettes he lit one after another, scarcely finishing one before he reached for the next.

Someone told me who he was, and the name startled me. This shabby, worn-looking figure was Stephen Crane, whose book everyone I knew had been talking about. He was traveling west as a correspondent, dispatched by a newspaper syndicate to send back reports, and Lincoln was only meant to be a stop along the way. But he had been detained. A payment he was owed had not yet come from the East, and until it did he was stranded among us with empty pockets and nothing to occupy him.

He was not friendly. When I ventured a few words to him he answered coldly, almost with irritation, as though conversation were an intrusion he could not afford. He struck me as a man wound too tightly, restless, prematurely aged by some private strain I could not name. I confess I was a little afraid of him, and a little dazzled too, for I had never before stood so near to someone who had actually done the thing I dreamed of doing. I did not guess, that first afternoon, how much I would come to learn from this unpromising stranger before his money finally arrived.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of this passage?
choices: The narrator explains why she disliked working at the newspaper office
The narrator recalls her first, surprising encounter with the shabby stranger who was the famous Stephen Crane
The narrator describes the city of Lincoln and its newspaper industry
The narrator argues that famous writers are usually disappointing in person
The narrator explains how Stephen Crane's book became famous across the country
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the narrator's first meeting with Crane, contrasting his unimpressive appearance with his fame; it opens with 'when I first laid eyes on him' and builds to recognizing him as Stephen Crane.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why was Crane stranded in Lincoln?
choices: He had fallen ill and could not travel
A payment he was owed had not yet arrived from the East
He was waiting to interview the narrator
His newspaper syndicate had ordered him to stay
He had spent all his money and could not afford to continue west
questions.explanation: The passage states he had been detained because 'a payment he was owed had not yet come from the East,' leaving him stranded with empty pockets.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the narrator felt which of the following toward Crane at their first meeting?
choices: Complete indifference
A mixture of intimidation and admiration
Open hostility and resentment
Pity mingled with contempt
Eager curiosity untouched by any real fear
questions.explanation: She admits she was 'a little afraid of him, and a little dazzled too,' since he had done the very thing she dreamed of doing—signaling both intimidation and admiration.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'gauntness' most nearly means
choices: extreme thinness
great height
nervous energy
poor eyesight
sickly paleness
questions.explanation: Crane is described as 'thin to the point of gauntness,' and elsewhere as pale and worn, so 'gauntness' refers to extreme thinness.

passages.title: The Reserve Wears Away
passages.attribution: Retelling of When I Knew Stephen Crane by Willa Cather — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action, in which repeated meetings soften Crane's coldness and he begins to speak openly about the writer's life.
passages.text:
Because the delayed money kept him idle in town for several days, I found that I could not avoid him, nor, it turned out, did I wish to. We crossed paths again and again—in the office, on the street, in the dull hours when there was nothing for either of us to do—and slowly the frost in his manner began to thaw. The man who had answered me so curtly at first grew, by degrees, willing to talk, and once he began he spoke as though he had been holding the words back a long while.

What he chose to talk about was his work, and I listened greedily, for it was the very subject that consumed me. He told me he wrote slowly and with pain, that the sentences did not come easily but had to be dragged out of him one by one. Above everything he cared for the truth of small things—the exact look of a face, the precise turn of a gesture—and he would labor over such details as though the whole worth of a page depended on them. Yet he confessed, with a bluntness that surprised me, that he was never certain his writing was any good. He seemed genuinely not to know whether what he made had value, and this uncertainty appeared to gnaw at him.

What galled him most was time, or the lack of it. Poverty forced him into hurried newspaper work, the kind of writing that pays quickly and means little, and every hour spent that way was an hour stolen from the real art he longed to make. He spoke of this with a low, steady bitterness. I began to see that beneath his prickly surface was not arrogance at all but a fierce, anxious devotion—the temper of a man who wanted only to do his work and was forever being kept from it.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about
choices: how the narrator taught Crane to write more quickly
how, over several days, Crane opened up and revealed his views on writing
the kinds of newspaper stories Crane preferred to write
a quarrel between the narrator and Crane over money
how poverty forced Crane to give up the real art he longed to make
questions.explanation: The passage traces how repeated meetings thawed Crane's reserve until he spoke freely about his work, his methods, and his frustrations.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did Crane care about above everything in his writing?
choices: Writing as many pages as possible each day
The truth of small details, like the exact look of a face
Earning praise from other famous writers
Finishing his newspaper assignments on time
Working slowly and painfully over every sentence
questions.explanation: The passage states he 'cared for the truth of small things—the exact look of a face, the precise turn of a gesture—and he would labor over such details.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the narrator came to believe Crane's initial coldness was caused by
choices: genuine dislike of the narrator
a devoted, anxious seriousness about his art rather than arrogance
his embarrassment about being poor
a natural shyness around strangers
his bitterness at being forced into hurried newspaper work
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes that 'beneath his prickly surface was not arrogance at all but a fierce, anxious devotion' to his work.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'galled' most nearly means
choices: confused
irritated or distressed
amused
encouraged
wearied or exhausted
questions.explanation: 'What galled him most was time'—followed by his 'low, steady bitterness' about stolen hours—shows that 'galled' means irritated or distressed.

passages.title: The Gloomy Confession
passages.attribution: Retelling of When I Knew Stephen Crane by Willa Cather — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The emotional climax, in which Crane confesses during a late, somber conversation that he does not expect to live long.
passages.text:
There was one conversation I have never forgotten, and it came late, when the day had gone gray and heavy and the ordinary cheer had drained out of everything. We were talking, as we so often did, of writing and of the years ahead, when his voice dropped and changed, and he said a thing that chilled me. He told me, quite simply and without any wish to be pitied, that he did not believe he would live very long.

He said it not as a fear but as a settled fact, the way a man might mention the weather. There was so much he still meant to do, he told me—so many things he carried in his mind that he had not yet found the strength or the time to set down—and he was certain, with a calm that was worse than any panic, that he would never reach the half of them. The work would outlast the life; the years would run short before the labor was done. He spoke of it wearily, as though the knowledge had lived with him a long while and he had grown used to its company.

I was young, and I did not fully grasp what I was hearing. To me such talk seemed like the passing gloom of a tired man on a dark afternoon, and I half expected him to shake it off. I could not imagine, then, that a person so alive, so restless with plans, could be right about his own ending. Yet something in the quiet certainty of his voice stayed with me and would not leave. He was not performing despair; he was telling me a truth he had already made his peace with, and I sat there unable to answer him.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of this passage?
choices: Crane cheerfully described his plans for future books
During a somber late talk, Crane calmly confessed his conviction that he would die young
The narrator persuaded Crane to rest and recover his health
Crane and the narrator argued about the meaning of art
The narrator comforted Crane through a dark and gloomy afternoon
questions.explanation: The passage builds around the single grave conversation in which Crane states, calmly, that he does not believe he will live long.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how did Crane deliver the news that he would not live long?
choices: With panic and open fear
Simply and calmly, as though stating a settled fact
Angrily, blaming his poverty
Jokingly, expecting to be contradicted
Wearily, as a long and painful complaint about his life
questions.explanation: The passage says he spoke 'quite simply and without any wish to be pitied' and 'as a settled fact, the way a man might mention the weather.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that, at the time of this conversation, the narrator
choices: fully understood and accepted Crane's prediction
did not truly grasp the weight of what Crane was telling her
believed Crane was lying to gain sympathy
was frightened into leaving the room
immediately sensed that Crane's prediction would prove true
questions.explanation: She admits, 'I was young, and I did not fully grasp what I was hearing,' expecting him to shake off the mood—showing she did not yet grasp its weight.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'settled' most nearly means
choices: comfortable
firmly fixed or certain
recently paid
peaceful and quiet
long-standing and familiar
questions.explanation: Crane states his death 'not as a fear but as a settled fact,' with 'quiet certainty'—so 'settled' means firmly fixed or certain.

passages.title: Understanding, Years Later
passages.attribution: Retelling of When I Knew Stephen Crane by Willa Cather — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution, in which Crane departs and the narrator, looking back after his death, finally understands the meaning of his weary words.
passages.text:
At last the money came. The draft he had been waiting for arrived from the East, and with it the reason for his lingering vanished. He gathered his few things and went on his way westward, back to the road and the assignments that awaited him, and I watched him go without any sense that I was witnessing anything I would need to remember. He was simply a traveler moving on, and I was a student with my own days to fill. We did not say any grand farewells. He left as unremarkably as he had appeared.

I am writing this now from a great distance of years, and everything looks different from here. Stephen Crane did die young, just as he had told me he would, on that gray afternoon when I was too green to believe him. And in dying he became more famous than ever, his name spoken now with a reverence that would have astonished the shabby, restless man I knew. The world remembers the writer; I remember the person, and the two do not always sit easily together in my mind.

Only now do I understand what he was really saying to me. At the time his weariness seemed like a mood; today I know it was foresight. The bitterness about stolen time, the calm certainty about his own short future—these were not the complaints of a tired man but the clear sight of one who knew exactly what he was racing against. I had stood close to something rare and had not known it. It has taken me all these years to hear, at last, the full meaning of words I once let pass as merely gloomy.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: The narrator regrets never saying goodbye to Crane properly
Looking back after Crane's early death, the narrator finally understands the meaning of his earlier words
The narrator explains how Crane became rich and famous
The narrator describes her own career as a student writer
The narrator mourns that she and Crane never met again after he left Lincoln
questions.explanation: The passage moves from Crane's departure to the narrator's present-day realization that she now understands what his weary words truly meant.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what finally allowed Crane to leave Lincoln?
choices: He recovered from an illness
The draft he had been waiting for arrived from the East
The narrator lent him money
His newspaper called him back home
He completed the assignment that had detained him in town
questions.explanation: The passage opens, 'At last the money came. The draft he had been waiting for arrived from the East,' removing the reason for his stay.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the narrator now regards her time with Crane as
choices: a rare experience whose value she failed to recognize at the time
an unpleasant memory she would rather forget
proof that famous people are not worth meeting
the beginning of a lasting friendship between them
a painful reminder of how ignorant she had been in her youth
questions.explanation: She writes, 'I had stood close to something rare and had not known it,' and that it took years to understand—showing she now values what she once overlooked.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'foresight' most nearly means
choices: a passing bad mood
the ability to see what will happen in the future
careful planning of a journey
a feeling of regret
deep bitterness about time already wasted
questions.explanation: The narrator contrasts what 'seemed like a mood' with what she now knows 'was foresight'—Crane's clear sight of his own short future—so it means the ability to foresee the future.

passages.title: The Cottage in the Tall Grass
passages.attribution: Retelling of Cider with Rosie by Laurie Lee — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening section introduces Laurie's earliest childhood, the family's arrival at the cottage, his father's departure, and his mother's struggle to raise the children.
passages.text:
I was three years old the summer they set me down in the grass, and the grass rose higher than my head, a green forest roaring with insects and swimming with light. My family had just carried our belongings up the steep lane to the rambling cottage in Slad, a crooked house tucked into a Cotswold hillside where the years still turned on the slow wheel of horses, oil lamps, and weather. This was England soon after the great war, though I knew nothing then of wars or dates. I knew only that the world was enormous and that I had been lost in it, and that when my sisters came to lift me I wept with relief and terror both.

My father did not stay long. He was a restless man, forever leaning toward some brighter city elsewhere, and one day he simply went and did not come back, leaving my mother with a houseful of children—her own and the stepchildren from his first marriage, eight of us in all—and almost no money to feed us. Yet she was not defeated. She was warm and scatterbrained and hopelessly loving, forgetting the kettle while she gazed at a sunset, but never once forgetting any of us.

My half-sisters Marjorie, Dorothy, and Phyllis became small mothers themselves, washing and scolding and cradling the younger ones. When a fever took hold of me and burned for days, it was my mother's steady hands and murmured songs that pulled me back from the dark. I remember waking weak and blinking, the room golden, and understanding without words that I had been kept alive by love alone. Poverty pressed on us always, but the cottage held its warmth like a banked fire.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of this passage?
choices: A restless father builds a prosperous new life in the city
Despite the father's departure and their poverty, the mother and sisters hold the family together with love
The village of Slad is dangerous for small children
The narrator's fever is caused by the family's move to a new house
The narrator's earliest memory is of being frightened and lost in the tall grass
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the father leaving and the mother and half-sisters keeping the family together with love and care despite near-poverty, as shown when the mother's care pulls the narrator through his fever.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who helped care for the younger children in the household?
choices: The father's business partners
Neighbors from a nearby city
The half-sisters Marjorie, Dorothy, and Phyllis
The village schoolteacher
The father, before he departed for the city
questions.explanation: The passage states that the half-sisters Marjorie, Dorothy, and Phyllis 'became small mothers themselves, washing and scolding and cradling the younger ones.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the narrator's mother was
choices: cold and neglectful toward her children
practical and highly organized in running the house
devoted to her children even though she was easily distracted
eager to leave the village and follow the father
overwhelmed by poverty and unable to care for the children after the father left
questions.explanation: The mother is described as forgetting the kettle while gazing at a sunset (easily distracted) yet 'never once' forgetting any of her children and nursing the narrator through his fever, showing deep devotion despite absent-mindedness.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "restless" most nearly means
choices: exhausted and sleepy
unable to settle and always wanting to be elsewhere
physically ill
generous and giving
anxious and full of worry
questions.explanation: The passage explains 'restless' by saying the father was 'forever leaning toward some brighter city elsewhere' and eventually left, showing it means unable to settle and always wanting to be somewhere else.

passages.title: Lessons, Seasons, and Silent Graves
passages.attribution: Retelling of Cider with Rosie by Laurie Lee — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action moves through the textures of village life: school, the feuding grannies, the turning seasons, and the darker events the village endures.
passages.text:
As I grew I was sent to the village school, a single crowded room where the fierce teacher we called Crabby ruled with a hard voice and a harder ruler, and where later the fairer Miss Wardley taught me, letter by letter, to read and to write. The days were measured not by clocks but by seasons—the frost that laced the windows, the hay that scented the whole valley, the blackberries fat in the autumn hedges.

Above all I remember the two old grannies who lived one atop the other in the cottage below ours. Granny Trill and Granny Wallon carried on a feud so ancient that neither could have named its cause, trading insults across the years like two cats guarding one wall. They quarreled over herbs, over ages, over the smallest slights, yet they were bound together as tightly as enemies can be. When at last one of them died, the other, robbed of her lifelong opponent, faded and followed within a few short weeks, as though the argument itself had been keeping her alive.

But the village was not all warmth and comedy. There were shadows in it too. One winter a man came home to the valley with money in his pockets, and he was set upon, robbed, and left in the freezing night to die—and afterward the whole village folded the memory away and agreed, without ever once speaking the agreement aloud, never to mention it again. A girl drowned in the dark water. A boy came close to taking his own life. I learned young that beauty and cruelty grew in the same soil, and that the world I loved could turn its face and frighten me.

questions.prompt: The passage as a whole is mainly about
choices: the narrator's rivalry with two elderly neighbors
how the narrator learned to read at school
the many sides of village life, from schooling and seasons to its quarrels and hidden cruelties
the reasons the village was abandoned
the bitter feud between Granny Trill and Granny Wallon and how it finally ended
questions.explanation: The passage surveys several facets of village life—school, seasons, the feuding grannies, and dark events like the robbery and drowning—showing both its warmth and its cruelty, rather than focusing on any single event.

questions.prompt: What happened after one of the two grannies died?
choices: The village held a large celebration
The other granny moved away from the valley
The other granny faded and died within a few weeks
The narrator inherited their cottage
The surviving granny, having won at last, lived on contentedly for years
questions.explanation: The passage states that when one granny died, the other 'faded and followed within a few short weeks, as though the argument itself had been keeping her alive.'

questions.prompt: The reaction of the village to the robbed man's death suggests that the villagers
choices: did not know the man had died
chose to bury the painful event in shared silence
immediately reported the crime to the authorities
blamed the two grannies for the death
were too terrified of the killer to say anything about it
questions.explanation: The passage says the village 'folded the memory away and agreed, without ever once speaking the agreement aloud, never to mention it again,' implying they dealt with the horror by a shared, unspoken silence.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "feud" most nearly means
choices: a friendly game
a business partnership
a long-lasting bitter quarrel
a religious ceremony
a brief misunderstanding soon forgotten
questions.explanation: The 'feud' is described as 'so ancient that neither could have named its cause' with the grannies 'trading insults across the years,' showing it means a long-lasting bitter quarrel.

passages.title: Rosie and the Cider under the Cart
passages.attribution: Retelling of Cider with Rosie by Laurie Lee — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax marks Laurie's passage into adolescence and his sexual awakening during a hot afternoon of haymaking with an older girl, Rosie Burdock.
passages.text:
The years turned me from a child into something restless and new. My body lengthened, my thoughts grew strange and hot, and the girls I had once ignored began to shimmer with a mystery I could not name. Desire crept into me the way warmth creeps into a stone left in the sun—slow, then sudden, then everywhere at once.

It came to a head on a golden afternoon in the hayfields, when the whole valley smelled of cut grass and the light lay thick as honey over everything. There was a girl named Rosie Burdock, older than I was and far bolder, with a knowing look that made my heart stumble. While the others worked and shouted in the heat, she caught my eye and drew me away, coaxing me down into the deep shade beneath a loaded hay-wagon, where the world shrank to a private green cave.

There she had hidden a stone jar of cider, cool and sharp and secret, and she made me drink. I had never tasted anything like it; it burned sweetly down my throat and set the whole afternoon spinning. Then, in that hidden hollow beneath the cart, she gave me my first kisses, clumsy and astonishing, and the ground seemed to tilt beneath me. I did not understand what was happening, only that I had crossed some invisible line and could never step back over it. When at last we crawled out into the ordinary daylight, blinking and unsteady, I felt that I had left my boyhood somewhere in the grass and would spend the rest of my life half-remembering where.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of this passage?
choices: The narrator learns how cider is made in his village
A hot afternoon with an older girl marks the narrator's awakening out of boyhood
The narrator and Rosie are caught by the other workers
The narrator prefers working in the hayfields to going to school
The narrator drinks too much cider and regrets it the following day
questions.explanation: The passage builds from the narrator's growing desire to the scene under the cart with Rosie, ending with the sense that he had 'left my boyhood somewhere in the grass'—an awakening into adolescence.

questions.prompt: What did Rosie share with the narrator beneath the hay-wagon?
choices: A basket of blackberries
A stone jar of hidden cider
A schoolbook
A letter from his father
A stone jar of honey
questions.explanation: The passage states that Rosie 'had hidden a stone jar of cider, cool and sharp and secret, and she made me drink.'

questions.prompt: The final sentence of the passage suggests that the narrator feels the experience has
choices: been quickly forgotten by the next morning
permanently changed him and ended his childhood
made him dislike Rosie Burdock
taught him a useful practical skill
left him confused about what happened but otherwise unchanged
questions.explanation: The narrator says he had 'crossed some invisible line and could never step back over it' and had 'left my boyhood somewhere in the grass,' implying a permanent change out of childhood.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "bold" (bolder) most nearly means
choices: shy and fearful
daring and unafraid
tired and weak
dishonest
rude and unkind
questions.explanation: Rosie is called 'older than I was and far bolder, with a knowing look,' and she boldly draws him away and offers cider, showing 'bolder' means more daring and unafraid.

passages.title: When the Old World Slipped Away
passages.attribution: Retelling of Cider with Rosie by Laurie Lee — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution is elegiac: the old rural England fades as modern life arrives, the sisters leave to marry, and the intimate village world of boyhood dissolves.
passages.text:
Looking back now, I can see that the world I was born into was already dying while I lived in it, though none of us knew to grieve. The valley that had run on horses and oil lamps and the patient turning of the seasons began, quietly at first and then all at once, to change beneath our feet.

The first motorcars came grinding up our lanes, scattering the hens and frightening the horses, and their noise and smell announced a future that had no place for the slow old ways. Roads were widened; the outside world, which had once seemed as distant as the moon, pressed closer every year. The great house on the hill lost its power, the old customs loosened, and the tight web of village life that had held us all began, thread by thread, to unravel.

My sisters, who had been the warm center of my childhood, grew up and fell in love and went away one by one to marry, and the crowded, noisy cottage that had always seemed too full grew strangely quiet. My mother lingered in it, still gazing at her sunsets, keeping the memory of us all. And I too felt the pull of the wider world, knowing that I would not stay.

So the England of my boyhood dissolved like breath on a cold window—not with any single blow, but by a thousand small departures. I have tried to hold it here in words because there was no other way to keep it. The village of my childhood is gone now, and the boy who lay in the tall grass is gone too, and only this remembering remains.

questions.prompt: The main idea of this passage is that
choices: the narrator's mother refused to let anyone leave the cottage
the old rural way of life faded as modern change arrived and the family scattered
the motorcars made the village wealthy
the narrator's sisters never married
the arrival of the first motorcars destroyed the village overnight
questions.explanation: The passage describes the arrival of cars and roads, the loosening of old customs, and the sisters leaving to marry, all conveying that the old rural world dissolved as modern life came and the family dispersed.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happened to the narrator's sisters?
choices: They took over the great house on the hill
They grew up, fell in love, and left one by one to marry
They moved to the city to work in factories
They stayed to care for the mother forever
They grew up and left one by one to find work in the wider world
questions.explanation: The passage states the sisters 'grew up and fell in love and went away one by one to marry,' leaving the cottage strangely quiet.

questions.prompt: The narrator's statement that he 'tried to hold it here in words because there was no other way to keep it' suggests that he
choices: believes writing can bring the village physically back
wrote the memoir to preserve a vanished world in memory
dislikes remembering his childhood
hopes to sell the old cottage
wrote the memoir mainly to record how motorcars had changed England
questions.explanation: Because the village 'is gone now' and only 'this remembering remains,' the line implies he wrote in order to preserve the lost world in memory, the only way left to keep it.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "dissolved" most nearly means
choices: appeared suddenly
faded gradually away
grew larger and stronger
became frozen in place
broke apart in a single violent blow
questions.explanation: The England of his boyhood is said to have 'dissolved like breath on a cold window—not with any single blow, but by a thousand small departures,' showing 'dissolved' means faded gradually away.

passages.title: A Guest in Name Only
passages.attribution: Retelling of Dracula by Bram Stoker — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening section: a young lawyer arrives at a remote castle and discovers his host is a monster who has trapped him.
passages.text:
Jonathan Harker, a young and ambitious English lawyer, had come to the far mountains of Transylvania on what seemed a simple errand: to help a nobleman named Count Dracula purchase a house in England. The peasants at the last inn had behaved strangely, pressing crucifixes into his palms and crossing themselves at the mention of his host, but Harker dismissed their fear as country superstition. A carriage carried him up switchback roads beneath a sky loud with wolves, and at last the great castle rose before him, half-crumbled and black against the stars.

At first the Count was a courteous host, old and pale and oddly cold to the touch, with a strength that belied his ancient years. But small wrongnesses gathered like dust. The Count cast no reflection in Harker's shaving glass. He crawled face-down along the outer walls like a lizard. No servants were ever seen, and every door Harker tried was locked. Slowly the terrible truth settled over him: he was not a guest but a prisoner, and his host was no ordinary man.

One night, drowsy in a forbidden room, Harker was nearly overcome by three beautiful women with sharp white teeth and burning eyes, who bent over him with dreadful hunger until the Count himself drove them back, claiming Harker for his own purpose. Harker understood then that the Count meant to travel to England, carrying his ancient evil across the sea, and that he was not meant to live to warn anyone. Alone and half-mad with dread, he began to plot a desperate escape down the sheer castle wall, choosing the risk of the rocks below over the certainty of the horror within.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Harker enjoys the hospitality of a kind and generous nobleman.
Harker studies the history and architecture of an old castle.
Harker gradually realizes he is the prisoner of a monstrous, inhuman host.
Harker helps peasants overcome their foolish superstitions.
Harker successfully arranges the purchase of an English house for a foreign nobleman.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from Harker's simple errand to his slow discovery of 'small wrongnesses' and the truth that 'he was not a guest but a prisoner, and his host was no ordinary man,' ending with his plan to escape.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did the peasants at the last inn do when Harker's host was mentioned?
choices: They pressed crucifixes into his palms and crossed themselves.
They offered to guide him safely to the castle.
They laughed at his fears and wished him luck.
They warned him to buy the house quickly and leave.
They pressed silver coins into his hands for protection.
questions.explanation: The passage states directly that the peasants were 'pressing crucifixes into his palms and crossing themselves at the mention of his host.'

questions.prompt: It can best be inferred from the passage that the Count drives the three women back because he
choices: is jealous that the women admire Harker's appearance.
wishes to prove to Harker that the castle is safe.
fears the women will help Harker escape the castle.
intends to keep Harker alive for a purpose of his own.
believes he alone has the right to feed upon Harker's blood.
questions.explanation: The passage says the Count drove them back 'claiming Harker for his own purpose,' and Harker then realizes the Count means to use him and does not want him dead yet, implying he is being kept alive deliberately.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "belied" most nearly means
choices: matched
contradicted
confirmed
explained
revealed
questions.explanation: The Count is 'old and pale' yet has 'a strength that belied his ancient years,' meaning his strength was inconsistent with, or contradicted, his apparent age.

passages.title: The Ship of the Dead
passages.attribution: Retelling of Dracula by Bram Stoker — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action: Dracula reaches England, and the lively Lucy sickens, dies, and rises as an undead creature the hunters must destroy.
passages.text:
While Harker lay trapped abroad, the Count made his own crossing. He hid within great boxes of his native earth in the hold of a ship, and as the vessel neared England a savage storm rose. One by one the sailors vanished, until the ship ran aground with a dead captain lashed to the wheel and not a living soul aboard. The Count had arrived.

In the seaside town, two young women exchanged letters. Mina Murray, Harker's steady and clever fiancee, worried over her silent lover; her dear friend Lucy Westenra, warm and full of life, had lately received three proposals of marriage in a single day. She accepted the gentle Arthur Holmwood, though the American Quincey Morris and the asylum doctor John Seward loved her too.

Soon Lucy began to walk in her sleep, drawn out into the night as if summoned. Each morning she woke paler and weaker, and two small wounds appeared upon her throat. Dr. Seward, baffled by her decline, sent for his old teacher, Professor Van Helsing, a learned man who had studied strange diseases. Van Helsing alone guessed the truth, and though the men gave Lucy their own blood and hung garlic flowers about her room, the thing that visited her in the dark was stronger than their remedies. Lucy died.

But she did not rest. Rumors spread of a 'Bloofer Lady' who lured small children into the darkness and returned them frightened and bleeding. Van Helsing led the grieving men to Lucy's tomb, where they found her changed, beautiful and cruel and undead. With heavy hearts they drove a stake through her heart, struck off her head, and so at last set her tormented soul free. The hunt for the Count had truly begun.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: the friendship between Mina Murray and Lucy Westenra.
how Dracula reaches England and how Lucy becomes his victim and then a monster.
the medical training of Professor Van Helsing and Dr. Seward.
the dangers faced by sailors crossing the sea in storms.
how a mysterious wasting disease spreads through a quiet seaside town.
questions.explanation: The passage traces the Count's arrival by ship and then Lucy's sleepwalking, death, transformation into the 'Bloofer Lady,' and destruction, ending as 'the hunt for the Count had truly begun.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, which man did Lucy agree to marry?
choices: Quincey Morris
John Seward
Arthur Holmwood
Jonathan Harker
Professor Van Helsing
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'She accepted the gentle Arthur Holmwood, though the American Quincey Morris and the asylum doctor John Seward loved her too.'

questions.prompt: That the men act 'with heavy hearts' when they destroy Lucy most strongly suggests that they
choices: still love her and act out of sorrowful duty rather than hatred.
are uncertain whether Lucy is truly dead.
are afraid Van Helsing has misled them.
hope to be rewarded for their bravery.
believe destroying Lucy is the only way to protect the town's children.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'heavy hearts,' combined with the earlier description of these grieving men who had loved Lucy, implies their act is painful duty meant to 'set her tormented soul free,' not cruelty.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "baffled" most nearly means
choices: delighted
angered
relieved
puzzled
saddened
questions.explanation: Dr. Seward is 'baffled by her decline' and so must send for his teacher to explain it, showing that he is confused or puzzled by what is happening to Lucy.

passages.title: The Mark upon Her Brow
passages.attribution: Retelling of Dracula by Bram Stoker — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and turn: the hunters strike at Dracula's resting places, but he retaliates by cursing Mina, whose new bond becomes the key to tracking him.
passages.text:
United by grief and purpose, the little band swore to hunt the Count to his destruction. Van Helsing explained his one great weakness: the vampire must rest by day in boxes of his own Transylvanian soil, and he had scattered many such boxes in hiding places across London. If every box could be found and sanctified, the Count would have nowhere left to sleep and nowhere to hide. So the hunters set out, breaking into cellars and lonely houses, laying holy wafers in the earth to spoil it.

The Count, however, was not idle. To punish the men who pursued him, he crept by night into the very house where they had gathered and fed upon Mina, now Harker's wife. Worse, he forced her to drink his own blood, binding her to him and beginning to turn her slowly into a creature like himself. When Van Helsing later pressed a sacred wafer to her forehead to bless her, it seared her skin like a brand, leaving a red scar that marked her as unclean. Mina wept, terrified that her soul was already half-lost.

Yet the curse cut both ways. Because the Count's blood now ran in Mina's veins, a strange link had formed between them. Under Van Helsing's hypnosis, Mina could sense what the Count sensed: the lap of water, the creak of a ship, the darkness of a hold. The hunters realized their enemy had fled England by sea, retreating toward his distant castle. The very bond meant to enslave Mina had become the thread by which they might follow him home.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the main idea of the passage?
choices: Mina decides to leave the group so she cannot endanger them.
Van Helsing discovers a cure that frees Mina from the vampire's blood.
The Count gives up his plan and abandons his boxes of soil in London.
The hunters attack Dracula's resting places, and Mina's curse turns into a way to track him.
Mina's hypnosis reveals the hiding place of every box of soil the Count owns in London.
questions.explanation: The passage describes the hunters sanctifying the soil-boxes, the Count's revenge on Mina, and how her resulting bond 'became the thread by which they might follow him home.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did the hunters place in the boxes of earth to ruin them?
choices: garlic flowers
holy wafers
wooden stakes
silver crosses
Transylvanian soil
questions.explanation: The passage states the hunters were 'laying holy wafers in the earth to spoil it,' so the Count could no longer rest there.

questions.prompt: That the sacred wafer burns Mina's forehead most strongly implies that she
choices: is secretly working against the group on the Count's behalf.
has already fully become a vampire like the Count.
has been partly tainted and is falling under the vampire's power.
was never truly in danger from the Count at all.
can no longer bear the touch of holy objects because she has lost her faith.
questions.explanation: The wafer, described as holy, sears her and leaves a scar 'that marked her as unclean' after she was forced to drink the Count's blood, implying she is becoming tainted but is not yet fully lost.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "sanctified" most nearly means
choices: made holy
purchased
hidden away
torn apart
buried underground
questions.explanation: The hunters use 'holy wafers' to bless the earth so the vampire cannot rest in it; to sanctify the boxes is to make them holy and unusable to him.

passages.title: Dust at Sundown
passages.attribution: Retelling of Dracula by Bram Stoker — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: the hunters chase Dracula to his homeland, destroy him at last, and pay a painful price for their victory.
passages.text:
The chase swept across Europe. The hunters split their forces, some racing by train and others by riverboat, all converging on the wild country where the Count had been born. Mina rode with them, her strange bond to the vampire growing stronger as they neared his home; each time Van Helsing lulled her into a trance, she reported the swaying of a cart and the nearness of the mountains. They were closing in, but so was the sunset, after which the Count would wake at full strength.

On a snowbound road below the castle, they at last overtook the wagon that carried his final box of earth, guarded by a band of fierce gypsies. In the failing light the men fought their way to the coffin and tore off its lid. There lay the Count, awake now and glaring with triumph, certain that night would save him. But the sun had not quite set. Harker slashed his great knife across the monster's throat, while the brave American, Quincey Morris, drove his blade deep into the vampire's heart.

In an instant the ancient evil crumbled into dust and was gone. As it vanished, the red scar faded from Mina's forehead, and her face shone with peace at last; her soul was saved, the curse undone. But the victory had cost them dearly. Quincey, wounded in the struggle, sank into the snow and died among his friends, smiling to see Mina freed.

Years afterward, Harker and Mina lived quietly with their young son. They had given the boy a string of names honoring every man of that brave company, but they called him, above all, Quincey, so that the child born of their survival would carry the name of the friend who had died for it.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: the final pursuit and destruction of the Count and the price of that victory.
the long train and riverboat journey the hunters take across Europe.
Mina's growing fear that she will never be freed from the curse.
the birth and naming of Harker and Mina's young son.
how Mina's bond to the Count grows stronger the closer they come to his home.
questions.explanation: The passage builds to the killing of the Count, the lifting of Mina's curse, and Quincey's death, closing with how the family honors that costly victory.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who drove a blade into the vampire's heart?
choices: Jonathan Harker
Professor Van Helsing
Arthur Holmwood
Quincey Morris
Dr. John Seward
questions.explanation: The passage states that Harker slashed the Count's throat 'while the brave American, Quincey Morris, drove his blade deep into the vampire's heart.'

questions.prompt: It can best be inferred that Harker and Mina name their son Quincey in order to
choices: hide the fact that the boy is not truly their own child.
honor the friend who gave his life so they could survive.
warn the boy about the dangers of the Count's homeland.
please Van Helsing, who had led the company.
remember the distant land where the Count was finally destroyed.
questions.explanation: The passage says the boy carries 'the name of the friend who had died for it,' showing the naming is an act of gratitude and remembrance for Quincey's sacrifice.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "converging" most nearly means
choices: turning back
giving up
coming together
resting quietly
moving swiftly
questions.explanation: The hunters travel by different routes 'all converging on the wild country where the Count had been born,' meaning their separate paths come together at the same place.

passages.title: The Bright Red Warning
passages.attribution: Retelling of His New Mittens by Stephen Crane — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening establishes Horace, his brand-new mittens, and the tempting snowball war he is forbidden to join.
passages.text:
Snow had fallen all morning, and by noon the little town was hushed and white, its fences capped in soft ridges and its roofs gleaming. Horace stood in the doorway of his house, his hands buried deep in a pair of mittens so new and so red that they seemed to glow against the pale world. His mother had knitted or bought them only days before, and she had pressed them upon him with a warning that still rang in his ears. He was not, under any circumstances, to soak them, to dirty them, or to spoil their bright color with rough play. Mittens like these, she reminded him, did not grow on trees.

Horace loved the mittens. He also loved, more fiercely and more secretly, the racket rising from the vacant lot down the street. There the neighborhood boys had divided themselves into two shouting armies and were pelting one another with snowballs, their breath steaming, their cheeks scarlet, their laughter carrying across the frozen air like the cries of gulls. It was a war, a glorious war, and every boy in it looked to Horace like a hero.

He wanted to run to them. His feet ached to carry him into the thick of the fight. But each time he took a step, the memory of his mother's face rose before him, and he glanced down at the two red things on his hands as if they were shackles rather than gifts. So he lingered at the edge of the yard, torn between obedience and longing, a small figure caught between a warm house and a cold, thrilling battlefield. The snow fell on, indifferent, and the shouting only grew louder, pulling at him like a rope.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Horace is torn between obeying his mother about his new mittens and joining an exciting snowball fight
Horace dislikes the cold weather and wishes to stay indoors
The neighborhood boys are unkind to Horace and refuse to let him play
Horace's mother regrets giving him such an expensive pair of mittens
The heavy snowfall forces the boys to give up their game and go home
questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly shows Horace's inner conflict: he loves the mittens and remembers his mother's warning, yet longs to join the war, ending 'torn between obedience and longing.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what warning did Horace's mother give him about the mittens?
choices: That he should share them with the other boys
That he must not soak, dirty, or spoil the color of them through rough play
That he should only wear them indoors
That he must return them if he grew tired of them
That he must be careful not to lose them in the snow
questions.explanation: The passage states she warned him 'not, under any circumstances, to soak them, to dirty them, or to spoil their bright color with rough play.'

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that Horace views the mittens as
choices: worthless objects he wishes to throw away
a source of pride he does not care about
both a treasured gift and a frustrating restriction on his freedom
a disguise that lets him blend in with the other boys
a practical tool that will keep his hands warm during the fight
questions.explanation: He 'loved the mittens' yet looked at them 'as if they were shackles rather than gifts,' showing he sees them as both treasured and confining.

questions.prompt: In the final sentence, the phrase 'pulling at him like a rope' suggests that the shouting
choices: physically injured Horace
strongly tempted and drew Horace toward the battle
frightened Horace away from the lot
reminded Horace of his mother's voice
made Horace feel trapped and unable to leave his yard
questions.explanation: The simile compares the shouting to a rope tugging him, emphasizing how powerfully the war tempted him, consistent with his 'feet ached to carry him' into the fight.

passages.title: Grandma Boy
passages.attribution: Retelling of His New Mittens by Stephen Crane — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action: mockery from Jimmie Trescott shames Horace into defying his mother and plunging into the snowball battle.
passages.text:
It was Jimmie Trescott who noticed him first. Jimmie was a bold, loud boy, quick with his tongue, and he spotted Horace hovering at the edge of the lot like a bird afraid to land. A grin spread across his face. Pointing one wet glove toward the newcomer, he called out for the whole army to hear, and the other boys turned to look.

Horace, they jeered, was a grandma boy. He could not play because his mother would not let him. He had to keep his pretty little mittens clean. The taunts came in a chorus now, each voice sharper than the last, and the boys laughed and mimicked a fussy old woman shaking her finger. To a small boy, there is no whip more stinging than the ridicule of his fellows, and each word landed on Horace like a blow. His face burned. His throat tightened. The warmth of his mother's warning, which had held him so firmly a moment before, dissolved into a hot, reckless shame.

He could not bear it. Something in him snapped, and pride flooded in where obedience had been. Without another thought for the mittens, Horace scooped up a fistful of snow, packed it hard between his palms, and hurled himself into the very middle of the fight. He threw and dodged and shouted with the rest, and for a while the joy of belonging drowned out every caution. Snowballs flew and burst against his coat. He rolled in the drifts, he grabbed at the frozen earth, and he no longer cared what happened to his hands.

By the time the armies broke apart, panting and triumphant, the two bright red mittens were soaked through, gray and heavy and streaked with dirt, their glory quite ruined.

questions.prompt: What is the main event described in this passage?
choices: Horace teaches the other boys a new snowball game
Jimmie Trescott apologizes to Horace for teasing him
Horace, shamed by the boys' mockery, joins the fight and ruins his mittens
Horace's mother comes to bring him home from the lot
Horace resists the taunts and walks home with his mittens still clean
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the boys' jeering, Horace's shame, his decision to join the battle, and the resulting ruin of the mittens.

questions.prompt: What specific insult do the boys hurl at Horace?
choices: That he is a coward who cannot throw straight
That he is a grandma boy who must keep his mittens clean
That he is too small to join their army
That he stole the mittens he is wearing
That he is afraid of getting his coat wet
questions.explanation: The boys jeer that Horace is 'a grandma boy' who 'had to keep his pretty little mittens clean.'

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that Horace joins the fight primarily because
choices: he suddenly stops loving the mittens
he cannot endure being ridiculed by the other boys
he forgets that he is wearing the mittens
his mother gives him permission to play
he wants to prove that he can throw better than Jimmie Trescott
questions.explanation: His shame at the taunts—'no whip more stinging than the ridicule of his fellows'—overwhelms his obedience, so 'he could not bear it' and threw himself in.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'stinging' most nearly means
choices: numbing with cold
causing sharp emotional pain
making a buzzing sound
gently soothing
briefly irritating before being quickly forgotten
questions.explanation: The ridicule is called a 'whip' whose words 'landed on Horace like a blow' and made his face burn, showing 'stinging' means sharply painful to his feelings.

passages.title: The Runaway
passages.attribution: Retelling of His New Mittens by Stephen Crane — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The turning point: scolded at home and swollen with wounded pride, Horace refuses dinner and dramatically runs away into the cold.
passages.text:
His mother met him at the door, and her eyes went at once to his hands. The soaked, filthy mittens told the whole story. She scolded him then, her voice rising with disappointment, reminding him of every word of her warning, and Horace stood before her with his chin thrust out and his heart hardening. He would not say he was sorry. Instead a great, aching pride swelled up inside him until it filled his whole chest, and he told himself that he was terribly, grandly wronged.

At supper he sat stiff and silent. The good smell of food rose from the table, but Horace refused to touch it. When his mother pressed him, he clamped his mouth shut and glared at his plate, playing the part of a martyr with all the seriousness a small boy can summon. In his mind he had already become a tragic figure, a wanderer cast out and misunderstood, and there was a bitter sweetness in imagining how sorry everyone would be.

So he made his decision. He would run away. He rose from the table, and though some corner of him hoped to be stopped, he pulled on his coat and pushed out into the winter night. The door closed behind him, and the cold rushed to meet him.

The streets were dark and empty. Snow squeaked beneath his boots, and the wind found every gap in his clothing. At first he marched with his head high, rehearsing his grievances, picturing himself brave and free. But the town stretched on, block after silent block, and the romance of his flight began to thin. His stomach growled. His fingers ached. The grand adventure was shrinking, step by frozen step, into something small and cold and lonely.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best states the main idea of this passage?
choices: Horace's mother forgives him instantly and feeds him a warm supper
Wounded pride drives Horace to refuse dinner and run away, only for his adventure to turn miserable
Horace apologizes to his mother and promises to be careful next time
Horace decides the snowball fight was not worth the trouble it caused
Horace enjoys his walk through the snowy streets and feels brave and free
questions.explanation: The passage traces Horace's hardened pride, his refusal to eat, his decision to run away, and the flight collapsing into cold loneliness.

questions.prompt: What does Horace do at the supper table?
choices: He eats quickly and asks to be excused
He refuses to eat and sits stiff and silent
He argues loudly with his mother about the mittens
He cries and begs for forgiveness
He eats only a few bites before rising from the table
questions.explanation: The passage states 'he sat stiff and silent' and 'refused to touch' the food, clamping his mouth shut.

questions.prompt: The detail that 'some corner of him hoped to be stopped' suggests that Horace
choices: truly wants to leave home forever
is not as certain about running away as he pretends
is planning to return with a gift for his mother
is afraid of the other boys finding him
expects his mother to apologize to him before he goes
questions.explanation: Hoping to be stopped reveals that beneath his dramatic defiance, part of Horace does not really want to go, showing his uncertainty.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'the romance of his flight began to thin' means that
choices: Horace began to move more slowly through the snow
the exciting, heroic feeling of running away started to fade
Horace fell in love with the idea of adventure
the streets grew narrower as he walked
the bitter cold made it harder for Horace to keep walking
questions.explanation: 'Romance' here means the glamorous, heroic feeling; it 'began to thin' as his stomach growled and the adventure shrank into misery, so the thrill was fading.

passages.title: The Warmth of Home
passages.attribution: Retelling of His New Mittens by Stephen Crane — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: cold and defeated at the butcher's shop, Horace is found by his uncle and brought back to his forgiving mother.
passages.text:
By the time Horace reached the lighted window of Stickney's shop, his grand rebellion had crumbled entirely. Stickney the butcher kept his place warm and bright against the winter, and the glow spilling onto the snow drew the boy like a moth. Horace lingered near the doorway, half in and half out of the light, no longer a tragic wanderer but only a small, chilled child who did not know how to go home and could not bear to stay out. His anger had burned down to ash, and beneath it there was nothing but hunger and cold and a quiet, growing wish to be found.

And found he was. It was his uncle, Dr. Trescott, who came upon him there. The doctor did not scold or lecture. He simply took in the situation with a calm, kindly eye and gathered the boy up, steering him gently back through the dark streets toward the house Horace had fled hours before. There was no need for speeches. The warmth of a grown man's steadiness said everything that needed saying.

Home opened its door to him. His mother, who had surely been sick with worry, did not meet him with fresh scolding. Instead she took him in, peeled off his frozen coat, and set him near the heat, and soon there was food before him, hot and plain and wonderful. The whole enormous drama of the day—the mittens, the taunts, the flight into the night—dissolved in the ordinary comfort of being cared for.

Horace ate. He grew warm. His great pride, which had seemed so vast and important, shrank quietly to its true size, and in its place came something better: the plain, forgiving warmth of home, which had been waiting for him all along.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Horace runs even farther away to escape his uncle
Horace's runaway ends when his uncle finds him and his mother welcomes him home with warmth
Horace decides to live at Stickney's shop for the winter
Horace is punished severely for ruining his mittens
Horace's uncle scolds him sternly before marching him back home
questions.explanation: The passage describes Horace being found by Dr. Trescott and returned to his forgiving mother, where comfort replaces his drama.

questions.prompt: Who discovers Horace outside the butcher's shop?
choices: Jimmie Trescott
Stickney the butcher
His uncle, Dr. Trescott
His mother
A stranger passing by in the dark streets
questions.explanation: The passage states 'It was his uncle, Dr. Trescott, who came upon him there.'

questions.prompt: How does Horace's mother most likely feel when he returns, based on the passage?
choices: Still furious and eager to punish him
Indifferent to whether he came back
Relieved and forgiving, having worried about him
Amused by his dramatic behavior
Disappointed but too tired to say anything to him
questions.explanation: She 'had surely been sick with worry' yet met him not with scolding but by warming and feeding him, indicating relief and forgiveness.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'his grand rebellion had crumbled entirely' means that Horace's defiance had
choices: become even stronger and angrier
completely fallen apart and collapsed
been rewarded by the other boys
turned into a plan for a new adventure
been carefully hidden so his uncle would not notice it
questions.explanation: 'Crumbled entirely' means fallen apart; the next lines show his anger 'burned down to ash,' leaving only a chilled child wishing to be found.

passages.title: The Four Sisters by the Fire
passages.attribution: Retelling of Little Women by Louisa May Alcott — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening introduces the four March sisters, their mother, and their new friendship with the boy next door as they weather a poor wartime Christmas.
passages.text:
The parlor was thin on coal and thinner on money, but the four March girls filled it with more noise than any furnace could. Meg, the eldest, smoothed her worn gloves and wished aloud for pretty things. Jo, restless and blunt, sprawled by the hearth grumbling that a girl ought to be allowed to run and write and matter. Beth said little, but her fingers moved as though they already touched piano keys, while small Amy fretted over her nose and her drawings in equal measure.

Their father was far away, serving as a chaplain to Union soldiers, and so the household leaned on Marmee, their mother, whose patience seemed to stretch wider than their want. When Christmas came with no feast, she quietly asked her daughters to carry their own breakfast to a starving family down the lane. They grumbled, then went, and returned lighter than they had left.

Beside their modest house stood a grand one, where a lonely rich boy named Laurie watched the sisters through his window like a traveler watching a warm inn. Jo, never one to wait for an introduction, marched over and befriended him outright. Soon his stern grandfather, old Mr. Laurence, unbent enough to notice shy Beth and, to her astonishment, gave her a piano of her own.

There were quarrels, of course. Amy, stung by being left out, burned the book of stories Jo had labored over, and Jo's fury nearly froze into hatred. But when Amy broke through thin ice and Jo dragged her, dripping and terrified, from the pond, the anger melted. Marmee taught them that forgiveness, like charity, cost something and was worth the cost. So the sisters learned, small lesson by small lesson, how to live together.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: The March family recovers a lost fortune during the war.
Despite poverty and quarrels, the March sisters learn kindness, friendship, and forgiveness.
Laurie's grandfather adopts the four sisters as his heirs.
Jo abandons writing after her manuscript is destroyed.
The sisters compete with one another to win the friendship of the wealthy boy next door.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from the sisters' wants and squabbles to charity, new friendship, and forgiveness after the ice incident, showing they learn to live kindly together despite their poverty.

questions.prompt: What gift does Mr. Laurence give to Beth?
choices: A box of drawing pencils
A new pair of gloves
A piano
A book of stories
A book of printed music to play
questions.explanation: The passage states that old Mr. Laurence 'gave her a piano of her own,' delighting the shy Beth.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Jo's rescue of Amy on the ice caused Jo to
choices: distrust Amy even more than before
let go of her anger and forgive her sister
refuse to speak to Marmee
stop spending time with Laurie
feel newly proud of her own bravery
questions.explanation: After Jo pulls the terrified Amy from the pond, the passage says 'the anger melted,' implying the danger led Jo to forgive her.

questions.prompt: In the sentence 'his stern grandfather... unbent enough to notice shy Beth,' the word 'unbent' most nearly means
choices: collapsed physically
became less rigid and softened
refused to help
grew angry
straightened his posture
questions.explanation: The context shows the previously 'stern' grandfather warming enough to give Beth a gift, so 'unbent' means he softened and became less rigid.

passages.title: The Telegram and the Fever
passages.attribution: Retelling of Little Women by Louisa May Alcott — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action tests the family when their father falls ill and Beth catches a dangerous fever while Marmee is away.
passages.text:
The winter that followed brought a fear the sisters had not rehearsed. A telegram arrived, its few words heavier than a whole book: their father lay gravely ill in a Washington hospital, and Marmee must go to him at once. The house, usually loud, fell into a hush of hurried packing and counted coins.

Money was the trouble, as always. Jo disappeared for an afternoon and came back changed, a bonnet pulled low. When she lifted it, her thick chestnut hair was gone, cropped short and sold to help pay for the journey. She laughed to hide the sting of it, though that night her sisters heard her weep for the one beauty she had thought she owned. Still, no one doubted the love behind the sacrifice.

With Marmee gone, the girls tried to keep the household running. Beth, gentlest of them all, continued visiting a poor immigrant family whose baby was sick, carrying broth and comfort as their mother had taught. But sickness is not choosy. Beth caught the scarlet fever from that failing home, and the illness burned through her small frame with frightening speed. Amy was sent away to stay with cranky Aunt March so she would not fall ill too, and she went unwillingly.

For days Beth hovered between the world and something beyond it, her fever climbing until even the doctor grew quiet. Jo, who had faced the sale of her hair with a joke, could find no joke now; she sat through the dark hours listening to Beth's ragged breathing and bargaining silently with heaven. At last the fever broke, and Beth opened her eyes weak but living. Soon after, better news arrived: their father was mending and would be home by Christmas.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: Amy's happy visit to Aunt March
how the family endures fear and sacrifice during illness
Jo's decision to become a writer
a joyful Christmas party at the March house
Beth's decision to train as a nurse for the poor
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the father's illness, Jo's sacrifice of her hair, and Beth's near-fatal fever, all showing the family enduring fear and sacrifice.

questions.prompt: Why does Jo sell her hair?
choices: To buy a new bonnet for herself
To help pay for Marmee's trip to their sick father
Because Amy dared her to
To give the money to the immigrant family
To buy medicine for the feverish Beth
questions.explanation: The passage says Jo sold her hair 'to help pay for the journey' after the telegram called Marmee to their ill father in Washington.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Beth became ill because she
choices: refused to eat during the winter
continued visiting a poor sick family out of kindness
traveled to Washington with Marmee
stayed too long with Aunt March
caught the fever from Marmee before she left for Washington
questions.explanation: The passage says Beth kept 'visiting a poor immigrant family whose baby was sick' and 'caught the scarlet fever from that failing home,' linking her kindness to the illness.

questions.prompt: In the phrase 'the illness burned through her small frame with frightening speed,' the word 'frame' most nearly means
choices: a wooden border
her body
a picture
a schedule
the structure of the house
questions.explanation: Describing an illness moving through Beth, 'small frame' refers to her body, not a border or structure.

passages.title: A Refusal and a Farewell
passages.attribution: Retelling of Little Women by Louisa May Alcott — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The turning point sees the sisters grow up, Jo reject Laurie's proposal and leave home, and the family suffer its deepest loss.
passages.text:
The girls did not stay children. Meg was the first to step into a new life, marrying John Brooke, Laurie's mild-mannered tutor, and setting up a small household where love had to stretch further than income. In time she cradled twins, and the March circle widened.

Laurie, meanwhile, had grown from a lonely boy into a young man who believed he loved Jo, and one afternoon he asked her to marry him. Jo, who cared for him as she cared for a brother, could not pretend otherwise. Gently but firmly she refused, and though it wounded them both, she would not marry where she did not feel the right kind of love. To clear her head and chase her old ambition, she went to New York to write. There she met Friedrich Bhaer, a poor, older German professor with kind eyes and blunt honesty, who read her sensational stories and quietly urged her toward writing that mattered.

Amy, for her part, sailed to Europe as the companion of wealthy Aunt March, sketching cathedrals and learning the polish of the wider world.

But the family's brightest thread was fraying. Beth had never truly regained her strength after the fever years before. She did not rage against it; she simply grew thinner and more transparent, like a candle burning low, until the household understood what was coming and could not stop it. When she died, the house that had always been full of her quiet music went terribly still. Jo, home again, felt the loss like a wound that would not close, and for a while even her pen lay idle.

Grief scattered them for a season. Yet across the ocean, sorrow drew two mourners together, and something new began to grow where Beth's absence had left an ache.

questions.prompt: Which best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: The sisters remain unmarried and unchanged.
As the sisters grow up, they face new love, difficult choices, and the loss of Beth.
Jo happily agrees to marry Laurie.
Amy decides never to leave home.
Jo's rise to fame as a celebrated New York writer.
questions.explanation: The passage covers Meg's marriage, Jo's refusal of Laurie and move to write, Amy's travels, and Beth's death, all showing the sisters growing up and facing change and loss.

questions.prompt: Why does Jo refuse Laurie's proposal?
choices: She is already engaged to Professor Bhaer.
She loves him only as a brother, not romantically.
Her mother forbids the marriage.
She dislikes his grandfather.
She has decided she will never marry anyone.
questions.explanation: The passage says Jo 'cared for him as she cared for a brother' and 'would not marry where she did not feel the right kind of love.'

questions.prompt: The final sentence about 'two mourners' drawn together across the ocean most likely hints that
choices: Beth will recover after all
Laurie and Amy, both grieving, will fall in love
Jo will return to New York
Meg's twins will travel to Europe
Jo and Professor Bhaer will soon marry
questions.explanation: The passage places Amy in Europe and describes grief drawing two mourners together into 'something new,' foreshadowing Laurie and Amy's romance.

questions.prompt: In the phrase 'like a candle burning low,' the comparison mainly emphasizes that Beth is
choices: angry and rebellious
slowly and gently fading toward death
suddenly cured
hidden from her family
growing calm and content in her recovery
questions.explanation: The passage says Beth 'grew thinner and more transparent,' not raging but fading, so the low candle image emphasizes a slow, gentle decline toward death.

passages.title: A House Full of Boys
passages.attribution: Retelling of Little Women by Louisa May Alcott — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution brings weddings, an inheritance, and a family gathered in gratitude around Marmee.
passages.text:
In time the clouds of grief thinned, and the March story turned toward its warm ending. Laurie and Amy, who had found each other in their sorrow abroad, came home married, no longer the squabbling boy and vain little girl of years past but a settled, generous couple.

Jo, meanwhile, had not forgotten the honest professor from New York. When Friedrich Bhaer at last came to find her, poor in pocket but rich in heart, she discovered that the brotherly affection she had felt for Laurie was nothing beside this deeper feeling. They agreed to marry, though neither had much to offer beyond devotion and work.

Fortune, in its odd way, arranged the rest. Aunt March, who had always seemed more vinegar than sugar, left Jo her large country house, Plumfield. Jo, who had once dreamed only of writing fame, found a new and larger dream in those empty rooms. She and Professor Bhaer opened the estate as a school for boys, filling the halls with mischief, lessons, and the kind of second chances that lonely children rarely receive. Jo, who had felt boxed in as a girl, now had a house she could fill with noise on purpose.

The novel closes not with riches but with gathering. On a mellow autumn day the surviving sisters came together with their husbands and children around Marmee, whose patient love had steered them through poverty, sickness, and loss. There were empty chairs remembered and full ones celebrated. Meg had her family, Amy her comfort, Jo her boys and her professor, and all of them had one another. The lesson of their childhood held true to the end: that a home built on love outlasts and outshines any home built on wealth.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of this final passage?
choices: Wealth is the surest path to happiness.
A home built on love matters more than one built on riches.
The sisters regret marrying at all.
