Fame as a writer was Jo's only real reward.
Time eventually erases every trace of a family's sorrow.
questions.explanation: The closing lines state directly that 'a home built on love outlasts and outshines any home built on wealth,' summing up the passage's message.

questions.prompt: What does Jo do with Plumfield, the house she inherits?
choices: She sells it to pay her debts.
She opens it as a school for boys with Professor Bhaer.
She gives it to Laurie and Amy.
She lives there alone to write novels.
She turns it into a quiet home for her own large family.
questions.explanation: The passage says Jo and Professor Bhaer 'opened the estate as a school for boys,' filling it with lessons and second chances.

questions.prompt: The mention of 'empty chairs remembered and full ones celebrated' most likely refers to
choices: a shortage of furniture at Plumfield
the family remembering lost members like Beth while celebrating those still present
Jo's plan to buy more chairs
guests who failed to arrive at the gathering
the many boys who now fill the seats at Jo's new school
questions.explanation: Given Beth's earlier death, the 'empty chairs remembered' point to lost loved ones, while 'full ones celebrated' honor the surviving family gathered around Marmee.

questions.prompt: In the description of Aunt March as 'more vinegar than sugar,' the phrase suggests she was
choices: sweet and generous by nature
sour or sharp-tempered rather than warm
physically ill
fond of cooking
unpredictable and changeable in her moods
questions.explanation: Contrasting vinegar with sugar, the phrase paints Aunt March as sour and sharp in manner, which makes her leaving Jo the house a surprise.

passages.title: The Long Ride to the Prairie
passages.attribution: Retelling of My Ántonia by Willa Cather — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: This opening section introduces Jim Burden and the Shimerda family as they first arrive on the Nebraska frontier and Jim's friendship with Ántonia begins.
passages.text:
When Jim Burden was ten years old, both of his parents died, and he was sent from the green hills of Virginia to live with his grandparents on their farm far out on the Nebraska plains. The journey was long and strange, and on the same westbound train rode a family who spoke no English at all. They were the Shimerdas, immigrants from Bohemia, and their older daughter had dark, quick eyes that seemed to take in everything at once. Her name was Ántonia.

The country Jim rode into was unlike anything he had known. There were no fences, no rivers he could name, no familiar trees—only an endless sea of red grass that ran shivering to the edge of the sky in every direction. At first the emptiness frightened him, as though he had been dropped off the edge of the world. But his grandparents were steady, kindly people, and their sod house soon felt like a safe harbor against the enormous, windy land.

The Shimerdas had far less luck. They had been cheated by a countryman into buying a miserable dugout carved into a bank of earth, and they knew nothing of prairie farming. Still, Ántonia was full of eager life. She and Jim became inseparable companions, roaming the grasslands together, and Jim began teaching her English word by word, pointing to the sky, the grass, and the animals until she repeated each name back with delight. In those first golden weeks the prairie seemed less lonesome. For both children the vast land held the promise of a new beginning, though the older people already sensed how hard that beginning would be.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: An orphaned boy adjusts to the Nebraska prairie and forms a friendship with an immigrant girl.
A family from Bohemia learns modern methods of prairie farming.
Two children argue about who knows the land better.
A boy decides to return to Virginia after finding the plains too empty.
An immigrant girl teaches an American boy the language and customs of Bohemia.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Jim arriving from Virginia after his parents' deaths and beginning his friendship with Ántonia; the other choices contradict or overstate details in the text.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how did the Shimerdas come to own such a poor home?
choices: They chose the dugout because it was warmest in winter.
They were cheated by a countryman into buying a miserable dugout.
Jim's grandfather sold it to them cheaply.
They built it themselves after studying prairie farms.
They spent nearly all of their money on the long journey west and could afford nothing better.
questions.explanation: The passage states the Shimerdas 'had been cheated by a countryman into buying a miserable dugout carved into a bank of earth.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Jim's grandparents were
choices: wealthy landowners who disliked immigrants
reliable and comforting people who eased Jim's fear of the land
newcomers who knew as little about the prairie as the Shimerdas
eager to send Jim back to Virginia
reluctant settlers who resented having to take in an orphaned grandson
questions.explanation: The text calls them 'steady, kindly people' whose home 'felt like a safe harbor,' implying they comforted Jim against his fear of the vast land.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the phrase 'safe harbor' most nearly means
choices: a body of water for ships
a place of shelter and security
a distant and unreachable goal
a source of income
a brief stopping place before moving farther west
questions.explanation: The sod house is described as feeling 'like a safe harbor against the enormous, windy land,' meaning a place of shelter and security, not a literal harbor.

passages.title: The Cruel First Winter
passages.attribution: Retelling of My Ántonia by Willa Cather — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: In this rising-action section the Shimerdas' hardships deepen, the homesick father takes his own life, and Ántonia is forced into heavy field labor.
passages.text:
The first winter on the prairie fell hard upon the Shimerdas. The wind came screaming out of the north, and their earthen dugout could barely hold out the cold. They had little food and less comfort, and the family that had once known music and neighbors in the old country now huddled together in the dark, hungry and afraid.

No one suffered more than Mr. Shimerda. He was a gentle, refined man who had loved his violin and the company of friends back in Bohemia. On the empty plains he found nothing that spoke to his heart. His shoulders bent, his eyes grew distant, and a heavy sorrow settled over him that no kind word could lift. He was homesick to the bone, aching for a world he would never see again. One bitter morning that winter, unable to carry his grief any longer, he took his own life with his gun.

His death struck the family like a second winter. Ántonia, who had been so bright and eager, wept until she had no tears left. But grief does not pause the work of a farm. Her older brother, Ambrosch, a hard and grasping young man, took charge of everything. He cared more for money and survival than for his sister's future, and he set Ántonia to labor in the fields like a hired man—plowing, harvesting, and hauling from dawn to dark. Jim watched all this with growing anger. He hated to see his clever friend, who had once been so quick to learn her letters, turned brown and rough and denied any chance to go to school. Yet Ántonia bore it without complaint, and something proud and unbreakable in her only seemed to grow stronger.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about
choices: how the Shimerda family is broken by hardship and loss during their first winter
the friendship between Jim and Ambrosch
the pleasures of farming on the Nebraska prairie
Mr. Shimerda's success as a musician in America
the bitter rivalry that grows between Ántonia and her brother Ambrosch
questions.explanation: The passage traces the family's suffering, the father's suicide, and Ántonia being forced into labor—all consequences of a devastating first winter.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did Mr. Shimerda love back in Bohemia?
choices: Farming the open plains
His violin and the company of friends
Traveling to distant cities
Teaching English to his children
The wide, open spaces of the Nebraska prairie
questions.explanation: The text says he 'had loved his violin and the company of friends back in Bohemia.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Ambrosch forced Ántonia into field work because he
choices: wanted her to become better educated
valued the family's survival and money over her schooling
believed she enjoyed heavy labor
was following Jim's advice
hoped to keep her away from her friendship with Jim
questions.explanation: The passage describes Ambrosch as 'hard and grasping,' caring 'more for money and survival than for his sister's future,' which explains why he set her to labor instead of school.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'refined' most nearly means
choices: purified by heating
cultured and gentle in manner
wealthy and powerful
weak and sickly
hardened by years of suffering
questions.explanation: Mr. Shimerda is called 'a gentle, refined man who had loved his violin,' indicating a cultured, gentle character rather than any literal purifying process.

passages.title: Town Lights and Turning Points
passages.attribution: Retelling of My Ántonia by Willa Cather — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: In this turning-point section the story moves to the town of Black Hawk, where Ántonia works as a hired girl and Jim grows up and leaves for university.
passages.text:
A few years later Jim's grandparents grew tired of the hard farm labor and moved into the nearby town of Black Hawk. Ántonia soon followed, taking a place as a hired girl for the Harlings, a lively family who lived next door to Jim. In town Ántonia bloomed again. She cooked, cleaned, and minded children by day, but in the evenings she and the other immigrant hired girls—the beautiful, ambitious Lena Lingard and the shrewd Tiny Soderball—became the brightest figures at the town dances. They worked harder than the town-born girls and laughed louder, and the young men could not stay away from them.

But Black Hawk was a small, proper place, and it frowned on such spirited pleasure. When Ántonia's love of dancing grew too bold, the Harlings gave her an ultimatum, and she left them to work instead for Wick Cutter, a cruel and scheming moneylender. One night Cutter arranged to have his wife away and crept home meaning to attack Ántonia. Warned in time, she narrowly escaped his trap, shaken but unharmed. It was a near thing, and it showed how exposed a poor working girl could be.

Jim, meanwhile, was climbing a different road. He studied hard, shone at his lessons, and left Black Hawk for the university, where a thoughtful teacher named Gaston Cleric opened his mind to literature and the classics. There Jim met Lena again and drifted into a gentle romance with her, but his ambitions pulled him onward, and he soon left for Harvard. The old prairie world and the girls he had loved began to slip behind him, though Ántonia stayed lodged in his memory like a bright coal that would not cool.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: As Ántonia works in Black Hawk and faces danger there, Jim grows up and leaves for university.
Jim and Ántonia open a dance hall together in Black Hawk.
The Harlings adopt Ántonia as their own daughter.
Wick Cutter becomes a respected member of the town.
Lena Lingard and Tiny Soderball compete against Ántonia for the town's young men.
questions.explanation: The passage follows two parallel paths—Ántonia's life as a hired girl and her escape from Cutter, and Jim's schooling and departure—making choice 0 the best summary.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who was Gaston Cleric?
choices: A moneylender who threatened Ántonia
A young man who courted Lena Lingard
A teacher who introduced Jim to literature and the classics
Ántonia's employer in Black Hawk
A young man Jim met at the Black Hawk town dances
questions.explanation: The text describes 'a thoughtful teacher named Gaston Cleric' who 'opened his mind to literature and the classics.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the town of Black Hawk
choices: welcomed the hired girls' lively behavior at dances
held strict, disapproving standards about proper conduct
was larger and more modern than the university town
paid its hired girls more than the town-born girls earned
encouraged its young men to court and marry the immigrant hired girls
questions.explanation: The passage calls Black Hawk 'a small, proper place' that 'frowned on such spirited pleasure,' implying strict and disapproving social standards.

questions.prompt: As used in the last paragraph, the phrase 'like a bright coal that would not cool' suggests that Ántonia
choices: remained vividly and warmly present in Jim's memory
had become angry with Jim for leaving
was in danger of being forgotten completely
had moved far away to a warmer climate
filled Jim with a burning ambition to leave the prairie behind
questions.explanation: The comparison follows the statement that Ántonia 'stayed lodged in his memory,' so the glowing, uncooling coal represents a warm, lasting memory.

passages.title: The Road Back
passages.attribution: Retelling of My Ántonia by Willa Cather — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: This closing section resolves the story as Jim returns after twenty years to find Ántonia grown into a thriving mother, and reflects on their shared past.
passages.text:
Years passed, and Jim's life carried him far from the prairie. He became a successful lawyer in the East, prosperous but strangely lonely, married to a woman he did not truly love. From time to time news of Ántonia reached him, and not all of it was happy. She had fallen in love with a railroad man named Larry Donovan and promised to marry him, but he had abandoned her when she was pregnant and left her with nothing. Alone and unmarried, Ántonia had gone home in disgrace and raised her baby daughter without shame, working the fields once more and holding her head high.

For twenty years Jim stayed away, half afraid of what he might find. At last he returned to Nebraska and drove out to see her. What he found astonished and moved him. Ántonia was now married to a good-natured Bohemian farmer named Anton Cuzak, and she was the mother of a whole tribe of children who came tumbling out of the fruit cellar into the light, healthy and laughing. Her farm was rich with orchards and animals, the very picture of abundance she had once only dreamed of.

Time and toil had worn her hard; her teeth were fewer and her skin was brown and lined. Yet Jim thought her more beautiful than ever, for she glowed with a deep, generous vitality, as though the strength of the whole prairie lived inside her. She had endured everything and remained unbroken. As Jim prepared to leave, he walked out along the faint old road that had first carried the two of them into that country as children, and he understood that whatever else he had lost, he and Ántonia would always share the precious past that had made them who they were.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: Jim's return after twenty years to find Ántonia flourishing despite her hardships
Larry Donovan's career on the railroad
Jim's happy marriage and legal career in the East
the sale of Ántonia's farm to Anton Cuzak
Jim's struggle to rescue his loveless marriage in the East
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on Jim's long-delayed return and his discovery that Ántonia, though worn by hardship, is thriving—making choice 0 the central idea.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did Larry Donovan do to Ántonia?
choices: He married her and moved her to the East.
He abandoned her when she was pregnant, leaving her with nothing.
He gave her a rich farm with orchards.
He helped her raise her daughter.
He promised to marry her but died before the wedding could take place.
questions.explanation: The passage states Donovan 'had abandoned her when she was pregnant and left her with nothing.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Jim considered Ántonia beautiful mainly because of her
choices: youthful, unchanged appearance
wealth and fashionable clothing
inner strength and generous vitality
skill at arguing law cases
large and healthy family of laughing children
questions.explanation: Though 'worn hard' physically, she 'glowed with a deep, generous vitality,' and Jim found her 'more beautiful than ever,' pointing to inner strength rather than looks or wealth.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'abundance' most nearly means
choices: emptiness
plentiful supply and richness
exhaustion
loneliness
modest, hard-won comfort
questions.explanation: Ántonia's farm is described as 'rich with orchards and animals, the very picture of abundance,' meaning a plentiful, rich supply.

passages.title: An Uneasy Welcome at Styles Court
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening: Hastings arrives at the country estate and senses the tensions simmering beneath a polite household.
passages.text:
Captain Arthur Hastings, still weary from a wound earned in the war, was glad of the invitation that carried him away from hospitals and into the green quiet of the countryside. His old friend John Cavendish had asked him to Styles Court, a rambling old house that seemed to promise nothing but rest. Yet from his first evening there, Hastings felt that the calm was only a surface, and that something troubled moved underneath it.

The estate belonged, in truth, to John's stepmother, Emily Inglethorp, an elderly woman whose fortune held the whole family together like a knot. Everyone at Styles depended on her generosity, and everyone, it seemed, resented that dependence. Not long before, Emily had startled them all by marrying Alfred Inglethorp, a much younger man with a carefully groomed black beard. The family regarded him coldly. To their eyes he was plainly a fortune hunter, smiling and attentive only because Emily's money glittered behind her.

At dinner and afterward, Hastings began to sort out the household. There was Mary, John's wife, poised and unreadable; Lawrence, John's quieter brother; Evelyn Howard, Emily's blunt and devoted companion; and young Cynthia Murdock, who spent her days working in a hospital dispensary. Each person carried some private strain that Hastings could sense but not yet name.

Most unsettling of all was a rumor that reached him: Emily had recently quarreled with someone, her voice raised behind a closed door. No one would say plainly with whom, or about what. Hastings told himself he was imagining shadows where there were none. Still, as he lay awake that first night, listening to the old house settle and creak, he could not shake the sense that Styles Court was a place waiting, tensely, for something to break.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Hastings arrives at Styles Court and senses hidden tensions beneath the household's calm surface.
Hastings recovers fully from his war wound during a peaceful stay in the country.
Emily Inglethorp explains to Hastings why she chose to marry Alfred.
The Cavendish family plans a celebration in honor of Hastings's visit.
Poirot arrives to investigate a murder that has already taken place at Styles Court.
questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly stresses that the calm is 'only a surface' and that Hastings feels 'something troubled moved underneath it,' making the sensed tension the central point.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does the family regard Alfred Inglethorp?
choices: They admire him for making Emily happy.
They view him coldly as a fortune hunter after Emily's money.
They barely notice him because he keeps to himself.
They rely on him to manage the estate's finances.
They fear him because he now controls Emily's fortune.
questions.explanation: The passage states the family 'regarded him coldly' and saw him as 'plainly a fortune hunter' interested only because 'Emily's money glittered behind her.'

questions.prompt: The rumor about Emily's recent quarrel most strongly suggests that:
choices: Emily was planning to leave Styles Court for good.
conflict was already brewing before Hastings's arrival.
the servants were responsible for the household's problems.
Hastings had misremembered something from his time at war.
Emily had already decided to change the terms of her will.
questions.explanation: The overheard quarrel, which 'no one would say plainly' about, combined with Hastings's sense of a house 'waiting, tensely, for something to break,' implies conflict predates his arrival.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'strain' most nearly means:
choices: a melody or tune
a physical injury
tension or stress
a family bloodline
the act of filtering something through a sieve
questions.explanation: Each person is said to carry 'some private strain that Hastings could sense but not yet name,' matching the passage's mood of hidden tension rather than a tune, injury, or bloodline.

passages.title: A Death in the Locked Room
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action: Emily is poisoned, Poirot investigates, and the trail of evidence leads to an arrest.
passages.text:
The break came in the dark hours of the night. From behind the locked door of Emily Inglethorp's bedroom came sounds no one at Styles would forget: cries, and the terrible thrashing of a body in convulsions. By the time the household forced their way in, it was too late. Emily was dead. Before long the doctors gave the ugly word for it: strychnine, a swift and merciless poison.

Hastings knew at once who might make sense of the horror. Living nearby was his old acquaintance Hercule Poirot, a Belgian refugee and former police officer, small and precise, with an egg-shaped head and a habit of tidying whatever lay crooked before him. Poirot agreed to look into the matter, and his sharp eyes began at once to gather what the others overlooked.

Suspicion swung immediately toward Alfred, the unloved husband who stood to inherit. Yet Poirot grew thoughtful rather than satisfied. The case against Alfred was almost too neat, too generously supplied with proof. Stranger still, Alfred seemed to go out of his way to fix himself a solid alibi, as though he wished to be suspected and then cleared. That eagerness troubled the little detective more than any accusation could.

The house yielded a scatter of curious clues. A fragment of a will lay charred in the grate, deliberately burned. A coffee cup had been spilled and, oddly, crushed to powder. There were sleeping powders containing bromide, and a dispatch case that someone had taken pains to lock. Each fact seemed to point one way, then another.

When the circumstantial evidence hardened, it did not fall on Alfred at all. Instead the law seized upon John Cavendish, Hastings's own friend, and John was arrested and sent to stand trial for murder. Hastings was aghast; Poirot only watched, and waited, and said little.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
choices: Poirot explains exactly how the murder was committed.
Emily is poisoned, Poirot begins investigating, and John is arrested for the crime.
Hastings decides to leave Styles Court after Emily's death.
Alfred confesses to poisoning his wife for her fortune.
Poirot names the true murderer and clears John Cavendish of the charge.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from Emily's poisoning, through Poirot's arrival and investigation, to John Cavendish's arrest, which together form its central sequence of events.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what puzzles Poirot about Alfred?
choices: Alfred refuses to speak to the detective at all.
Alfred seems eager to establish a strong alibi, as if wanting to be suspected then cleared.
Alfred was not present in the house on the night of the death.
Alfred openly admits that he disliked Emily.
Alfred appears to have burned the fragment of the will himself.
questions.explanation: The passage says Alfred 'seemed to go out of his way to fix himself a solid alibi, as though he wished to be suspected and then cleared,' and that this eagerness 'troubled' Poirot.

questions.prompt: Poirot's decision to watch and wait rather than accept the obvious suspect suggests that he:
choices: believes John Cavendish is certainly guilty.
suspects the true solution is more complicated than the evidence first shows.
has lost interest in solving the case.
trusts the police to reach the correct verdict.
is certain Alfred is the poisoner but simply lacks the proof to say so.
questions.explanation: Because the case against Alfred struck Poirot as 'too neat,' and he 'only watched, and waited, and said little' after John's arrest, the passage implies he doubts the surface explanation.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'merciless' most nearly means:
choices: showing no mercy; cruel and deadly
slow to take effect
difficult to detect
harmless in small amounts
impossible to cure or treat
questions.explanation: Strychnine is called 'a swift and merciless poison' that killed Emily quickly, so 'merciless' here means cruel and deadly, not slow, hidden, or harmless.

passages.title: The Detective's Reckoning
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and turn: Poirot gathers his proof and unmasks the true conspirators behind Emily's murder.
passages.text:
Poirot had held his tongue for a reason. He would not speak until every thread lay firmly in his hand, and at last the moment came. Gathering the household and the officials together, the little Belgian laid out the truth with the calm delight of a man completing a puzzle he had long studied.

The guilty pair, he announced, were the two people who had seemed to hate each other most. Alfred Inglethorp and Evelyn Howard, Emily's loyal companion, were in fact secret lovers and partners in the crime. Their loud, public quarreling had been a performance, a screen drawn across their alliance so that no one would dream of linking them. It was a clever disguise, and it had nearly worked.

Then Poirot explained the poison itself, and here his reasoning shone. The strychnine had already been present, harmlessly dissolved, in a medicine Emily took. Alfred had added bromide from the sleeping powders to that tonic. The chemistry did the rest: the bromide caused the strychnine to gather and settle, concentrated and lethal, so that Emily's final dose became fatal. The delay was no accident. It gave Alfred time to be elsewhere and to arrange his careful alibi.

His whole scheme, Poirot revealed, had rested on a piece of cunning. Alfred had wanted to be arrested early, while the proof was thin, counting on acquittal. Once tried and cleared, he could never be charged for the same murder again, no matter what came to light. That was why Poirot had waited. He refused to strike until his evidence was crushing, so that when Alfred fell, he would fall for good. The trap that Alfred had set for the law now closed upon Alfred himself.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the main idea of the passage?
choices: Poirot reveals that Alfred and Evelyn Howard were secret partners who murdered Emily, and explains how he trapped them.
Poirot decides he cannot solve the case and hands it back to the police.
Evelyn Howard confesses her love for Alfred to the whole household.
John Cavendish escapes from prison before the trial ends.
Poirot proves that Alfred acted entirely alone in poisoning Emily.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Poirot's climactic explanation: naming Alfred and Evelyn as the conspirators and detailing how he outmaneuvered Alfred's plan.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how did the bromide make the poison deadly?
choices: It replaced the strychnine with a stronger poison.
It caused the strychnine already in the medicine to gather and settle, concentrated and lethal.
It made Emily fall asleep so she could not call for help.
It hid the taste of the strychnine so Emily would drink more.
It reacted with the strychnine to form an entirely new poison.
questions.explanation: The passage states the bromide 'caused the strychnine to gather and settle, concentrated and lethal,' making the final dose fatal.

questions.prompt: Why did Alfred want to be arrested early, while the evidence was still weak?
choices: He hoped to protect John Cavendish from suspicion.
He expected to be acquitted and then be safe from ever being charged for the murder again.
He wanted to confess and end the investigation quickly.
He believed the police would never suspect him at all.
He wanted the trial to draw attention away from Evelyn Howard.
questions.explanation: The passage explains Alfred counted on 'acquittal' so that, 'once tried and cleared, he could never be charged for the same murder again,' which is why an early arrest served his plan.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'screen' most nearly means:
choices: a flat surface for showing pictures
a mesh covering a window
a concealing cover or disguise
a wire barrier for filtering
a movable partition that divides a room
questions.explanation: The staged quarreling is called 'a screen drawn across their alliance so that no one would dream of linking them,' meaning a concealing cover or disguise.

passages.title: What the Truth Set Right
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Mysterious Affair at Styles by Agatha Christie — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: with the guilty exposed, the innocent are freed and old wounds among the family begin to heal.
passages.text:
With the truth spoken aloud, the weight that had pressed on Styles Court began, slowly, to lift. Alfred Inglethorp and Evelyn Howard could no longer hide behind their pretended hatred; the disguise that had shielded them was torn away, and the law that Alfred had meant to cheat now held him fast. Poirot had timed his blow so exactly that the guilty man had no clever escape left.

For Hastings, the sweetest part was the freeing of his friend. John Cavendish, who had stood in the shadow of the gallows for a crime he never committed, was cleared at last. The relief of it seemed to reach beyond the courtroom and into John's own home. He and his wife Mary had grown cold and distant with each other through the long ordeal, each nursing private doubts and wounded pride. Now, with the danger past, the ice between them melted. They spoke honestly, forgave what needed forgiving, and turned back toward the marriage they had nearly let slip away.

There was tenderness, too, in a quieter corner of the house. Lawrence, John's reserved brother, had long carried a love he never dared confess for young Cynthia Murdock. Cynthia, for her part, had feared she was unwanted and alone in the world. When at last the two understood each other, the misunderstanding fell away, and they became engaged, to the gentle joy of all who watched.

Poirot took his satisfaction not in praise but in the neatness of a thing set right. The innocent walked free; the guilty answered for their deed; and the little detective, brushing an imaginary speck from his sleeve, could rest content. Styles Court, so lately a house of suspicion and dread, breathed easily once more.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: With the guilty exposed, the innocent are freed and the family's relationships begin to heal.
Poirot decides to move away from the village near Styles Court.
Alfred and Evelyn Howard escape punishment through a clever loophole.
Hastings returns to the hospital to recover from his wound.
Poirot takes great pride in the public praise he earns for solving the case.
questions.explanation: The passage describes John's exoneration, the reconciliation of John and Mary, and Lawrence and Cynthia's engagement, all flowing from the guilty pair being caught.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens between Lawrence and Cynthia?
choices: They quarrel and stop speaking to each other.
They understand each other at last and become engaged.
They leave Styles Court together to work at the hospital.
They remain strangers who barely interact.
Lawrence confesses his love, but Cynthia turns him away.
questions.explanation: The passage states that once Lawrence and Cynthia 'understood each other, the misunderstanding fell away, and they became engaged.'

questions.prompt: The description of John and Mary speaking honestly and forgiving each other suggests that:
choices: the stress of the murder case had damaged their marriage.
Mary had been the true murderer all along.
they intended to separate as soon as the trial ended.
they had never truly cared for each other.
Mary had privately suspected John of the murder during the trial.
questions.explanation: Because they had 'grown cold and distant' during 'the long ordeal' and reconciled only once 'the danger past,' the passage implies the case's strain had harmed their marriage.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the phrase 'held him fast' most nearly means:
choices: moved him quickly
kept him firmly in its grip
released him without charge
judged him unfairly
punished him severely for his crime
questions.explanation: The law 'that Alfred had meant to cheat now held him fast,' meaning it kept him firmly caught, contrasting with his failed hope of escape.

passages.title: Leaving the Green Parish
passages.attribution: Retelling of North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening section, in which Margaret's peaceful southern life collapses and her family is uprooted to the industrial North.
passages.text:
Margaret Hale had always believed that Helstone would hold still for her. The little southern village, with its thatched cottages and its hedgerows heavy with roses, was the one place her heart called home, even after long years spent in the polished drawing rooms of her wealthy London relatives. Her father was the parson there, a gentle, bookish man, and their days moved to the slow rhythm of church bells and garden walks.

Then came the news that unmade everything. Mr. Hale, tormented by doubts he could no longer silence, confessed that he could not in conscience continue to serve the Church of England. He meant to resign his position and take the family away. Margaret received the blow first, before even her mother, and had to carry the weight of it in silence, watching her father's hands tremble as he spoke.

The destination was Milton, a smoke-blackened manufacturing city in the North, where Mr. Hale would earn a modest living as a private tutor. To Margaret it felt like an exile to another country. Gone were the soft skies and quiet lanes; in their place rose chimneys that stained the clouds grey and streets that roared with the traffic of trade and money.

Her mother, always delicate and inclined to melancholy, sank further under the change, grieving for the comforts they had surrendered. Margaret, though her own heart ached, set her jaw and shouldered the practical burdens of the move. She would not let herself be crushed. Yet as the train carried them farther from the green fields she loved, she could not help feeling that the settled, familiar world of her girlhood had been torn cleanly away, and that whatever waited in Milton would demand a harder, stranger kind of courage.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A young woman's secure southern life is overturned when her father's crisis of faith forces the family to move north.
A parson decides to improve his income by becoming a private tutor in a wealthy city.
A delicate mother refuses to leave her comfortable home despite her family's wishes.
A girl returns from London to enjoy a peaceful life in the countryside.
A family weighs whether the quiet life of the South is truly better than the busy world of the North.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on how Mr. Hale's religious doubts and resignation uproot Margaret from Helstone and send the family to Milton, overturning her settled life.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does Mr. Hale decide to leave his position?
choices: He has been offered a better-paying job in the North.
He can no longer in conscience serve the Church of England because of his religious doubts.
His wife's poor health requires a change of climate.
He wishes to be closer to his London relatives.
He hopes that a change of home will lift his wife out of her melancholy.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Mr. Hale, 'tormented by doubts,' confessed he could not in conscience continue to serve the Church and meant to resign.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Margaret responds to the family's crisis by
choices: refusing to accept that the move will happen
relying on her mother to make the difficult decisions
taking on responsibility and steadying herself despite her own grief
blaming her father for destroying their happiness
sinking into sorrow alongside her melancholy mother
questions.explanation: The passage shows Margaret carrying the news 'in silence,' setting 'her jaw,' and shouldering 'the practical burdens of the move,' indicating she takes responsibility despite her pain.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'exile' most nearly means
choices: a brief and pleasant holiday
a forced removal from one's home
a profitable business opportunity
a religious ceremony
a long and tiring journey by train
questions.explanation: Margaret feels the move to Milton is like 'an exile to another country,' meaning a banishment or forced departure from the home she loves, contrasted with the world 'torn cleanly away.'

passages.title: Smoke, Strikes, and a Refused Hand
passages.attribution: Retelling of North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action, as Margaret takes sides in Milton's labor conflict, faces a mob, and rejects Thornton's proposal.
passages.text:
In Milton, Margaret's world filled with new and difficult people. John Thornton, a cotton-mill owner who had built his fortune with his own hands, came to her father as a pupil, hungry to learn. Margaret thought him hard and unbending; she disliked the way he spoke of his workers as though they were merely parts of his machinery. He, for his part, found her pride magnificent, and fell quietly and helplessly in love.

Among the mill folk Margaret found friends her own class would never have chosen. There was Nicholas Higgins, a rough but honest weaver, and his daughter Bessy, a girl her own age whose lungs had been ruined by years breathing the cotton fluff of the factory floor. Through them Margaret began to see the strike then gathering in the streets not as lawless rebellion but as the desperate protest of people pushed too far.

The conflict broke into violence. A crowd of enraged strikers surged toward Thornton's mill, and Thornton stood before them, refusing to hide. Margaret, seeing his danger, rushed out and threw her own body between him and the mob. A stone flung from the crowd struck her, and she fell, wounded, in front of them all.

Thornton read her reckless courage as a declaration of love. The next day he came and offered her his hand in marriage. But Margaret, mortified that he should imagine she had acted from romantic feeling rather than simple humanity, refused him coldly. She had shielded any man in such peril, she insisted; it meant nothing more. Her words wounded his pride deeply, yet neither of them could truly close the door on the other. The refusal only tangled their feelings further, leaving a knot that would take grief and time to loosen.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily about
choices: how Thornton built his fortune from nothing
how Margaret becomes entangled in Milton's labor conflict and rejects Thornton's proposal
the medical causes of Bessy Higgins's illness
the reasons the strikers finally win their demands
the deep friendship that grows between Margaret and Bessy Higgins
questions.explanation: The passage traces Margaret's sympathy with the workers, the mob scene where she shields Thornton, and her cold refusal of his marriage proposal, all part of the rising conflict.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens when the mob approaches Thornton's mill?
choices: Thornton hides inside the mill until the crowd disperses.
Margaret throws herself between Thornton and the crowd and is struck by a stone.
Nicholas Higgins calms the strikers before anyone is hurt.
The police arrive and arrest the leaders of the strike.
Thornton steps before the crowd and quiets them with a speech.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Margaret 'rushed out and threw her own body between him and the mob' and that a stone struck her, wounding her.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Margaret refuses Thornton's proposal because
choices: she is in love with another man in Milton
she is insulted that he assumes she acted out of romantic love rather than basic human decency
she fears her father will not approve of the marriage
she blames Thornton for causing the strike
she believes a mill owner and a parson's daughter are too different in class to marry
questions.explanation: The passage says Margaret was 'mortified that he should imagine she had acted from romantic feeling rather than simple humanity,' which is why she refuses him coldly.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'mortified' most nearly means
choices: deeply embarrassed and offended
physically injured
pleasantly surprised
completely indifferent
filled with grief and sorrow
questions.explanation: Margaret is 'mortified' that Thornton misread her motives; the context of her insulted, cold refusal shows the word means humiliated or deeply offended.

passages.title: A Lie for a Brother's Sake
passages.attribution: Retelling of North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and turn, in which piled-up losses and a protective lie deepen the rift between Margaret and Thornton.
passages.text:
Sorrow came to Margaret not once but in waves. First Bessy Higgins died, her worn-out lungs finally giving way, and Margaret mourned the friend the North had given her. Then, more crushingly, her own gentle mother slipped away, leaving the house in Milton hollow and grey.

Into this grief crept danger. Margaret's brother Frederick, a fugitive wanted for taking part in a mutiny aboard a naval ship, returned in secret to see their dying mother one last time. His presence in England could cost him his life if he were discovered. Late one night, hurrying Frederick toward his escape, Margaret was seen at the railway station, and a man who recognized him grappled with them in the dark. When the police came asking questions, Margaret, to shield her brother, told them a flat lie: she had not been there at all.

The trouble was that Thornton, who served the city as a magistrate, learned of the falsehood. He also knew Margaret had been seen at night in the company of a young man he did not recognize. Bound by honor as an official, he was troubled that the woman he admired had lied. Worse, in his private heart, he leaped to a bitter conclusion: that the stranger was a secret lover, and that Margaret was not the pure, truthful spirit he had believed her to be. His good opinion of her fell.

Margaret could not explain without betraying Frederick, so she bore Thornton's silent disappointment alone. Meanwhile death continued its work. Mr. Bell, an old family friend, and at last her father, Mr. Hale, both died. Margaret found herself suddenly alone in the world, yet unexpectedly wealthy, for Mr. Bell left her his entire fortune, which included, by a strange turn, the very property on which Thornton's mill stood.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the central idea of the passage?
choices: Margaret grows wealthy and decides to invest in Thornton's mill.
A series of deaths and a protective lie leave Margaret alone but rich, while damaging Thornton's opinion of her.
Frederick clears his name and is welcomed back to England.
Thornton discovers the truth about Frederick and forgives Margaret.
Margaret mourns the loss of her friend Bessy and then of her gentle mother in Milton.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the deaths of Bessy, Mrs. Hale, Mr. Bell, and Mr. Hale, and on the lie Margaret tells to protect Frederick, which lowers Thornton's opinion of her while she becomes wealthy through inheritance.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does Margaret lie to the police?
choices: To protect her fugitive brother Frederick from being discovered.
To hide the fact that she loves Thornton.
To avoid being blamed for a crime she committed.
To keep her inheritance from Mr. Bell a secret.
To shield the stranger who had grappled with them at the station.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Margaret told the police 'a flat lie' 'to shield her brother,' who could lose his life if discovered.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Thornton's opinion of Margaret falls because
choices: he learns that she has become wealthier than he is
he wrongly concludes that the unknown young man is her secret lover
he blames her for his mill's financial troubles
she publicly accuses him of mistreating his workers
he is offended that she lied to him in his role as a magistrate
questions.explanation: The passage says Thornton 'leaped to a bitter conclusion: that the stranger was a secret lover,' which caused his good opinion of her to fall; he does not know the man is her brother.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'fugitive' most nearly means
choices: a naval officer of high rank
a person fleeing from the law
a wealthy landowner
a loyal family servant
a sailor who took part in a mutiny
questions.explanation: Frederick is described as 'a fugitive wanted for taking part in a mutiny' whose presence 'could cost him his life if he were discovered,' showing the word means someone running from the law.

passages.title: The Loan That Became a Vow
passages.attribution: Retelling of North and South by Elizabeth Gaskell — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution, in which Margaret and Thornton at last understand their feelings and reconcile, uniting South and North.
passages.text:
Back in London, surrounded once more by the ease and elegance of her wealthy relatives, Margaret discovered that comfort was not the same as contentment. The smooth, idle days of the South could not fill the space inside her. To her own surprise, she found herself longing for smoky Milton, for the blunt honesty of its people, and, most of all, for the difficult, upright man she had once refused. Slowly she admitted the truth she had hidden even from herself: she loved John Thornton.

In the North, meanwhile, Thornton's fortunes had darkened. Hard times fell upon the cotton trade, and his mill, built with such labor and pride, began to fail. He faced the collapse of everything he had made, too honorable to gamble with money that was not his own to risk. Ruin seemed certain.

Because Margaret had inherited Mr. Bell's estate, she now held the very ground his mill stood upon; she was, in effect, his landlord. When the two met again, she came not with pride but with an open hand, offering to lend him the money he needed to keep the business alive. It was a plain, practical proposal, spoken in careful terms of interest and security.

But beneath the businesslike words, both of them heard something else. Thornton understood at last what her offer truly meant, that the woman who had once rejected him now trusted him, respected him, and loved him. The old misunderstandings fell away like smoke clearing from a winter sky. He reached for her, and she did not pull back. Their engagement joined not only two people who had wounded and forgiven each other, but two ways of life, the gentle South and the striving North, reconciled at last in a single, hopeful bond.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: how Thornton rebuilds his mill without any help
how Margaret and Thornton finally understand their love and reconcile
why Margaret prefers London to Milton
the causes of the downturn in the cotton trade
how Margaret comes to value the blunt honesty of the North over the elegance of the South
questions.explanation: The passage follows Margaret's realization that she loves Thornton and their reunion, ending in an engagement that reconciles them and the two regions they represent.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does Margaret offer Thornton when they meet again?
choices: A share in her London relatives' business.
A loan of money to save his failing mill.
An apology for lying to the police.
A position as a tutor to her family.
Her forgiveness for the misunderstanding over her brother.
questions.explanation: The passage states Margaret came 'with an open hand, offering to lend him the money he needed to keep the business alive,' framed as a practical proposal.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Thornton's mill fails because
choices: he refuses to take risks with money that is not his to gamble during hard economic times
Margaret withdraws her financial support from him
his workers go on strike a second time
he decides to leave the cotton business for good
Margaret, as his landlord, raises the rent on the ground his mill stands upon
questions.explanation: The passage says 'hard times fell upon the cotton trade' and that Thornton was 'too honorable to gamble with money that was not his own to risk,' implying his caution during a downturn leads to ruin.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'contentment' most nearly means
choices: deep satisfaction or peace of mind
great physical wealth
restless ambition
public reputation
comfortable and elegant surroundings
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts 'comfort' with 'contentment,' noting that ease 'could not fill the space inside her,' showing contentment means inner satisfaction or peace, not mere material ease.

passages.title: The Door With No Knocker
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: In the opening, a lawyer hears a disturbing story about a cruel stranger, which stirs his suspicions about a friend's mysterious will.
passages.text:
On their customary Sunday walk through the grey London streets, the lawyer Gabriel Utterson and his distant cousin Richard Enfield passed a shabby door set into a blind wall. Something about it made Enfield pause. He had a story, he admitted, and it was not a pleasant one.

Late one black winter morning, he had watched a small, ill-favoured man come stumping along an empty street. At the corner the man collided with a little girl running for a doctor, and rather than help her up he calmly walked over her body, leaving her screaming on the stones. It was, Enfield said, the coldest thing he had ever seen — not the act of a man so much as the act of some trampling machine.

A crowd gathered, furious, threatening to drag the stranger's name through every newspaper in the city. To Enfield's surprise the man did not run. Instead he offered money, and, to buy his peace, he unlocked that very door and returned with a cheque signed by a name so respectable that the whole street knew it. The signature, Enfield was certain, belonged to a good and famous man.

Utterson listened without a word, but his lawyer's mind was already turning. The respectable name troubled him deeply, for he was keeper of a strange will. His friend Dr. Henry Jekyll had lately drawn up a document leaving his entire fortune to a certain Edward Hyde — the very brute Enfield had described. Utterson had disliked the arrangement from the first, and now a cold suspicion took root in him. Who was this Hyde, and what hold could such a creature possibly have over a gentleman as decent and generous as Jekyll?

questions.prompt: What is the main focus of this passage?
choices: Enfield's unsettling account of a cruel stranger sparks Utterson's suspicion about his friend's mysterious will.
Utterson and Enfield argue about the best route for their Sunday walk.
A little girl recovers from an accident thanks to a generous stranger.
Dr. Jekyll explains to Utterson why he wrote his will.
Utterson sets out to prove that the respectable cheque was a forgery.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Enfield telling the story of the trampling and then reveals Utterson's private dread because the man is the heir in Jekyll's will; the final paragraph states his 'cold suspicion.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did the small man do after he collided with the little girl?
choices: He carried her to a nearby doctor.
He walked over her and left her screaming on the ground.
He ran away before the crowd could stop him.
He apologized and helped her to her feet.
He offered money to the girl's family to avoid a scandal.
questions.explanation: The passage states he 'calmly walked over her body, leaving her screaming on the stones,' and that he did not run when the crowd gathered.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Utterson is troubled by Enfield's story chiefly because
choices: he had witnessed the trampling himself.
he recognizes that the man Enfield describes is the heir named in Jekyll's will.
he dislikes taking walks on Sunday mornings.
he plans to report Enfield to the police.
he fears the famous signature on the cheque may have been forged.
questions.explanation: The last paragraph explains Utterson's dread: he keeps Jekyll's will, which leaves everything to Edward Hyde — 'the very brute Enfield had described.'

questions.prompt: As used in the first sentence, 'customary' most nearly means
choices: habitual
expensive
forbidden
exhausting
leisurely
questions.explanation: 'Customary Sunday walk' describes a regular, repeated routine, so 'customary' means habitual or usual.

passages.title: The Cane in the Fog
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: In the rising action, Hyde commits a shocking murder that binds him even more tightly to Jekyll and drives Utterson deeper into fear.
passages.text:
Nearly a year later, London woke to a crime that horrified the entire city. A maidservant, sitting dreamily at her upstairs window in the light of a full moon, saw two men meet in the quiet street below. One was a white-haired, courtly old gentleman who seemed to be asking the way with the politest of bows. The other she recognized with a shudder — a small, wicked-looking man named Hyde who had once visited her master and whom she had loathed on sight.

What happened next she could scarcely believe. Without any provocation, Hyde suddenly broke into a fury. He lifted his heavy walking-cane and clubbed the old man to the ground, then went on striking the helpless body with such ape-like madness that the bones could be heard snapping. The maid fainted at the sight. When she came to and called the police, the elderly victim lay dead, and the murder weapon — a stout cane snapped in two by the force of the blows — lay beside him.

The dead man carried a letter addressed to Utterson, and so the lawyer was summoned. To his dismay, he identified the victim as Sir Danvers Carew, a kindly and distinguished member of Parliament. The broken half of the cane, he realized with dread, was one he himself had given to Jekyll years before.

Utterson led the police to Hyde's rooms in a grim quarter of the city, but the murderer had already fled. Only the other half of the cane and signs of a hasty escape remained. Hyde had vanished into the fog as though he had never existed, leaving a nation clamouring for his capture and Utterson more frightened than ever for the safety and the honour of his friend Jekyll.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: a maid's promotion to a new household.
a brutal, unprovoked murder that ties Hyde more closely to Jekyll and deepens Utterson's fear.
the friendship between Sir Danvers Carew and Mr. Hyde.
the police's successful capture of a dangerous criminal.
a lawyer's realization that a cane he once gave as a gift became a murder weapon.
questions.explanation: The passage narrates Hyde's murder of Carew, connects it to Jekyll through the cane, and ends on Utterson being 'more frightened than ever' for Jekyll.

questions.prompt: Who was the murdered man identified as?
choices: A stranger no one could name.
Richard Enfield, Utterson's cousin.
Sir Danvers Carew, a member of Parliament.
Dr. Henry Jekyll.
The master whom Hyde had once visited.
questions.explanation: The third paragraph states Utterson 'identified the victim as Sir Danvers Carew, a kindly and distinguished member of Parliament.'

questions.prompt: The detail about the broken cane suggests that
choices: Hyde had stolen the cane from a shop.
the murder was an accident.
there is a connection between Hyde and Dr. Jekyll.
the maid had invented her story.
Hyde and Sir Danvers Carew were secretly acquainted.
questions.explanation: The cane used by Hyde was one Utterson 'had given to Jekyll years before,' implying a link between the murderer and Jekyll.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'provocation' most nearly means
choices: a loud noise
a cause or reason for anger
an apology
a hidden weapon
a spoken insult
questions.explanation: 'Without any provocation, Hyde suddenly broke into a fury' shows the attack had no cause or reason, so 'provocation' means a cause or reason for anger.

passages.title: Behind the Locked Door
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: At the climax, mysterious events reach a breaking point when Utterson and the servant Poole batter down the laboratory door and make a terrible discovery.
passages.text:
In the months that followed, Dr. Jekyll seemed at first to recover his old self. He reopened his house, dined with friends, and returned to his charities, as though a shadow had lifted. Then, without warning, he shut his doors again and refused all visitors, hiding in the laboratory above his yard.

Stranger still was what happened to Dr. Lanyon, another old friend of both men. Utterson found him suddenly aged and trembling, a man with the look of death already upon him. Lanyon would say only that he had suffered a shock he could never speak of, and that he wished never to hear Jekyll's name again. Within weeks he was dead, leaving behind a sealed letter marked to be opened only after Jekyll himself had died or disappeared.

The end came on a wild night. Jekyll's servant Poole, pale and shaking, arrived at Utterson's door and begged him to come. For days, Poole said, his master had locked himself in the laboratory, crying out for a certain medicine and sending the servants racing to every chemist in London. But the voice behind the door, Poole swore, was not his master's voice at all. He feared foul play — that Jekyll had been murdered, and that whatever now moved and wept inside that room was someone else entirely.

Together the two men crept into the yard and listened. Then, steeling themselves, they took an axe and battered down the door. Inside, by the quiet firelight, they found not Jekyll but the twisted body of Edward Hyde, still twitching, a crushed vial in his hand and the sharp smell of poison in the air. Of Dr. Jekyll there was no sign — living or dead. On the table lay papers that would, at last, explain the impossible truth.

questions.prompt: What mainly happens in this passage?
choices: A series of alarming events leads Poole to fetch Utterson, and together they discover Hyde's body behind the locked door.
Dr. Jekyll throws a large dinner party for his friends.
Dr. Lanyon recovers from his illness and reconciles with Jekyll.
Utterson decides to give up the practice of law.
Dr. Lanyon finally reveals the shocking secret he had witnessed.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from Jekyll's withdrawal and Lanyon's death to Poole summoning Utterson and their breaking down the door to find Hyde dead.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did Dr. Lanyon leave behind?
choices: A large sum of money for Jekyll.
A sealed letter to be opened only after Jekyll's death or disappearance.
A new medicine for Jekyll's illness.
A signed confession admitting to a crime.
A warning that his friends should never speak Jekyll's name again.
questions.explanation: The second paragraph states Lanyon died 'leaving behind a sealed letter marked to be opened only after Jekyll himself had died or disappeared.'

questions.prompt: The crushed vial and the smell of poison most strongly suggest that
choices: Hyde had taken his own life.
Jekyll was hiding in the yard.
Poole had attacked his master.
the laboratory had caught fire.
Jekyll had poisoned Hyde before escaping the laboratory.
questions.explanation: Hyde's twitching body with a crushed vial in his hand and the smell of poison implies he poisoned himself.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'steeling themselves' most nearly means
choices: stealing something quickly
bracing or hardening their resolve
falling asleep
calling for help
arming themselves with weapons
questions.explanation: 'Steeling themselves' comes just before they force themselves to break down the door, so it means bracing or hardening their resolve for something difficult.

passages.title: The Confession of Henry Jekyll
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde by Robert Louis Stevenson — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: In the resolution, Jekyll's written confession finally explains the secret behind Hyde and reveals why he chose to end both lives at once.
passages.text:
The papers that Utterson carried home that night told a story stranger than any he could have imagined. One was Lanyon's account; the other, thick and sealed, was the full confession of Henry Jekyll himself. Together they revealed the secret both men had carried to their graves.

Years earlier, Jekyll wrote, he had come to believe that every human soul is not one but two — a decent self and a hidden, hungry, wicked self, forever at war inside the same body. Fascinated, he had compounded a chemical draught that could loosen the two apart. When he drank it, his evil nature took bodily shape as a smaller, younger, deformed man he called Edward Hyde. As Hyde he could sin freely and feel no shame; then, with a second dose, he could become respectable Dr. Jekyll once more, safe from blame.

For a time the arrangement delighted him. But the evil self grew stronger with every indulgence, until one dreadful morning Jekyll awoke as Hyde without having taken the potion at all. The transformations began to seize him against his will, and each time he needed a larger dose to fight his way back. Then Lanyon, summoned to witness the change, saw Hyde swallow the drug and turn into Jekyll before his very eyes — a horror that broke his heart and killed him.

Worse followed. The particular batch of chemical that made the potion work ran out, and no fresh supply would do the same. Jekyll understood at last that he was trapped: soon he would become Hyde forever, a hunted murderer with the hangman waiting. Rather than face the scaffold, he wrote his confession while a sliver of himself remained, laid down his pen, and let Hyde end them both. The good and the evil died together.

questions.prompt: The passage mainly serves to
choices: reveal, through Jekyll's confession, how and why he created Hyde and why he chose to die.
describe Utterson's journey home through London.
explain the chemistry of a common medicine.
introduce a new suspect in the murder investigation.
explain how Lanyon's letter helped the police track down Hyde.
questions.explanation: The passage summarizes Jekyll's confession: his belief in two selves, the potion that made Hyde, the loss of control, and his final choice of death.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why could Jekyll no longer turn back into himself?
choices: He had forgotten the recipe for the potion.
Lanyon had stolen his supply of the drug.
The particular batch of chemical ran out and no fresh supply worked the same way.
He no longer wished to be Jekyll.
The repeated transformations had left him too weak to mix a fresh dose.
questions.explanation: The final paragraph states 'the particular batch of chemical that made the potion work ran out, and no fresh supply would do the same.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Jekyll first created the potion because he wanted to
choices: cure a deadly disease.
indulge his wicked desires without harming his good reputation.
become famous as a scientist.
help his friend Dr. Lanyon.
prove his scientific theory that every soul is divided in two.
questions.explanation: The passage says that as Hyde he 'could sin freely and feel no shame,' then return to being 'respectable Dr. Jekyll once more, safe from blame,' implying he wanted wickedness without damage to his reputation.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'compounded' most nearly means
choices: mixed or prepared
swallowed
purchased
described
invented
questions.explanation: He 'had compounded a chemical draught' that he later drinks, so 'compounded' means mixed or prepared the chemical mixture.

passages.title: A Marriage Against Advice
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening: a spirited young woman ignores warnings and marries a charming man she believes she can improve.
passages.text:
When Helen Lawrence was young, her aunt watched over her with an anxious, careful eye. The older woman had lived long enough to know that a pleasing face and a quick tongue were poor foundations for a lifetime, and she urged her niece again and again not to be dazzled by mere charm. A husband, she insisted, should be chosen for steadiness of character, not for the sparkle of his manners at a party. Helen listened politely, but her heart had already wandered where her judgment was told not to follow.

The man who captured her was Arthur Huntingdon, and everything about him seemed designed to delight. He was handsome, easy in company, and endlessly amusing, and when he turned his attention on Helen she felt singled out and understood. That others whispered about his loose habits and reckless spending did not frighten her. On the contrary, it stirred a private confidence in her: surely a wife who loved him truly, and who set a good example, could coax him away from his worst inclinations. She imagined herself not merely as his bride but as the gentle force that would remake him into a better man.

So, over her aunt's plain objections, she married him. In the first bright weeks she clung to her hopeful vision, reading small kindnesses as proof that her plan was working. But the qualities that had charmed her from a distance began to press closer, and the sparkle that had drawn her started to look, in the ordinary light of daily life, a good deal less golden than it had at the ballroom. Helen had bound her whole future to a wager on another person's improvement, and the wager had already been cast.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: Helen's aunt secretly approved of Arthur once she met him.
Helen marries a charming but questionable man, confident she can reform him despite warnings.
Arthur gives up his reckless habits as soon as he falls in love with Helen.
Helen prefers a steady, plain suitor over an exciting one.
Helen's family forbids the match and she is forced to elope in secret.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Helen ignoring her aunt's warnings and marrying Arthur out of confidence that her love could improve him ('surely a wife who loved him truly... could coax him away from his worst inclinations').

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what advice did Helen's aunt repeatedly give her?
choices: To choose a husband for steadiness of character rather than charm
To marry as young as possible
To avoid attending parties and balls
To wait until Arthur reformed himself before marrying
To postpone marriage until she had traveled and seen the world.
questions.explanation: The passage states the aunt 'urged her niece again and again not to be dazzled by mere charm' and that a husband 'should be chosen for steadiness of character, not for the sparkle of his manners.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the final paragraph that Helen's marriage will most likely
choices: quickly fulfill all of her hopes
have no effect on her future
turn out to be a disappointment
cause her aunt to apologize
end almost at once in a quiet separation
questions.explanation: The closing lines note the charm looked 'a good deal less golden' in daily life and that she had staked her future on 'a wager on another person's improvement,' signaling coming disappointment.

questions.prompt: As used in the last sentence, the word "wager" most nearly means
choices: a formal wedding vow
a risky bet on an uncertain outcome
a written contract
a friendly argument
a generous gift offered in good faith
questions.explanation: Helen has 'bound her whole future' to Arthur's uncertain improvement, so 'wager' here means a risky bet on an outcome she cannot control.

passages.title: The Souring of the House
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: Rising action: married life turns cruel, and Helen resolves to flee to protect her young son.
passages.text:
The hopeful bride learned quickly how heavy her wager had become. Arthur Huntingdon, freed from the effort of courtship, showed the temper that charm had concealed. He drank without restraint, filled the house with rowdy friends, and treated his wife's seriousness about faith and duty as a private joke to be mocked at the dinner table. Worse, he made little secret of his attachment to Lady Annabella Lowborough, the wife of one of his own companions, so that Helen was forced to watch betrayal unfold beneath her own roof.

She might have borne much of this in silence, as many women of her time were expected to do. What she could not bear came later, with the birth of her son, whom they named Arthur after his father. The child was Helen's one clear joy, and she poured her hope into raising him well. But her husband, half out of idleness and half out of spite, set about undoing her work. He thought it amusing to teach the small boy to swallow strong drink and to repeat coarse, ugly words, delighting in the way it wounded his wife's tender care.

Here Helen's patience finally broke. She could accept her own unhappiness, but she would not stand by while her child was deliberately corrupted into a copy of the man who was ruining him. Slowly, quietly, a resolve hardened inside her. If the house could not be made safe, then she would take her son out of it, whatever the cost to her reputation or her comfort. The decision frightened her, for the law and the world both stood against a wife who dared to leave. Yet the alternative, she now understood, was to watch her boy destroyed. From that grim clarity, a plan of escape began to form.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: how Arthur reforms after his son is born
why Helen decides she must escape her marriage to protect her son
the friendship between Arthur and Lord Lowborough
Helen's efforts to become a better hostess
how Helen learns to enjoy Arthur's lively circle of friends
questions.explanation: The passage traces the marriage's cruelty and culminates in Helen's resolve to leave in order to save her son from being corrupted.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what finally made Helen unable to endure the marriage?
choices: Arthur's drinking with his friends
Arthur mocking her religious seriousness
Arthur deliberately corrupting their young son
Arthur's attachment to Lady Lowborough
Arthur's refusal to allow her to paint at all
questions.explanation: The passage says she could bear much, but 'what she could not bear' was Arthur teaching the boy to drink and swear; 'Here Helen's patience finally broke.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that leaving her husband was dangerous for Helen because
choices: she had no love for her child
Arthur would happily let her go
the law and society opposed a wife who left her husband
she had nowhere she could paint
she could not afford the cost of travelling away
questions.explanation: The passage states 'the law and the world both stood against a wife who dared to leave,' implying real risk to her reputation and legal standing.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "resolve" most nearly means
choices: a firm determination
a sudden fear
a clever excuse
a quiet sorrow
a passing moment of weakness
questions.explanation: The 'resolve hardened inside her' and led to 'a plan of escape,' showing it means a firm determination to act.

passages.title: The Tenant Nobody Knew
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and turn: Helen hides under a false name, gossip and Gilbert's jealousy erupt, and the truth is finally revealed through her diary.
passages.text:
With the help of her brother, Frederick Lawrence, and her devoted servant Rachel, Helen slipped away from her husband and vanished into a new life. She called herself Mrs. Graham, took shelter in the crumbling rooms of Wildfell Hall, and kept herself and her son by selling the paintings she made. To the villagers she was a puzzle: a young widow, handsome and reserved, who welcomed no questions and offered no history.

Among those drawn to the mystery was Gilbert Markham, a young farmer who found himself increasingly fascinated by the guarded newcomer. But a quiet woman with secrets is an easy target for a talkative countryside, and unkind rumors soon spread that Mrs. Graham was not respectable at all. Gilbert saw her speaking privately and warmly with Frederick Lawrence and, knowing nothing of their true relation, leaped to the worst conclusion. Jealousy poisoned his affection. In a fit of temper he even struck Frederick, wounding a man who had done nothing but shelter his own sister.

Helen could not let the falsehoods stand, yet she could not defend herself with ordinary words without exposing everything. So she made a bold and painful choice: she placed her private diary in Gilbert's hands and let him read it for himself. In those pages the whole hidden story lay open — the reckless marriage, the cruelty, the ruined child, the desperate flight. What the village had turned into scandal was in truth an act of courage. As Gilbert read, his jealousy collapsed into shame and understanding. The mysterious tenant was no fallen woman but a mother who had risked everything to do right, and the truth, once revealed, changed the way he would love her for the rest of his life.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Gilbert and Frederick become close friends over their shared farming.
A misjudged woman finally reveals her true story, transforming a jealous man's understanding of her.
Helen decides to sell Wildfell Hall and move to another village.
The villagers discover that Mrs. Graham is a famous painter.
Gilbert spreads fresh rumors that drive Helen out of the village.
questions.explanation: The passage moves from gossip and Gilbert's mistaken jealousy to Helen revealing her diary, which changes his understanding: 'his jealousy collapsed into shame and understanding.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how did Helen support herself and her son at Wildfell Hall?
choices: By borrowing money from Frederick
By selling the paintings she made
By working as a servant like Rachel
By renting rooms to the villagers
By accepting a small pension left by her late husband
questions.explanation: The passage states she 'kept herself and her son by selling the paintings she made.'

questions.prompt: Why did Gilbert wrongly grow jealous of Frederick Lawrence?
choices: He believed Frederick had stolen his farmland.
He did not know Frederick was Helen's brother.
He thought Frederick had spread the rumors.
He envied Frederick's wealth.
He suspected Frederick of secretly courting her servant Rachel.
questions.explanation: The passage says Gilbert saw Helen speaking warmly with Frederick and, 'knowing nothing of their true relation, leaped to the worst conclusion,' implying he mistook the brother for a rival.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "guarded" most nearly means
choices: protected by soldiers
secretive and reserved
physically fenced in
openly friendly
cheerfully open and talkative
questions.explanation: The 'guarded newcomer' is described as reserved, welcoming 'no questions' and offering 'no history,' so 'guarded' means secretive and reserved.

passages.title: After the Mourning
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Tenant of Wildfell Hall by Anne Brontë — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: Helen dutifully nurses her dying husband, and after mourning and misunderstanding, she and Gilbert are finally free to marry.
passages.text:
Even after Gilbert knew the truth and loved her honestly, Helen would not let herself act. However cruel Arthur had been, she was still his wife in the eyes of the law, and her conscience would not permit her to give her heart away while that bond remained. Gilbert, who now understood her strength, had to learn a harder lesson than jealousy: patience.

Then word arrived that changed everything. Arthur Huntingdon had suffered a bad fall and was slowly dying, ruined at last by the very life he had refused to give up. Many wives, so wronged, might have turned away. Helen did not. She returned to the man who had betrayed her and nursed him through his final illness, hoping even then that suffering might soften him toward repentance. It did not; he died as stubborn and unchanged as he had lived. But Helen had done her duty, and she left that grim deathbed with her conscience clear.

What followed was not an instant reunion. A proper period of mourning had to pass, and there were fresh misunderstandings to untangle, for Helen's changed circumstances and newfound fortune made Gilbert fear she now stood far above his humble station. Pride and hesitation nearly kept the two apart even after every real barrier had fallen. At last, though, Gilbert set aside his doubts and went to her, and the honesty they had earned made plain speech possible. They confessed their love, they married, and together they raised young Arthur into the good man his father had never been — a quiet, hard-won happiness at the end of a long trial.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: how Helen and Gilbert overcome the last obstacles and finally marry
how Arthur repents on his deathbed
why Gilbert remains jealous of Frederick
Helen's decision never to remarry
how Gilbert loses hope and marries another woman instead
