questions.explanation: The passage follows Helen's duty to her dying husband and the final barriers of mourning and pride before she and Gilbert marry and raise young Arthur together.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why did Helen refuse to give her heart to Gilbert at first?
choices: She no longer loved him.
She was still legally married to Arthur.
She planned to leave the country.
She distrusted Gilbert's honesty.
She had resolved to devote herself entirely to her young son.
questions.explanation: The passage says 'she was still his wife in the eyes of the law, and her conscience would not permit her to give her heart away while that bond remained.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Gilbert hesitated to approach Helen after Arthur's death because
choices: he had stopped loving her
he feared her new wealth placed her above him
he was still angry at Frederick
he doubted that Arthur was truly dead
he had promised Frederick that he would stay away from her
questions.explanation: The passage explains her 'newfound fortune made Gilbert fear she now stood far above his humble station,' so pride and doubt about status held him back.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "duty" most nearly means
choices: a tax that must be paid
a moral obligation
a pleasant reward
a legal punishment
an unwelcome chore forced upon someone
questions.explanation: Helen nurses the husband who wronged her and leaves 'with her conscience clear,' showing 'duty' here means a moral obligation she felt bound to fulfill.

passages.title: The House Across the Tarn
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening: a traveler answers a friend's desperate summons and first beholds the decaying mansion that will consume the story.
passages.text:
For an entire day I had ridden alone through a flat and colorless stretch of country, and only as the light began to fail did the house of my old friend rise at last before me. The mere sight of it pressed on my spirit like a weight I could not throw off. There was nothing I could name as the cause—no ruined tower, no obvious wreck—yet a heaviness settled over me, a gloom that no memory of pleasant scenery could soften. I gazed at the bleak walls, at the empty windows that stared back like vacant eyes, and at a few white trunks of dead trees, and I felt the sort of dull dread a dreamer feels on waking.

I had come because Roderick Usher had written to me. His letter was wild and pleading; he spoke of a sickness of the nerves and of the mind, and he begged that I, his one companion from boyhood, would come and stay awhile, as though my presence alone might ease him. I could not refuse so earnest a call, and so I had made the long journey to answer it.

Before I crossed to the door, I paused at the edge of a small, black lake that lay motionless beside the house, and I looked down into it. The still water gave back the whole gray picture: the walls, the dead trees, the hollow windows. Studying the building itself, I traced a thin crack that ran from the roofline down the front of the stones and vanished into the dark water below. It seemed a small flaw. Still, I shivered, and rode forward to the entrance with reluctance heavy in my chest.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: The narrator arrives at his friend's grim mansion and is overcome by an unexplained sense of dread.
The narrator becomes lost during a long journey through unfamiliar country.
The narrator repairs a crack he discovers in an old friend's house.
The narrator decides never to answer his friend's letter.
The narrator reunites with a boyhood friend and spends a cheerful evening remembering the past.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the narrator's arrival at Usher's house and the heavy, unexplained gloom he feels; the letter and the crack are supporting details, not the focus.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why has the narrator traveled to the house?
choices: He inherited the estate and has come to claim it.
Roderick Usher wrote a pleading letter asking his boyhood friend to stay with him.
He wishes to study the strange black lake beside the house.
He was passing through the country and stopped for shelter.
He came to examine the thin crack running down the front of the house.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Roderick Usher's wild, pleading letter, describing a sickness of the nerves and mind, begged his one boyhood companion to come and stay.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the narrator's feeling of dread comes from
choices: a specific, obvious danger he can point to at the house
his exhaustion after a single hour of easy riding
the overall gloomy atmosphere of the place rather than any single cause
his anger at having been summoned against his will
the sight of his own face reflected in the black lake
questions.explanation: The narrator says there was nothing he could name as the cause and no obvious ruin, yet a gloom settled over him, implying the dread arises from the atmosphere itself, not a single identifiable threat.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "flaw" most nearly means
choices: a defect or imperfection
a sudden gust of wind
a decorative carving
a locked gate
a thin crack in the stone
questions.explanation: The word describes the thin crack running down the stone front of the house, so "flaw" here means a defect or imperfection in the building.

passages.title: The Master and His Sister
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action: inside the mansion, the narrator finds his friend transformed and helps entomb the friend's stricken twin sister.
passages.text:
Within the walls I found Roderick sadly altered. His face was ghostly pale, his eyes wide and glittering, and the smallest things tormented him. He could not bear a strong light, nor a loud voice, nor any but the faintest flavors; the touch of ordinary cloth against his skin was agony, and only certain soft, dull sounds gave him any peace. He whispered a strange belief to me: that the house itself was somehow alive, that the very stones and the still water below had gained a power over him, and that this influence had shaped him into the trembling creature he now was.

His dread, he confessed, was made far deeper by another sorrow. His twin sister, the Lady Madeline, was the only other Usher left in the world, and she was dying by slow degrees of an illness the doctors could not explain. Once, while we spoke, she passed through a distant part of the room without a word and vanished, silent as a shadow. I never heard her voice. Soon after, she sank at last into a rigid, death-like stillness, and Roderick told me she was gone.

He feared that curious physicians might dig up her body, and so he resolved to keep her, for a time, in a sealed chamber deep within the house. Together we bore the coffin down to that damp and airless vault and fastened the lid with screws. Before we closed her from sight, I looked once at her face. To my unease, a faint warmth of color still lingered in her cheeks, and the ghost of a smile seemed to rest on her lips—hardly the look of the truly dead. I said nothing, but the sight followed me up the stairs and would not leave my mind.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: the narrator's decision to leave the house at once
Roderick's altered condition and the entombment of his sister Madeline
a disagreement between Roderick and the visiting doctors
the history of the Usher family's wealth
Roderick's belief that the house itself is alive and holds power over him
questions.explanation: The passage describes Roderick's changed, hypersensitive state and then narrates Madeline's apparent death and the placing of her coffin in the vault; these two events form its core.

questions.prompt: Which detail does the passage give about Roderick's condition?
choices: He had lost the ability to walk.
He could no longer remember the narrator.
He was painfully sensitive to light, sound, taste, and touch.
He refused to speak of his sister at all.
He could bear only bright lights and loud sounds.
questions.explanation: The passage states that he could not bear strong light or loud voices, tolerated only faint flavors, and found ordinary cloth against his skin agonizing.

questions.prompt: The narrator's reaction to the color in Madeline's cheeks suggests that he
choices: is certain she has been dead for many days
privately doubts that she is truly dead
believes she is only pretending to be ill
is relieved that her suffering has ended
assumes the damp air of the vault has preserved her color
questions.explanation: He notes the faint warmth and lingering smile as unease, calling it 'hardly the look of the truly dead,' and says the sight would not leave his mind, implying private doubt about her death.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "rigid" most nearly means
choices: cheerful
stiff and unmoving
talkative
feverish
pale and lifeless
questions.explanation: The word describes Madeline sinking into a 'death-like stillness,' so 'rigid' here means stiff and unmoving.

passages.title: The Night of the Storm
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax: on a wild night a story read aloud seems to summon the buried sister, and the terrible truth is revealed.
passages.text:
In the days that followed, Roderick grew wilder still. He wandered the halls with an unsteady step, staring at nothing, listening to sounds I could not hear, as though some horror hung always just beyond the walls. Then came a night of storm, with wind that shrieked around the house and shook it to its stones. Neither of us could sleep, and to steady his shaking nerves I took up an old book of knightly adventure and began to read aloud.

But as I read, the tale betrayed me. Where the story told of a knight cracking open a door, I heard a real cracking somewhere below us. Where it told of a shriek and a clang, faint answering sounds seemed to rise from deep within the house itself. I tried to believe the wind had made them, yet each noise in the book was matched by a noise in the dark, and my voice began to falter.

At last Roderick, who had been rocking and muttering, turned his white face to me and spoke the words I most dreaded. We had made a mistake, he said. The sounds were not the storm and not the story. We had placed his sister living in her tomb, and she had been struggling there for days, and now she was coming. Even as he cried out, the heavy door of the room swung wide. There in the entrance stood the Lady Madeline, her white robes stained, worn thin by her long torment. For one dreadful moment she swayed upon the threshold; then she fell forward upon her brother, and in that final embrace the two of them sank to the floor together, both undone by terror. I stood frozen, unable to move or make a sound.

questions.prompt: What is the central event of this passage?
choices: The narrator finally falls asleep during the storm.
Madeline, buried alive, breaks free and both siblings collapse in terror.
Roderick recovers from his illness after hearing the story.
The narrator and Roderick repair the vault door together.
The storm tears the house apart while the two men read by candlelight.
questions.explanation: The passage builds to Roderick's revelation that Madeline was buried alive and to her appearance at the door, where she falls upon him and both are undone by terror.

questions.prompt: Why does the narrator begin to read aloud from the book of knightly adventure?
choices: To pass the time until the storm ends
To teach Roderick a lesson about courage
To steady Roderick's shaking nerves
To drown out a knocking at the front gate
To match the sounds in the story to the noises in the house
questions.explanation: The passage states directly that the narrator took up the old book 'to steady his shaking nerves' when neither man could sleep.

questions.prompt: The matching of the story's sounds to the noises in the house leads the reader to understand that
choices: the wind alone is responsible for every sound
Madeline is actually struggling out of her tomb as the narrator reads
Roderick has been making the noises himself as a trick
the book is enchanted and creates the storm
the storm has grown strong enough to break the house apart
questions.explanation: The narrator's dread and Roderick's revelation reveal that the real cracking and shrieking come from Madeline breaking free below, matching the events in the tale being read.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "falter" most nearly means
choices: grow louder and bolder
become unsteady and weaken
turn cheerful
stop lying
quicken and race ahead
questions.explanation: The narrator's voice 'began to falter' as each noise in the book was echoed in the dark, meaning it became unsteady and weakened with fear.

passages.title: Into the Black Water
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Fall of the House of Usher by Edgar Allan Poe — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: the narrator flees the mansion, which splits along its old crack and sinks into the lake below.
passages.text:
I could not stay a moment longer in that room of death. Wrenching myself free of the horror that held me, I turned and ran from the house, out through its dark passages and into the storm. The wind still tore at the night, and though the moon should have been hidden, a strange, unnatural glow now hung over everything, so that the ground and the crumbling walls seemed lit by an eerie light.

As I fled across the causeway, something made me look back one last time. The thin crack I had noticed on the day of my arrival—the flaw I had once thought so small—was small no longer. As I watched, it widened swiftly, splitting the front of the house from roof to foundation. There came a long, wild shout, like the roar of a thousand rushing waters, and then the mighty walls parted and gave way. The whole ancient mansion trembled, leaned, and rushed downward all at once.

Before my eyes the House of Usher crumbled into ruin and sank into the deep, still lake at its foot. The black water closed over the last of the stones, and the roaring died away into silence. Where the house had stood there was now only the dark surface of the tarn, smooth again, reflecting the fading, sullen light. The family of Usher had ended, its last two members dead within the walls, and the very building that bore their name had followed them down into the water. I did not stop, but rode on into the night, and I never returned to that dreary country again. What I carried away with me was not a memory of stone and mortar, but of a dread I would never fully escape.

questions.prompt: The main idea of this passage is that
choices: the narrator returns to rescue his friend from the ruins
the narrator escapes as the mansion splits apart and sinks into the lake
the storm passes and the house is left standing but empty
the narrator decides to rebuild the House of Usher
the narrator pulls the last of the Ushers from the collapsing house
questions.explanation: The passage describes the narrator fleeing and then watching the house split along its crack and collapse into the tarn, ending the Usher line.

questions.prompt: What happens to the crack the narrator had noticed when he first arrived?
choices: It is sealed shut by the storm.
It widens and splits the house from roof to foundation.

It disappears into the lake without harming the house.
It glows in the strange light but stays the same narrow size.
questions.explanation: The passage states the once-small flaw widened swiftly, splitting the front of the house from roof to foundation just before the walls gave way.

questions.prompt: The final sentences suggest that the experience left the narrator
choices: eager to tell the story for amusement
permanently haunted by fear
proud of his own bravery
indifferent to what he had seen
grateful and relieved to have survived
questions.explanation: He says what he carried away was 'a dread I would never fully escape' and that he never returned, implying the terror left a lasting, haunting mark.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "sullen" most nearly means
choices: bright and cheerful
gloomy and dim
warm and inviting
loud and busy
faint and fading
questions.explanation: The 'sullen light' reflects off the dark, silent water after the house has sunk, so the word means gloomy and dim.

passages.title: The Shop of Dust and Secrets
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening establishes Nell's life with her grandfather above the antique shop and the debt that threatens to destroy them.
passages.text:
In a narrow corner of London stood a shop crammed with the strange leftovers of forgotten lives: rusted armor, chipped china, faded tapestries, and clocks that had long since stopped keeping time. Among all these dusty oddments lived a girl named Nell Trent, barely fourteen, whose gentleness seemed almost out of place against the gloom. She kept the little rooms above the shop tidy and warm, and she loved her grandfather with a devotion that never wavered.

The old man loved her in return, more than his own life. Yet a sickness gnawed at him that Nell only half understood. Night after night he crept out into the darkness, certain that one lucky turn of the cards would build a fortune to shield her future. Instead, each night carried more of his money away. To keep playing, he turned to a lender named Daniel Quilp, a small, twisted man with a cruel grin and a temper that made even his own wife tremble at his approach.

Nell noticed the shadows deepening in her grandfather's face. She did not know the whole of it, but she felt the weight of some looming ruin pressing closer. Meanwhile, far from the shop, her idle older brother Fred hatched a selfish scheme with his lazy companion Dick Swiveller, plotting to marry Nell off for a fortune that existed only in their imaginations.

The debts mounted until they could no longer be hidden. Quilp, sensing weakness, tightened his grip like a spider drawing in its web. The old curiosity shop, with all its silent relics, was about to slip from the grandfather's hands. And Nell, still faithful, still hoping, stood on the edge of a change she could not yet name.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: A devoted girl's home and security are threatened by her grandfather's secret gambling and mounting debts
A London shop sells rare and valuable antiques to wealthy collectors
Two young men plan a successful business partnership together
An old man teaches his granddaughter how to repair broken clocks
A cruel lender named Quilp works to take over a family's antique shop for his own profit
questions.explanation: The passage centers on Nell's loving devotion and the danger created by her grandfather's gambling and his debts to Quilp, which threaten their home.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does the grandfather continue to gamble?
choices: He enjoys the company of other players at night
He believes a lucky win will secure a fortune for Nell's future
He is trying to pay back money he owes to Fred
He wants to buy new items for the curiosity shop
He is desperate to repay the debts he already owes to Daniel Quilp
questions.explanation: The passage states he was 'certain that one lucky turn of the cards would build a fortune to shield her future.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Nell
choices: fully understands her grandfather's financial troubles
is aware something is wrong but does not know the full extent of the danger
is secretly working with Quilp against her grandfather
plans to leave London to escape her brother Fred
has already begun making a plan to escape the coming ruin
questions.explanation: The passage says Nell 'only half understood' the sickness and 'did not know the whole of it, but she felt the weight of some looming ruin,' showing partial awareness.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "oddments" most nearly means
choices: expensive jewels
odd leftover objects or scraps
loyal customers
written records
valuable antiques
questions.explanation: The word describes the 'strange leftovers of forgotten lives' filling the shop—rusted armor, chipped china, and faded tapestries—so it means odd leftover objects.

passages.title: The Long Road from London
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: After losing the shop, Nell and her grandfather flee London and wander the countryside, meeting kind strangers while the grandfather's gambling threatens them again.
passages.text:
When Quilp finally seized the shop, Nell and her grandfather were cast out with nothing but the clothes they wore. Rather than face pity or capture, Nell made a quiet, brave decision: they would leave London behind and wander the open country as beggars, trusting to the kindness of strangers.

The road was long and often cruel, but it was not without light. They fell in with a traveling Punch-and-Judy troupe whose painted puppets and easy laughter lifted their spirits for a while. Later a gentle schoolmaster took them in, offering warmth and food without asking anything in return. Best of all was Mrs. Jarley, a cheerful woman who ran a waxwork show of famous figures. She gave Nell honest work explaining the exhibits to visitors, and for a brief season the girl knew something close to safety.

Yet the old man's illness had not been left behind in the city. The craving to gamble returned like a fever, and one dark night Nell discovered that her grandfather had crept toward her small savings, tempted to steal from the very child he loved. Her heart broke, but she did not scold him. Again and again she had to guide him away from ruin, hiding their few coins, coaxing him back to the road, bearing a burden far too heavy for her years.

The country grew harsher as they traveled on. They passed at last through a grim industrial town where furnaces glowed and smoke smothered the sky, and there they nearly starved, footsore and faint. Nell's strength was quietly draining away, though she hid her weariness so her grandfather would not despair. Still she pressed forward, searching for some peaceful place where the two of them might finally rest.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Nell and her grandfather enjoy a carefree holiday touring the English countryside
Nell leads her grandfather through a hard journey, protecting him from his gambling while their strength fades
A schoolmaster and Mrs. Jarley compete to hire Nell for work
Quilp chases Nell and her grandfather from town to town across England
Kind strangers along the road finally cure the grandfather of his gambling illness for good
questions.explanation: The passage describes their difficult wandering, Nell repeatedly steering her grandfather from gambling, and her fading strength as she searches for a place to rest.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what work did Mrs. Jarley give Nell?
choices: Sewing costumes for a puppet troupe
Teaching in the schoolmaster's classroom
Explaining the exhibits at her waxwork show
Selling coins and antiques on the road
Painting the figures for her waxwork show
questions.explanation: The passage states Mrs. Jarley 'gave Nell honest work explaining the exhibits to visitors' at her waxwork show.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that Nell hid her weariness from her grandfather because she
choices: did not want him to lose hope or despair
was planning to leave him behind in the town
hoped he would give her more of their money
wanted to return secretly to London
believed her weariness would soon pass on its own
questions.explanation: The passage says she 'hid her weariness so her grandfather would not despair,' indicating she wanted to protect his hope.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "coaxing" most nearly means
choices: forcing harshly
gently persuading
ignoring completely
punishing severely
quietly tricking
questions.explanation: Nell is described as hiding their coins and 'coaxing him back to the road' without scolding him, showing gentle persuasion rather than force.

passages.title: The Turning of the River
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: As Nell settles in a quiet village, redemption and rescue begin back in London while the villain Quilp meets his end.
passages.text:
At last the kind schoolmaster helped Nell and her grandfather settle in a hushed village beside an ancient church. The place was steeped in a solemn peace; ivy climbed the gray stones, and the quiet graves seemed almost gentle in the fading light. Nell was given the task of tending those graves, and she wandered among them like a small, pale spirit, worn thin by the long journey yet grateful for the stillness at last.

Far away in London, other threads of the story were drawing tight. Dick Swiveller, once idle and foolish, had befriended a mistreated little servant girl whom he fondly nicknamed the Marchioness. When Dick fell dangerously ill with a fever, she nursed him faithfully through the worst of it, and in return he began to change, growing kinder and more honest. Together they helped bring hidden wrongdoing to light.

Meanwhile, a warm-hearted single gentleman had been searching the country desperately for Nell. He was revealed to be the grandfather's long-lost younger brother, returned at last to rescue the family he had never forgotten. He followed every rumor and clue, racing to reach the girl before it was too late.

But justice was closing on the villain first. Daniel Quilp, hunted now by the law for his many crimes, fled through the dark. In the blackness he lost his footing near the swollen river, and the cold water swallowed him. The cruel little man who had tormented so many drowned alone, unseen and unmourned, his malice finally silenced by the current.

So the wicked were punished and the good drew near to their reward. Yet as the searchers hurried toward the peaceful village, none of them yet knew how much the long road had already cost the gentle girl waiting among the graves.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the main idea of the passage?
choices: Quilp escapes the law and begins a new life in the countryside
As Nell rests in a quiet village, redemption reaches Dick Swiveller and Quilp meets his end, though rescuers do not yet know the cost of the journey
Dick Swiveller travels the country searching for Nell's grandfather
The schoolmaster builds a new church for the village where Nell lives
The grandfather's long-lost brother reaches the village and reunites the family at last
questions.explanation: The passage weaves together Nell's rest in the village, Dick's reform, Quilp's drowning, and the searchers' approach, ending on the hint that they don't know the journey's cost.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, who is revealed to be the grandfather's long-lost younger brother?
choices: The kind schoolmaster
Dick Swiveller
The warm-hearted single gentleman searching for Nell
Daniel Quilp
The mistreated servant girl called the Marchioness
questions.explanation: The passage states the 'single gentleman' searching for Nell 'was revealed to be the grandfather's long-lost younger brother.'

questions.prompt: The final paragraph most strongly suggests that
choices: Nell has already been seriously harmed by the hardships of the journey
Nell will soon return to London to live with Dick
the rescuers will arrive in time to celebrate with Nell
Quilp survived the river and is still a threat
the schoolmaster will care for Nell in her grandfather's place
questions.explanation: The closing line notes the searchers 'did not yet know how much the long road had already cost the gentle girl,' hinting the journey has gravely harmed Nell.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "malice" most nearly means
choices: sudden wealth
desire to do harm
deep sadness
physical strength
sudden misfortune
questions.explanation: The word describes cruel Quilp, who 'had tormented so many,' so 'malice finally silenced' means his desire to do harm was ended.

passages.title: Rest Among the Graves
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Old Curiosity Shop by Charles Dickens — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution brings sorrow for Nell and her grandfather but happiness for the reformed Dick Swiveller.
passages.text:
The rescuers came too late. In the quiet village beside the old church, the long journey had finally claimed its price. Little Nell, who had guarded her grandfather so faithfully and given up her own strength for his sake, slipped away peacefully, worn out at last. She died as gently as she had lived, and the villagers who had grown to love her wept for the girl who had tended their graves.

When the grandfather learned the truth, his mind could not hold it. He refused to believe that his beloved Nell was truly gone. Each day he sat by her grave, waiting, whispering, certain she would return to him as she always had. His grief hollowed him out until there was nothing left, and before long he too died, laid to rest beside the child he had never stopped loving. The two who had wandered so far together were reunited only in the churchyard's stillness.

Yet the story did not close entirely in shadow. Back in London, Dick Swiveller—once careless and idle—had become a better man through kindness and hardship. A sum of money came to him unexpectedly, enough to change his fortunes. Remembering the loyal servant girl who had nursed him through his fever, he lifted her out of her hard life. He gave the Marchioness a proper education and, in time, made her his wife.

So the tale ended as Dickens loved to end things: with tears and comfort mingled together. The innocent had suffered, and the reader's heart ached for Nell and the broken old man. But the once-worthless Dick had earned a quiet happiness, proving that even a wasted life might be redeemed, and that tenderness, though it cannot always save, is never truly wasted.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?
choices: Nell recovers and returns home with her grandfather
The story ends with sorrow for Nell and her grandfather but redemption and happiness for Dick Swiveller
Dick Swiveller loses his fortune and becomes idle again
The villagers forget Nell soon after she dies
Nell's death drives her grandfather to seek revenge on those who wronged them
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the deaths of Nell and her grandfather with Dick's redemption and marriage, showing sorrow and comfort mingled together.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does the grandfather respond to Nell's death?
choices: He returns immediately to London to find Quilp
He refuses to believe she is gone and waits by her grave until he dies
He blames the villagers for failing to save her
He marries Mrs. Jarley and starts a new life
He spends his remaining days building Nell a grand monument
questions.explanation: The passage states he 'refused to believe' Nell was gone, sat by her grave 'certain she would return,' and 'before long he too died.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that Dick Swiveller marries the Marchioness partly because
choices: he is forced to by her family
he feels gratitude for her care and has come to value her loyalty
she has inherited a large fortune of her own
he wants to return to his old idle habits
he pities her and feels he owes a debt to her family
questions.explanation: The passage says Dick, 'remembering the loyal servant girl who had nursed him,' lifted her out of hardship and married her, implying gratitude and appreciation of her loyalty.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word "redeemed" most nearly means
choices: made worse or ruined
sold for money
made good or saved from a wasted state
forgotten entirely
punished for past wrongs
questions.explanation: The passage says 'even a wasted life might be redeemed,' referring to how the once-worthless Dick became a better man, so it means made good or saved.

passages.title: The Grip of the Water
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Sea and the Wind that Blows by E. B. White — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening section, in which the aging narrator admits how completely the sea has held him since boyhood and how his love has always been braided with fear.
passages.text:
If I am honest, I have never been free of the sea. It took hold of me when I was still a boy, and it has never once let go. People assume that a man who has spent his life around boats must feel only affection for the water, but that has never been the whole truth for me. What I feel is closer to a tangle of two things that ought to cancel each other out and somehow do not: a deep, steady love, and a fear that has never entirely left me.

I remember being small and standing in a boat that seemed enormous, thrilled and frightened in the same breath. The thrill was obvious enough. The fear was quieter but just as real, and here is the strange part: as I grew older and learned my way around rigging and weather, that fear did not shrink the way you might expect. Experience did not cure it. It only made the fear better informed.

The pleasures, though, are almost impossible to explain to anyone who has not felt them. There is the tiller alive in your hand, answering the smallest pressure of your fingers. There is the wind leaning into the sails, filling them until the cloth goes taut and the whole boat seems to wake beneath you. Water, wind, and sailor are locked in a bargain that shifts from second to second, and staying inside that bargain is the entire art of the thing.

Over the years I have owned one boat after another, a whole quiet procession of them. Each was different, and each one held me. I have tried, once or twice, to picture myself as a man without a boat, standing on dry land with nothing tugging at me. I cannot do it. The picture simply will not form.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: The narrator's love of sailing has always been mixed with a fear that experience never erased.
The narrator believes that only expert sailors can truly enjoy being on the water.
The narrator regrets the many boats he has owned over the course of his life.
The narrator explains the technical steps required to rig and steer a small boat.
The narrator's growing fear of the sea has finally overpowered his love of sailing.
questions.explanation: The passage opens and closes on the idea that the sea has gripped him since boyhood and that his love is 'a tangle' of affection and fear that 'experience did not cure'; the details about tiller and sails serve that larger point rather than being the point themselves.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happened to the narrator's fear as he gained sailing experience?
choices: It disappeared entirely once he mastered rigging and weather.
It did not shrink, but instead became more informed.
It grew until he could no longer enjoy sailing at all.
It was replaced by pure affection for the water.
It stayed exactly the same as it had been when he was a boy.
questions.explanation: The narrator states directly that 'that fear did not shrink the way you might expect. Experience did not cure it. It only made the fear better informed.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the narrator regards sailing as
choices: a hobby he could easily abandon if he chose to
an activity he pursues only out of habit rather than feeling
a defining part of who he is that he cannot imagine living without
a skill he values mainly for impressing other people
a pursuit he loves chiefly for the challenge of mastering rigging and weather
questions.explanation: When he tries to 'picture myself as a man without a boat' he says 'I cannot do it. The picture simply will not form,' implying sailing is inseparable from his sense of self.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'bargain' most nearly means
choices: a low price paid for something valuable
a shifting agreement or balance among forces
an argument between two opponents
a promise made and then broken
a settled deal that no longer needs any attention
questions.explanation: The 'bargain' is described as something 'locked' among water, wind, and sailor that 'shifts from second to second' and must be maintained, meaning a delicate, ongoing balance rather than a purchase or a quarrel.

passages.title: The Feel of the Tiller
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Sea and the Wind that Blows by E. B. White — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: The rising action, in which the narrator lingers on the sensory joys of handling a boat and the long line of vessels that have carried him through his years.
passages.text:
Let me try to set down what it is actually like, because the feeling is the whole reason. A small boat under sail is not a machine you operate; it is a creature you persuade. You take the tiller in one hand, and almost at once it begins to talk to you. Through that single wooden arm you can feel the rudder biting the water, the drag when you turn too sharply, the sweet ease when you find the right line. Nothing else in my life has spoken back to me so plainly.

And then there is the wind. You learn to read it long before it arrives, watching the dark cat's-paws stroke across the surface, feeling it press first against one cheek and then the other. When it fills the sail, the boat does not merely move; it comes alive and leans into its work, and you lean with it, the two of you tilted against the same slope of air. To hold that arrangement steady, to keep water and wind and hull all pulling in agreement, takes a kind of constant, delicate attention that never grows dull.

I have kept up this conversation with the water for the better part of a lifetime, and I have not kept it up alone. There has always been a boat. When one grew old or too small or simply passed out of my hands, another took its place, and I came to think of them almost as a succession of companions rather than a set of possessions. Each had its own temper, its own habits in a gust, its own way of coming about.

I could no more give a full accounting of them than a man could list every friend he has loved. What I know is that the succession never broke. From boyhood forward there was always something of mine tied to a mooring, waiting, and the knowledge that it waited there was itself a kind of contentment.

questions.prompt: The main purpose of this passage is to
choices: describe the sensory pleasures of sailing and the lifelong series of boats the narrator has owned
warn readers about the dangers of sailing in strong wind
compare sailing a boat to operating a complicated machine
explain how to choose the correct boat for a beginner
teach beginners the technique of reading the wind off the water's surface
questions.explanation: The passage devotes its first half to how sailing feels through tiller and wind and its second half to the 'succession' of boats he has owned; together these convey the pleasures and the constancy of his sailing life.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does the narrator know the wind before it reaches him?
choices: By listening to the sound of the rudder
By watching the dark cat's-paws move across the water's surface
By checking the tension of the mooring line
By counting how long since the last gust
By feeling the tiller begin to tremble in his hand
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'You learn to read it long before it arrives, watching the dark cat's-paws stroke across the surface,' identifying that visual cue as how he anticipates the wind.

questions.prompt: The narrator's comparison of his boats to 'a succession of companions' suggests that he
choices: found his boats difficult and unreliable
valued his boats as relationships rather than as mere objects
preferred sailing with other people aboard
was frequently forced to sell his boats for money
felt lonely at sea and wished his boats could be real friends
questions.explanation: He calls them 'companions rather than a set of possessions' and likens listing them to naming 'every friend he has loved,' implying an emotional, relational attachment rather than ownership of objects.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'persuade' most nearly means
choices: to force by strength
to coax or gently work with
to purchase from an owner
to warn of danger
to convince through words and argument
questions.explanation: The boat is called 'a creature you persuade,' contrasted with 'a machine you operate,' and the surrounding text stresses delicate attention and reading the boat's responses, so 'persuade' here means to coax or work gently with rather than to force.

passages.title: The Weight of the Years
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Sea and the Wind that Blows by E. B. White — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and turning point, in which the narrator confronts how age has made sailing harder and more frightening, and repeatedly resolves to sell his boat.
passages.text:
I will not pretend that nothing has changed. It has. The plain fact is that I am old now, and the sea, which once merely frightened me, has begun to frighten me in a new and heavier way. The work of sailing has grown harder. Tasks that my hands once did without thought now leave me winded and unsure. Hauling a line, bracing against a heel, coming about in a freshening wind: each of these now costs me something it did not used to cost.

Worse than the tiredness is the fear, which has sharpened rather than faded. When the wind rises and the boat begins to strain, I no longer feel only the old thrill. I feel exposed. I am aware, as I was not aware at twenty, of how thin the margin is between a good afternoon and a bad one, and of how little strength I would have to spare if things went wrong. The water has not changed at all. I have changed, and that is the whole difficulty.

So I have done the sensible thing, at least in my head. I have told myself, plainly and more than once, that the time has come to give it up. Sell the boat. Be done. Let some younger person take the tiller and feel it talk to them, while I sit safely ashore and watch. It is, I know, exactly the reasonable course, the thing any prudent man my age would do.

And every single time, I fail to do it. I get as far as the thought, sometimes as far as nearly saying the words aloud, and then something in me refuses. The pull of the water proves stronger than my good sense. I cannot make my hand write the notice or close the deal. The caution is real, but it is not, it turns out, the strongest thing in me.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best states the central idea of this passage?
choices: Age has made sailing harder and more frightening, yet the narrator cannot bring himself to give it up.
The narrator has finally decided to sell his boat to a younger sailor.
The sea itself has grown more dangerous than it was in the narrator's youth.
The narrator no longer feels any fear when the wind rises.
Now that he has accepted his age, the narrator's fear of the sea has faded.
questions.explanation: The passage builds from the difficulty and fear of sailing in old age to his repeated resolve to quit, ending on the fact that 'every single time, I fail to do it' because the pull of the water beats his caution.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does the narrator say has actually changed?
choices: The sea has become rougher and more dangerous.
He himself has changed, while the water has not.
His boats have grown older and less seaworthy.
The wind now rises more often than it once did.
Both the sea and the narrator have changed together over the years.
questions.explanation: The narrator states directly: 'The water has not changed at all. I have changed, and that is the whole difficulty.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the narrator considers selling the boat to be
choices: a foolish idea he never seriously entertains
the reasonable choice, which he nonetheless cannot carry out
a decision he has already completed
something his family is forcing upon him
a choice his failing strength has forced upon him
questions.explanation: He calls quitting 'the sensible thing' and 'exactly the reasonable course,' yet says 'every single time, I fail to do it,' showing he judges it wise but cannot act on it.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'prudent' most nearly means
choices: wealthy
cautious and sensible
fearless
elderly
experienced and skilled
questions.explanation: A 'prudent man my age' is described as one who would do 'the reasonable course' and stay 'safely ashore,' so 'prudent' means cautious and sensible.

passages.title: Bound to the Wind
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Sea and the Wind that Blows by E. B. White — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution, in which the narrator quietly accepts that he will never truly let go of sailing and expects to still own a boat at the end of his life.
passages.text:
So I have stopped lying to myself about it. I am not going to give it up. Not this year, not next year, not ever. The struggle I described, the tug between my fear and my longing, is not really a struggle with a winner still to be decided. It was settled long ago, probably before I was old enough to know a decision was being made. I belong to the water, and that is simply the shape of my life.

There is no drama in saying so. No storm carries me to this conclusion, no single voyage seals it. It arrives instead as a plain, quiet recognition, the kind that comes to a man late in the day when he finally sees clearly what has been true all along. I will keep the boat. I will keep going out, more carefully now, more slowly, but out all the same, because the alternative, a life turned away from the sea, is not a life I am willing to accept.

When I try to imagine my own ending, I do not picture myself landlocked and safe. I picture a boat still mine, still tied and waiting, its lines still needing tending. I fully expect that when I die there will be a vessel with my name attached to it, riding at its mooring, ready to be sailed by someone with the strength I no longer have.

That thought does not sadden me. If anything, it steadies me. To be bound to the wind and the water for the whole of one's life, right up to the last of it, seems to me not a trap but a kind of faithfulness. The sea took hold of me when I was a boy. It never let go, and now, at the end, I find that I would not want it to.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of this final passage?
choices: The narrator accepts that he will never give up sailing and expects to own a boat when he dies.
The narrator decides that a single dangerous storm has changed his mind about the sea.
The narrator finally sells his boat and turns away from the water for good.
The narrator regrets having spent so much of his life sailing.
The narrator hopes a younger sailor will take over his boat so he can at last rest ashore.
questions.explanation: The passage resolves the earlier conflict by declaring 'I am not going to give it up... not ever,' and ends with the image of a boat still his at his death, framing his lifelong bond to the sea as acceptance.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator say his conclusion arrives?
choices: Through a single dramatic voyage that seals his decision
As a plain, quiet recognition of what has long been true
As a sudden change of heart during a storm
After a friend persuades him to keep sailing
Through the slow physical decline that finally forced his hand
questions.explanation: The narrator explicitly says 'No storm carries me to this conclusion, no single voyage seals it. It arrives instead as a plain, quiet recognition.'

questions.prompt: The narrator's remark that being bound to the sea is 'not a trap but a kind of faithfulness' suggests that he
choices: feels imprisoned by his inability to stop sailing
views his lifelong devotion to the sea as something positive and loyal, not a burden
wishes he had chosen a different way to spend his life
believes the sea has treated him unfairly
accepts sailing as a solemn duty he must endure until the end
questions.explanation: By rejecting the word 'trap' in favor of 'faithfulness' and saying the thought 'steadies me,' he frames his permanent bond to the sea as a loyal, welcome part of his life rather than a burden.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'landlocked' most nearly means
choices: surrounded and kept away from the sea
wealthy and secure
lost at sea
young and strong
anchored firmly within a single harbor
questions.explanation: 'Landlocked' is contrasted with picturing a boat 'still mine' and set beside 'safe,' describing a life cut off from the water; the surrounding phrase 'a life turned away from the sea' confirms it means kept away from the sea.

passages.title: A Summer at Dunnet Landing
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett — Part 1 of 4
passages.context: The opening: a writer arrives in a small Maine village for the summer and settles in with her landlady, Mrs. Todd.
passages.text:
When I stepped off the boat that June, Dunnet Landing looked no larger than a handful of gray houses scattered along the water, their windows facing the sea as if watching for something. I had come north to write, believing that a quiet coast would give me the long, undisturbed hours my work required. What I found instead was a village that pulled at my attention from every direction, so that my papers often lay untouched.

I took a room in the house of Almira Todd, a widow of middle age who kept a small green empire of herbs behind her door. The scent of mint and pennyroyal filled every corner. Neighbors came at all hours, tapping softly at the side entrance, and Mrs. Todd would measure out a tea for a cough or a bitter tonic for some ache she seemed to understand better than any doctor. She spoke of these remedies with the calm authority of a woman who had learned them from her mother, and her mother before her.

At first I told myself I would resist the constant coming and going. But Mrs. Todd's warmth was hard to refuse. She was broad and steady, with a low laugh and an endless store of talk about the people who lived along the shore. To recover my writing hours, I struck a bargain and rented the empty schoolhouse on the hill, a plain little building where I could sit alone with the door shut. Even there, though, I would look up from my page and find myself gazing out at the harbor, drawn into the slow rhythms of a place that was already becoming, without my quite noticing, a kind of home.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
choices: The narrator comes to a quiet village to write but is gradually drawn into its life and her landlady's world.
The narrator studies herbal medicine so that she can treat the village's illnesses.
The narrator regrets renting a room and decides to leave Dunnet Landing.
The narrator competes with Mrs. Todd for the attention of the neighbors.
The narrator spends the summer helping Mrs. Todd gather and sell her herbs to the neighbors.
questions.explanation: The passage shows the narrator arriving to write but repeatedly pulled into village life and warming to Mrs. Todd; the final lines say the place is becoming 'a kind of home.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does the narrator rent the empty schoolhouse?
choices: To store Mrs. Todd's herbs and remedies
To have a quiet, private place to do her writing
To hold classes for the village children
To watch the harbor for arriving boats
To get away from the strong scent of herbs in Mrs. Todd's house
questions.explanation: The passage states she rented the schoolhouse 'to recover my writing hours' so she could 'sit alone with the door shut.'

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that the villagers regard Mrs. Todd as
choices: a stranger they distrust
a trusted source of healing and remedies
a wealthy and distant landowner
someone who dislikes visitors
a rival who has driven the village doctor away
questions.explanation: Neighbors tap at her door 'at all hours' seeking teas and tonics, and she is said to understand ailments 'better than any doctor,' implying she is trusted for healing.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the phrase 'small green empire of herbs' most nearly suggests that Mrs. Todd's herbs
choices: are grown only for decoration
are dying and neglected
form a large, thriving, well-tended collection
are dangerous to the neighbors
have made her wealthy and powerful in the village
questions.explanation: Calling it an 'empire' filling 'every corner' with scent conveys an abundant, flourishing collection she carefully maintains.

passages.title: Voices Along the Shore
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett — Part 2 of 4
passages.context: Rising action: through visits and conversations, the narrator meets the memorable people of the coast, from Mrs. Todd's island family to a strange old sea captain.
passages.text:
As the weeks went on, Mrs. Todd led me out of the schoolhouse and into the wider web of the village and its waters. One clear morning we sailed to Green Island, a lonely spot far out where her aged mother, Mrs. Blackett, kept house with her bachelor son William. The old woman met us at the door with a brightness that made the small rooms feel full of light, and I saw at once how deeply this scattered family loved one another. William was shy to the point of silence, slipping off to his fishing and his fields, yet his devotion to his mother showed in every quiet thing he did. We gathered herbs on the slopes while the sea shone below.

Back on the mainland I came to know others. Captain Littlepage, a retired shipmaster grown solemn with age, sought me out to tell a tale so strange I hardly knew what to make of it. In a low, earnest voice he described a ghostly town he claimed sailors had glimpsed through the fog near the very top of the world, its dim figures neither living nor dead. He told it as sober fact, and his loneliness made the story linger.

Mrs. Todd, too, gave me the histories of those long gone. She spoke of 'poor Joanna,' a woman who, crushed by a lover's betrayal, had banished herself to bleak Shell-heap Island. There Joanna lived out her years entirely alone, refusing comfort, until at last she died in her chosen solitude. Each of these lives, offered up in Mrs. Todd's steady voice, deepened my sense of a community bound together by memory, endurance, and the long reach of the sea.

questions.prompt: The passage is primarily concerned with
choices: the narrator's growing acquaintance with the distinctive people of the coast
a dangerous storm that strikes Green Island
Mrs. Todd's decision to give up gathering herbs
the narrator finishing the book she came to write
the narrator's own voyage to the ghostly town near the top of the world
questions.explanation: The passage moves through the narrator meeting Mrs. Blackett, William, Captain Littlepage, and hearing of Joanna, all deepening her sense of the community.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why did Joanna go to live alone on Shell-heap Island?
choices: She wished to gather rare herbs there
She had been betrayed by a lover and exiled herself
She was sent there to keep a lighthouse
She wanted to be near Mrs. Blackett
She had lost her family to the sea and wished to mourn them alone
questions.explanation: The passage says Joanna, 'crushed by a lover's betrayal, had banished herself' to the island and lived there alone until she died.

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about Captain Littlepage from the passage?
choices: He is a cheerful man who enjoys company
He no longer remembers his years at sea
His isolation and age give weight to his strange tale
He does not truly believe the story he tells
He hopes the narrator will write his strange tale into a book
questions.explanation: He is 'solemn with age,' tells the tale 'as sober fact,' and his 'loneliness made the story linger,' implying his isolation lends the tale its haunting force.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'banished' most nearly means
choices: welcomed
sent or removed away
forgave
rescued
punished
questions.explanation: Joanna 'banished herself' to a lonely island where she lived alone, so the word means removed or sent away.

passages.title: The Bowden Reunion
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett — Part 3 of 4
passages.context: The climax and emotional turn: the whole family and community gather for the great Bowden reunion, a day that celebrates memory and belonging.
passages.text:
The high point of that summer came on the day of the Bowden family reunion. Word of it had run through the village for weeks, and on the morning itself Mrs. Todd was up early, her face lit with a purpose I had not seen in her before. She, her mother Mrs. Blackett, and I set out together, joining a long procession of relatives who had come from every direction by wagon and on foot.

When the whole company had assembled, they formed into ranks and marched across the fields to the old family grounds, moving almost like a small army under the open sky. There was something grand and moving in it, hundreds of people bound by shared blood and shared names, walking together toward the tables spread beneath the trees. I felt myself, an outsider, swept up in the current of their belonging.

The feast was enormous. Long boards groaned under pies and roasts and loaves, and one cake had been baked in the shape of the old house so that the family's past sat right there among the dishes. People ate and laughed and remembered aloud those who had died, and the living and the dead seemed for a while to sit together at one table. Mrs. Blackett, small and serene, was treated like a queen, and Mrs. Todd stood among her kin taller and prouder than I had ever seen her.

Watching them, I understood something about this coast that no single conversation had taught me. These scattered, weathered people were not truly alone. They were held, each of them, in a long chain of memory and kinship that the hard sea and the passing years could not break.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the central idea of the passage?
choices: The reunion reveals the deep bonds of family and community that hold the coastal people together.
The reunion is ruined by bad weather and poor planning.
Mrs. Todd argues with her relatives over the family grounds.
The narrator feels unwelcome and leaves the gathering early.
The gathering is chiefly a solemn ceremony to bury and mourn the family's dead.
questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly stresses shared blood, memory, and belonging, ending with the narrator's realization that the people are 'held... in a long chain of memory and kinship.'

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what detail shows the family's past being honored at the feast?
choices: A portrait of Mrs. Blackett hung on a tree
A cake baked in the shape of the old house
A speech read aloud from a family diary
A flag carried at the head of the procession
The relatives marching in ranks across the fields like a small army
questions.explanation: The passage specifies 'one cake had been baked in the shape of the old house so that the family's past sat right there among the dishes.'

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that the narrator's feelings during the reunion are best described as
choices: bored and impatient
moved and somewhat wistful as an outsider
angry at being excluded
frightened by the large crowd
proud to be counted as a true member of the Bowden family
questions.explanation: She calls herself 'an outsider... swept up in the current of their belonging' and reflects with feeling on the kinship she witnesses, suggesting she is moved and wistful.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'serene' most nearly means
choices: calm and peaceful
loud and demanding
weak and ill
sad and tearful
proud and commanding
questions.explanation: Mrs. Blackett is described as 'small and serene' and 'treated like a queen,' fitting a calm, peaceful bearing.

passages.title: The Shore Grows Small
passages.attribution: Retelling of The Country of the Pointed Firs by Sarah Orne Jewett — Part 4 of 4
passages.context: The resolution: summer ends, the narrator hears the last of the village's news and parts from Mrs. Todd before sailing away.
passages.text:
Summer could not last, and I felt its ending in the cooler mornings and the shortening light. Before I left, the village gave up its final pieces of news. The best of it concerned shy William, who had at last married Esther, the shepherdess he had loved in silence for so many patient years. That two such quiet lives should come together after all that waiting seemed to me a small, perfect happiness, and Mrs. Todd told it with obvious satisfaction, as though the whole coast had won something.

The day of my going arrived too soon. Mrs. Todd and I had grown close in a way that neither of us was inclined to speak of directly, and so our parting was made of few words. She was not one for tears or long farewells. She busied herself instead with small kindnesses, leaving little gifts for me to find, a keepsake here, a packet of herbs there, tokens that said more than any speech could. Then she set off on some errand up the road and walked away without once turning her head, as if looking back were more than she could bear.

I carried my bags down to the landing and boarded the boat. As we drew out into the bay, I stood at the rail and watched Dunnet Landing shrink behind me, the gray houses and the spruce-dark shore growing smaller until the whole village was only a thin line against the water. I felt no simple sorrow, but something fuller: a deep and lasting affection for the place and the people it had given me, folded away now to keep.

questions.prompt: The passage is mainly about
choices: the narrator's departure from Dunnet Landing and her feelings on leaving
a wedding celebration that the narrator plans
a quarrel between the narrator and Mrs. Todd
William's decision to move away from the coast
the wedding of William and Esther, which the narrator helps to arrange
questions.explanation: The passage centers on summer's end, the parting from Mrs. Todd, and the narrator watching the village shrink as she sails away with lasting affection.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what news does the narrator learn before she leaves?
choices: Captain Littlepage has gone back to sea
William has married Esther, the shepherdess
Mrs. Blackett has moved to the mainland
The schoolhouse is to be torn down
Esther has given up her flock of sheep to move into the village
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'shy William... had at last married Esther, the shepherdess he had loved in silence for so many patient years.'

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about Mrs. Todd from the way she says goodbye?
choices: She is glad to see the narrator go
She feels the parting deeply but prefers not to show it openly
She has already forgotten the narrator
She expects the narrator to return the next day
She is too occupied with her errands up the road to mind the parting
questions.explanation: She avoids words and tears, leaves meaningful gifts, and walks off 'without once turning her head, as if looking back were more than she could bear,' implying strong but hidden feeling.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, the word 'tokens' most nearly means
choices: payments owed
small objects given as signs of feeling
written messages
borrowed items
spoken words of farewell
questions.explanation: The 'tokens' are the little gifts and herbs Mrs. Todd leaves that 'said more than any speech could,' meaning objects that stand as signs of her affection.
